smart-contract.title: Blockchain and Solidity Foundations
smart-contract.description: This layer introduces how smart contracts run on the Ethereum Virtual Machine and the basic syntax of Solidity. Understanding these fundamentals is essential before writing any production logic.
smart-contract.challenge: Write and deploy a simple storage contract in Remix that stores and returns a number.

children.title: How the EVM Processes Transactions
children.description: Every Solidity function call compiles down to EVM opcodes executed in a sandboxed stack machine. Without understanding that each instruction costs gas and that state changes are only committed when a transaction succeeds, you will write contracts that run out of gas mid-execution or leave storage in an inconsistent state.

children.title: Gas and the Fee Market
children.description: Gas measures the computational cost of each opcode so that users compensate validators fairly and the network resists denial-of-service attacks. Misjudging gas limits causes reverts in production, and ignoring EIP-1559 base-fee mechanics leads to transaction failures during congestion.

children.title: Value Types vs Reference Types
children.description: Solidity separates value types (uint, bool, address) that are copied on assignment from reference types (arrays, structs, mappings) that point to a storage location. Confusing the two causes silent bugs where you think you have a copy but are mutating the original storage slot.

children.title: Visibility and Access Control
children.description: Functions and state variables can be public, external, internal, or private, controlling who may call them and at what cost. Incorrect visibility is the single most common access-control bug: marking an administrative function public or leaving a critical getter external exposes contract state to unauthorized callers.

children.title: Remix IDE for Rapid Prototyping
children.description: Remix runs entirely in the browser and provides a compiler, debugger, and one-click deployment to test networks, making it ideal for first experiments. Skipping Remix and going straight to a local toolchain without understanding the compile-deploy-interact cycle makes it much harder to debug early mistakes.

children.title: Solidity Compiler and Versioning
children.description: Pinning the pragma version prevents silent behavior changes between compiler releases, which have historically included breaking security fixes. Mismatched compiler versions between local tooling and deployment pipelines produce different bytecode and make audit findings non-reproducible.

children.title: Accounts: EOAs and Contracts
children.description: Ethereum has two account types — externally owned accounts controlled by private keys and contract accounts governed by bytecode. Misunderstanding the difference leads to sending ether to contracts that cannot receive it or failing to account for the cost of contract-to-contract calls.

children.title: ABI Encoding and Function Selectors
children.description: The ABI defines how calldata is encoded when calling a contract and how return values are decoded, and function selectors are the first four bytes of the keccak256 hash of the function signature. Without this knowledge you cannot decode raw transactions, write low-level calls, or understand why two functions with similar names can clash.

smart-contract.title: Contract Structure and Data Patterns
smart-contract.description: This layer covers how to organize contract state using structs, arrays, and mappings. Proper data modeling keeps contracts readable and gas efficient.
topics: Error handling with require and revert
smart-contract.challenge: Extend the storage contract into a simple registry that maps addresses to user profile structs.

children.title: Structs for Grouping State
children.description: Structs bundle related fields into a single named type, keeping contract storage readable and reducing the number of separate mappings you need to manage. Packing struct fields incorrectly wastes storage slots and increases gas costs for every read and write.

children.title: Dynamic Arrays and Storage Growth
children.description: Solidity dynamic arrays grow unboundedly in storage, which means iterating over them in a transaction can exhaust the block gas limit as the array grows. Ignoring unbounded loops is a common denial-of-service vector that renders contracts unusable at scale.

children.title: Mappings as the Primary Key-Value Store
children.description: Mappings provide O(1) storage access using keccak256-based slot derivation and return zero values for unset keys, making them ideal for balances and allowances. Forgetting that mappings cannot be iterated or deleted in bulk causes architectural mistakes that are expensive to fix after deployment.

children.title: Constructor Patterns and Initialization
children.description: The constructor runs exactly once at deployment, setting immutable ownership and initial state that the contract relies on for the rest of its life. Leaving critical initialization outside the constructor or using an initializer pattern without re-entrancy protection opens ownership-hijacking vectors.

children.title: Emitting Events for Off-Chain Consumers
children.description: Events write data to transaction logs at a fraction of the cost of storage and are the primary mechanism by which frontends and indexers learn about state changes. Missing events on critical state transitions makes a contract opaque to monitoring and breaks every off-chain integration that relies on logs.

children.title: Indexed Topics for Efficient Filtering
children.description: Marking up to three event parameters as indexed stores them as log topics, enabling Ethereum nodes to filter billions of logs by that field without scanning every block. Without indexed topics, querying historical events becomes impractically slow for any dApp with significant usage.

children.title: require, revert, and assert
children.description: require validates external inputs and reverts with a refund of remaining gas on failure, revert allows structured errors, and assert catches invariant violations that indicate a code bug. Using assert for input validation burns all remaining gas unnecessarily, while misusing require for internal invariants hides true bugs.

children.title: Custom Errors for Gas-Efficient Reverts
children.description: Custom errors defined with the error keyword encode only a selector and packed arguments on revert, costing far less gas than string messages while conveying structured diagnostic information. Sticking with string reverts in high-traffic contracts penalizes every user who triggers a failed transaction.

smart-contract.title: Local Development with Foundry
smart-contract.description: This layer sets up a professional development workflow using Foundry and the Forge toolchain. A fast local environment is critical for iterating on contracts confidently.
smart-contract.challenge: Port the registry contract into a Foundry project and deploy it to a local Anvil node with a script.

children.title: Installing Foundry and forge build
children.description: Foundry is a blazing-fast Rust-based toolchain that compiles, tests, and deploys Solidity without a JavaScript runtime, dramatically shortening feedback loops. Working without a reproducible local toolchain forces you to rely on Remix for everything, which does not scale to multi-file projects or CI pipelines.

children.title: Project Layout and Remappings
children.description: Foundry expects contracts in src/, tests in test/, and scripts in script/, with remappings.txt or foundry.toml controlling how import paths resolve. Incorrect remappings cause compilation failures when library imports do not resolve, which is the number-one setup problem for developers migrating from Hardhat.

children.title: Running Anvil for Local Testing
children.description: Anvil spins up a local Ethereum node in milliseconds with pre-funded accounts and instant mining, giving you a realistic execution environment without spending real or testnet ETH. Testing only against in-process mock providers hides timing bugs and gas estimation differences that only appear on a real node.

children.title: Mainnet Forking for Integration Tests
children.description: Anvil can fork mainnet at a specific block, giving your tests access to real protocol state, real token balances, and live oracle prices without deploying anything. Skipping fork tests means you discover integration bugs only after deploying to a public testnet, where iteration is slow and mistakes are visible.

children.title: Writing Forge Scripts
children.description: Forge scripts are Solidity contracts that run as deployment automation with access to cheatcodes for broadcasting transactions, making deployments reproducible and auditable. Manual deployment via copy-paste or Remix introduces human error, generates no on-chain record, and makes re-deployment to new networks error-prone.

children.title: Managing Dependencies with forge install
children.description: Foundry manages dependencies as git submodules, allowing you to pin exact library versions and reproduce builds months later. Copying library files manually into your project makes audits harder and causes you to miss security patches when upstream libraries are updated.

children.title: foundry.toml Configuration
children.description: foundry.toml centralizes compiler version, optimizer settings, network RPC URLs, and test verbosity, ensuring every team member and CI job uses identical settings. Undocumented per-machine settings cause tests that pass locally to fail in CI and make it impossible to reproduce gas benchmarks.

children.title: Environment Variables and Secrets
children.description: Private keys and RPC API keys must be loaded from environment variables or encrypted keystores, never hardcoded in scripts or committed to version control. A single leaked private key in a public repository has cost teams millions of dollars in stolen funds and protocol exploits.

smart-contract.title: Token Standards with OpenZeppelin
smart-contract.description: This layer teaches the standard token interfaces and how to build on audited OpenZeppelin contracts. Reusing battle-tested code reduces bugs and speeds delivery.
smart-contract.challenge: Create an ERC20 token that inherits from OpenZeppelin and add a capped supply.

children.title: The ERC20 Standard Interface
children.description: ERC20 defines the minimum set of functions — transfer, approve, allowance, balanceOf — that any token must implement for wallets and DeFi protocols to interact with it without custom integration. Deviating from the standard by returning nothing on transfer causes silent failures in contracts that expect a boolean return value, a class of bug that has drained real protocols.

children.title: Allowance and the Approve-TransferFrom Pattern
children.description: The allowance mechanism lets a third-party contract spend tokens on a user's behalf, which is how DEXes, lending protocols, and staking vaults operate. Misunderstanding the double-spend race condition in approve makes tokens vulnerable to front-running, which is why increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance exist.

children.title: Ownership and Transfer Mechanics
children.description: ERC721 assigns unique token IDs to specific owners and requires safeTransfer checks to prevent tokens from being permanently locked inside contracts that cannot handle them. Ignoring the safeTransfer vs transfer distinction causes NFTs to become permanently inaccessible when sent to contracts that do not implement the receiver hook.

children.title: Metadata and the tokenURI Pattern
children.description: The ERC721 metadata extension defines tokenURI, which returns a JSON pointer to off-chain metadata that marketplaces like OpenSea read to display images and traits. Returning a centralized server URL without a fallback makes your NFT collection worthless if that server goes offline.

children.title: Ownable and Single-Owner Contracts
children.description: OpenZeppelin's Ownable provides a single privileged address that can execute administrative functions, with transferOwnership and renounceOwnership for lifecycle management. Implementing ownership from scratch introduces subtle bugs like two-step transfers being skipped or renounce being irreversible without warning, which has caused permanent loss of contract control.

children.title: Role-Based Access with AccessControl
children.description: AccessControl lets you define multiple roles (MINTER, PAUSER, ADMIN) and grant them independently, which is essential once a single-owner model becomes too coarse-grained for production operations. Centralizing all privileges in one owner account creates a single point of failure that becomes the primary target of social engineering attacks.

children.title: Inheritance and super Calls
children.description: Solidity uses C3 linearization to resolve multiple inheritance, and failing to call super in overridden functions silently drops logic from parent contracts. A missing super._beforeTokenTransfer() call in an ERC721 override has caused NFT contracts to skip transfer hooks and break operator approvals.

children.title: OpenZeppelin Contract Wizard
children.description: The wizard generates audited, battle-tested boilerplate for ERC20, ERC721, and governance tokens with correct inheritance chains, saving hours of setup and avoiding common pitfalls. Using the wizard as a starting point rather than a finished product is key — you must still understand every generated line before deploying.

smart-contract.title: Testing and Coverage with Forge
smart-contract.description: This layer focuses on writing thorough unit tests in Solidity using Forge. Strong test coverage is the foundation of safe contract development.
smart-contract.challenge: Write a full Forge test suite for your ERC20 token covering minting, transfers, and the supply cap.

children.title: Test Contract Structure and setUp
children.description: Forge tests are Solidity contracts where any function prefixed with test is run as a test case, and setUp is called before each test to establish a clean state. Running tests without proper isolation means one test's side effects contaminate subsequent tests and produces non-deterministic results that are hard to debug.

children.title: Assertions and vm Cheatcodes
children.description: Forge ships assertEq, assertGt, and expectRevert for expressing test expectations, and the vm cheatcode object lets you manipulate block timestamps, caller addresses, and ether balances within a test. Without cheatcodes you cannot test time-locked logic, multi-user scenarios, or access-control reverts, leaving large portions of your contract untested.

children.title: Measuring Coverage with forge coverage
children.description: forge coverage instruments the contract bytecode and reports which lines, branches, and functions are exercised by your tests, surfacing untested code paths before deployment. Shipping contracts with low branch coverage means edge cases that could drain funds or permanently lock tokens have never been executed.

children.title: Gas Snapshots for Regression Prevention
children.description: forge snapshot records gas usage per test into a file, and subsequent runs diff against it to alert you when a change unexpectedly increases gas costs. Without snapshots, a refactor that adds a few hundred thousand gas to a frequently called function ships silently and surprises users with much higher transaction fees.

children.title: Expecting Reverts with vm.expectRevert
children.description: vm.expectRevert asserts that the next call reverts with a specific selector or message, which is how you verify that your access control, input validation, and invariant checks actually fire. Untested revert conditions mean your safety mechanisms might have typos or wrong conditions that let malicious inputs pass silently.

children.title: Asserting Events with vm.expectEmit
children.description: vm.expectEmit verifies that a specific event with exact topic and data values is emitted during a call, ensuring your logging stays in sync with your state transitions. Missing event assertions let broken emit statements ship unnoticed, breaking every frontend and indexer that relies on those logs.

children.title: Property-Based Fuzz Testing
children.description: Forge fuzz tests accept function parameters and automatically generate hundreds of random inputs, finding edge cases that no human would think to write as unit tests. A contract with 100% unit test coverage can still have a critical overflow bug that only manifests on a specific boundary value a fuzzer discovers in seconds.

children.title: Invariant Testing for Protocol Properties
children.description: Invariant tests define properties that must hold after any sequence of calls — such as total supply always equaling the sum of all balances — and Forge tries to violate them with stateful random call sequences. Without invariant tests, complex multi-function interactions that individually pass unit tests can combine to break fundamental protocol guarantees.

smart-contract.title: Hardhat and JavaScript Tooling
smart-contract.description: This layer introduces the Hardhat framework and its JavaScript ecosystem for scripting and testing. Many teams use Hardhat for its plugin richness and frontend integration.
smart-contract.challenge: Recreate the token tests in a Hardhat project using ethers.js and run them against a local network.

children.title: Hardhat Configuration and Network Setup
children.description: Hardhat uses a JavaScript/TypeScript configuration file to define compiler settings, network RPC endpoints, and plugin registrations, serving as the central control plane for the entire project. Misconfigured networks cause deployments to go to the wrong chain, and incorrect compiler settings produce bytecode that does not match the verified source on Etherscan.

children.title: Hardhat Tasks and the Console
children.description: Hardhat tasks are scriptable CLI commands that interact with deployed contracts, and the Hardhat console gives a REPL attached to a live network for exploratory debugging. Without tasks and the console, every interaction requires writing and running a full script, which slows down exploratory debugging and manual verification.

children.title: Writing Mocha/Chai Tests in Hardhat
children.description: Hardhat integrates with Mocha and Chai to let you write describe/it test suites in TypeScript that deploy contracts and assert on return values using a familiar JavaScript testing idiom. Teams familiar with JavaScript testing ecosystems can apply existing knowledge directly, but conflating JavaScript async patterns with Solidity synchronous state can introduce subtle timing bugs.

children.title: Providers, Signers, and Contract Instances
children.description: ethers.js wraps JSON-RPC through Provider objects, represents signing keys as Signer objects, and connects both to a contract ABI via Contract instances to produce a typed on-chain API. Confusing providers with signers causes read-only calls to succeed but write calls to fail with unhelpful errors about missing signer.

children.title: hardhat-etherscan for Contract Verification
children.description: The hardhat-etherscan plugin submits your constructor arguments and source files to Etherscan programmatically, making source verification a one-line command rather than a manual form submission. Unverified contracts are treated as suspicious by users, auditors, and DeFi integrations, which reduces adoption and trust.

children.title: hardhat-gas-reporter for Cost Analysis
children.description: hardhat-gas-reporter hooks into the test runner and prints a per-function gas table after every test run, making it easy to spot regressions without running separate benchmarks. Ignoring gas costs during development means users encounter unexpectedly expensive transactions only after deployment, when the cost of fixing them is highest.

children.title: Choosing the Right Toolchain
children.description: Hardhat excels at frontend integration, TypeScript tooling, and a rich plugin ecosystem, while Foundry is faster for pure contract development, fuzz testing, and gas benchmarking. Using only one toolchain means missing capabilities that the other provides; most production teams combine both in the same repository.

children.title: Hardhat Network Forking
children.description: Hardhat Network supports forking mainnet at a pinned block number, letting JavaScript tests interact with live protocol state such as Uniswap pools and Chainlink feeds without deploying anything. Running integration tests only against mock contracts misses the real behavior of external protocols your contract depends on.

smart-contract.title: Advanced Solidity Patterns
smart-contract.description: This layer explores advanced design patterns including upgradeable proxies and reusable libraries. These patterns enable maintainable and modular protocols.
smart-contract.challenge: Convert your token into an upgradeable proxy and ship a second implementation that adds a burn function.

children.title: Transparent Proxy Pattern
children.description: The transparent proxy separates the upgradeable storage proxy from the logic implementation, routing admin calls to the proxy and user calls to the implementation to avoid function selector clashes. Misunderstanding storage layout rules in upgradeable contracts causes storage collisions where a new implementation overwrites values written by the previous one, silently corrupting state.

children.title: UUPS Proxy for Gas-Efficient Upgrades
children.description: UUPS moves the upgrade logic into the implementation contract itself, reducing the proxy's deployed bytecode and costing less gas per call compared to transparent proxies. Forgetting to include the upgrade function in the new implementation permanently bricks the upgrade path, locking the contract at its current version forever.

children.title: Library Contracts and using for
children.description: Libraries are deployed once and called via DELEGATECALL, allowing multiple contracts to share bytecode and reduce deployment costs, while the using for directive attaches library functions as methods on a type. Linking libraries incorrectly causes calls to revert at deployment because the linker placeholder is never replaced with the real address.

children.title: Factory and Minimal Proxy (EIP-1167) Patterns
children.description: Factory contracts deploy many instances of the same contract, and the EIP-1167 minimal proxy standard deploys a tiny forwarding stub that delegates all calls to one master implementation, reducing deployment cost by 90%. Deploying a full copy of a large contract for every user makes your protocol prohibitively expensive to onboard at scale.

children.title: Understanding Storage Slot Allocation
children.description: The Solidity compiler packs state variables into 32-byte slots in declaration order, and mappings compute their slot via keccak256, a layout that is fixed forever once a contract is deployed. Reordering state variables in an upgrade shifts every variable's slot, overwriting existing data with incorrect values and corrupting the entire contract state.

children.title: Reentrancy Guards
children.description: A reentrancy guard uses a boolean lock in storage to prevent an external call from re-entering the same function before the first execution completes, blocking the classic drain-and-recurse attack pattern. The most expensive DeFi hacks in history exploited reentrancy: without a guard, any function that sends ETH before updating state is vulnerable.

children.title: Delegatecall and Context Preservation
children.description: DELEGATECALL executes another contract's code in the calling contract's storage context, which is how proxies work, but it means a malicious or buggy implementation can overwrite the proxy's own admin slot. Every proxy upgrade must be checked for storage slot collisions between the new implementation and the proxy itself.

children.title: Low-Level Call and Return Data Handling
children.description: Solidity's call, staticcall, and delegatecall return a boolean success flag and raw bytes that must be manually checked and decoded, unlike high-level calls that revert automatically on failure. Ignoring the success boolean on a low-level call is a critical vulnerability that lets failed fund transfers proceed as if they succeeded.

smart-contract.title: Gas Optimization and EVM Internals
smart-contract.description: This layer teaches how to measure and reduce gas costs by understanding EVM storage and opcodes. Optimized contracts save users money and improve scalability.
smart-contract.challenge: Refactor your token contract to pack storage and use custom errors, then prove the gas savings with snapshots.

children.title: SSTORE and SLOAD Costs
children.description: Writing a non-zero value to a storage slot for the first time costs 20,000 gas (SSTORE), updating it costs 2,900, and reading it costs 2,100 — the most expensive common operations in any contract. Fetching the same storage variable in a loop repeatedly rather than caching it in a local memory variable multiplies gas costs linearly with the loop count.

children.title: Packing Variables into Storage Slots
children.description: Declaring multiple small variables (uint128, bool, address) consecutively lets the compiler pack them into a single 32-byte slot, halving the number of SSTORE and SLOAD calls. Declaring alternating large and small types prevents packing, doubling storage costs for no benefit.

children.title: Using calldata Instead of memory for Parameters
children.description: Marking external function parameters as calldata reads them directly from the transaction input without copying to memory, saving the CALLDATACOPY gas cost for every byte. Marking large arrays as memory in external functions silently adds an O(n) memory copy on every call, which becomes prohibitive for large inputs.

children.title: Loop and Arithmetic Optimization
children.description: Caching array.length outside a for loop, using unchecked blocks for arithmetic that cannot overflow, and incrementing with ++i instead of i++ each save small amounts of gas that compound significantly across thousands of transactions. Leaving unchecked overflow potential is dangerous in Solidity below 0.8, but in 0.8+ using unchecked for provably-safe math recovers the pre-0.8 gas cost.

children.title: Reading and Writing Storage with Yul
children.description: Yul is Solidity's intermediate assembly language; using sstore and sload directly bypasses the Solidity overhead of type checks and ABI encoding, enabling slot-level precision that is impossible in pure Solidity. Inline assembly is a sharp tool — an off-by-one in a slot address overwrites an unrelated variable and introduces corruption bugs that are nearly impossible to diagnose.

children.title: Profiling with Foundry Gas Reports
children.description: Foundry's gas report table shows min, mean, and max gas for every function across your test suite, giving you a data-driven baseline before and after optimization. Optimizing without measurement leads to premature micro-optimizations that make code unreadable while missing the actual hot paths that account for most of the cost.

children.title: Solidity Compiler Optimizer
children.description: The optimizer runs a configurable number of rounds to inline small functions, deduplicate code, and eliminate dead branches, trading larger bytecode for fewer runtime gas costs when runs is high, or smaller bytecode when runs is low. Disabling the optimizer entirely makes contracts cheaper to deploy but far more expensive for users to call, and the opposite tuning wastes deployment ETH.

children.title: Custom Errors vs String Reverts
children.description: Custom errors encode only a 4-byte selector plus tightly packed arguments, costing far less gas than the 3-byte minimum-length string cost of a require message plus its content. Switching an entire protocol from string reverts to custom errors can reduce revert overhead by 50%, meaningfully lowering gas costs for users of any contract that validates inputs heavily.

smart-contract.title: Building a DeFi Protocol
smart-contract.description: This layer applies prior skills to build a small decentralized finance protocol with oracles and incentives. Integrating external data and economic logic mirrors real production systems.
smart-contract.challenge: Build a staking vault where users deposit your token and earn rewards based on a Chainlink price feed.

children.title: Staking and Reward Math
children.description: Staking vaults track a per-share reward index that grows over time so that rewards can be calculated in O(1) without iterating over all depositors; the rewardPerShare pattern is the foundation of every major yield protocol. Implementing naive per-user reward loops causes gas costs that scale with the number of stakers and DoS the contract once adoption grows.

children.title: Collateral, Liquidations, and Health Factors
children.description: Lending protocols express collateral adequacy as a health factor — the ratio of collateral value to borrowed value — and liquidate positions when it falls below a threshold to keep the protocol solvent. Miscalculating the health factor using integer truncation or using the wrong decimal precision causes either over-liquidation that penalizes users or under-liquidation that leaves the protocol holding bad debt.

children.title: Reading Chainlink Data Feeds
children.description: Chainlink Data Feeds expose a latestRoundData function that returns a signed price, a timestamp, and a round ID, allowing smart contracts to consume off-chain asset prices trustlessly. Using the price without checking the updatedAt timestamp means your contract may silently operate on stale data during Chainlink downtime, pricing collateral incorrectly and enabling arbitrage exploits.

children.title: Oracle Staleness and Manipulation Risks
children.description: Oracle prices can become stale if the network is congested, and spot prices can be manipulated in the same block using flash loans unless you use TWAPs or multi-source aggregation. Protocols that rely on a single oracle source without staleness checks have been exploited repeatedly for hundreds of millions of dollars.

sideRight.title: Fork and Fuzz Testing for DeFi

children.title: Mainnet Fork Testing with Real Protocols
children.description: Fork tests pin mainnet state at a specific block and let your DeFi contract interact with real Uniswap pools, Chainlink feeds, and AAVE reserves, surfacing integration bugs that mock contracts hide. Testing against mocks that always return hardcoded prices misses slippage, fee, and precision edge cases that only manifest with real protocol behavior.

children.title: Invariant Testing for DeFi Protocols
children.description: DeFi invariants such as total shares always backed by at least total assets and sum of all debts never exceeding collateral value are critical properties that invariant fuzz tests systematically try to violate. Without invariant tests, complex interaction paths between deposit, borrow, liquidate, and redeem can combine to break solvency in ways no unit test anticipated.

children.title: Fee-on-Transfer and Non-Standard Tokens
children.description: Some ERC20 tokens deduct a fee on transfer so the recipient receives less than the sender sent, breaking any protocol that assumes transferred amount equals received amount. Using the post-transfer balance delta instead of the requested transfer amount is the only safe way to account for fee-on-transfer tokens.

children.title: Protocol Fee Accumulation Patterns
children.description: Accumulating fees in a separate reserve variable rather than mixing them with user deposits makes accounting clear and enables governance to withdraw fees without touching user principal. Commingling protocol fees with user funds makes off-chain accounting impossible and creates legal and audit complications for the protocol team.

smart-contract.title: Auditing and Mainnet Deployment
smart-contract.description: This final layer covers security review, verification, and shipping contracts to mainnet. Careful deployment and monitoring protect real user funds.
smart-contract.challenge: Run Slither on your staking vault, fix findings, and deploy a verified version to a public testnet.

children.title: Running Slither and Interpreting Detectors
children.description: Slither performs static analysis on Solidity source code and reports issues across 80+ detectors covering reentrancy, unchecked returns, incorrect ERC20 implementations, and more without executing the code. Skipping static analysis before deployment is negligent: Slither catches classes of bugs in seconds that human reviewers routinely miss in multi-day audits.

children.title: Self-Audit Checklists
children.description: Structured checklists ensure that every contract goes through the same security review gates — reentrancy, access control, arithmetic overflow, oracle freshness, and upgrade safety — before deployment. Relying on memory rather than a checklist means reviewers consistently miss the same category of issues, which is how recurring exploit patterns persist across different protocols.

children.title: Verifying Source Code with Foundry or Hardhat
children.description: Source verification submits your Solidity source, compiler settings, and constructor arguments to Etherscan so anyone can confirm that the deployed bytecode matches the code they are reading. Unverified contracts cannot be read by users, auditors, or DeFi integrations, which kills trust and adoption.

children.title: Deployment Scripts and Secret Management
children.description: A deterministic deployment script stored in version control ensures every network gets identical contract state, and secrets must be sourced from environment variables or hardware wallets — never hardcoded. Hardcoding private keys or RPC secrets in scripts is the leading cause of mainnet fund theft from developer repositories.

children.title: Transferring Ownership to a Multisig
children.description: Deploying a contract with a developer EOA as owner and then transferring to a multisig like Gnosis Safe requires M-of-N keyholders to sign any administrative action, removing the single-private-key failure mode. Leaving ownership at a single EOA after mainnet launch means one phished or compromised developer key can drain or permanently modify the entire protocol.

children.title: Timelock Controllers for Protocol Changes
children.description: A timelock controller delays execution of privileged transactions by a configurable period, giving users time to exit the protocol if they disagree with an upcoming upgrade or parameter change. Protocols without timelocks can rug-pull users silently in a single block, which is why major DeFi protocols enforce 24–72 hour timelocks on all admin actions.

children.title: On-Chain Event Monitoring
children.description: Subscribing to contract events via websocket RPC or an indexing service like The Graph gives you real-time visibility into all state-changing transactions, enabling rapid response to anomalous behavior. Without monitoring, exploits can silently drain a protocol for hours before anyone notices, as happened in several nine-figure DeFi hacks.

children.title: Incident Response and Emergency Pause
children.description: OpenZeppelin's Pausable pattern lets an authorized address halt all state-changing operations in a single transaction, buying time to investigate and patch a live exploit. Deploying without a pause mechanism means your only option during an active exploit is to publicly announce it and hope users can withdraw faster than the attacker can drain.

ethereum-dev.title: Ethereum and the EVM
ethereum-dev.description: This layer explains how Ethereum works as a world computer and how the EVM processes transactions. A clear mental model of accounts and state is the basis for all later work.
ethereum-dev.challenge: Use a block explorer to trace a real transaction and document every field it contains.

children.title: Accounts, State, and the World Computer Model
children.description: Ethereum maintains a global state tree of accounts — externally owned accounts controlled by private keys and contract accounts governed by bytecode. Without understanding how state transitions are triggered by transactions and committed per block, you will misread block explorers, miscalculate gas, and build mental models that break when the network behaves unexpectedly.

children.title: Transactions, Gas, and Finality
children.description: Every state change on Ethereum is initiated by a signed transaction that pays gas to compensate validators and prevent spam. Misunderstanding the EIP-1559 fee market — base fee plus priority tip — causes transaction failures during high congestion and leads to overpaying or underpricing submissions that get stuck in the mempool.

children.title: EVM Execution and Opcode Model
children.description: The EVM is a stack-based virtual machine that processes bytecode opcodes one at a time inside a sandboxed environment, with each opcode costing a deterministic amount of gas. Understanding the stack, memory, and storage data locations is mandatory before optimizing any contract, because confusing them causes silent overspending or data corruption.

children.title: Blocks, Slots, and Proof-of-Stake Finality
children.description: Since the Merge, Ethereum uses a Beacon Chain of slots and epochs where validators propose and attest to blocks, replacing proof-of-work with proof-of-stake. Not knowing when a block is considered finalized (two epochs, roughly 13 minutes) leads to dApps that treat probabilistically confirmed transactions as final, creating double-spend windows.

children.title: Using Etherscan to Inspect Transactions
children.description: Etherscan decodes raw RLP-encoded transactions into human-readable fields — nonce, gas price, input data, and internal calls — making it the fastest debugging tool for live network issues. Developers who skip learning Etherscan waste hours trying to reproduce issues locally that would take seconds to diagnose on the explorer.

children.title: Wei, Gwei, and Ether Unit Conversions
children.description: Ethereum denominations span 18 orders of magnitude — 1 ether equals 10^18 wei — and most library functions expect wei, not ether. Passing an ether value where wei is expected silently inflates amounts by 10^18, a class of bug that has caused catastrophic miscalculations in production contracts.

children.title: Mainnet, Testnets, and the Development Stack
children.description: Ethereum has multiple live testnets (Sepolia, Holesky) and local simulation environments (Hardhat Network, Anvil) that each behave slightly differently in terms of block times and precompile availability. Developing only against a local node and skipping testnet deployment hides issues with RPC rate limits, real gas markets, and actual block timing that only appear on public networks.

children.title: JSON-RPC and Node Providers
children.description: Applications communicate with Ethereum nodes over JSON-RPC by calling methods like eth_getTransactionReceipt and eth_call, either against a self-hosted node or a provider like Infura or Alchemy. Using a single provider endpoint without fallback logic and rate-limit handling makes your dApp unavailable whenever that provider has an outage.

ethereum-dev.title: Writing Smart Contracts in Solidity
ethereum-dev.description: This layer builds core Solidity skills for writing functional contracts. Knowing the language well lets you express on-chain logic safely.
ethereum-dev.challenge: Write a crowdfunding contract that accepts ether and tracks each contributor.

children.title: Types, Storage Layout, and Data Locations
children.description: Solidity has three data locations — storage (persistent on-chain), memory (temporary per call), and calldata (read-only input) — and the compiler assigns each variable to one based on type and context. Misassigning data locations copies large arrays unnecessarily into memory on every call or writes ephemeral values to expensive persistent storage, creating both gas waste and correctness bugs.

children.title: Functions, Visibility, and Modifiers
children.description: Functions in Solidity carry visibility specifiers (public, external, internal, private) that control who can call them, and modifiers that inject pre/post conditions around function bodies. Marking an administrative function public instead of external, or forgetting the onlyOwner modifier, is the most common access-control bug and has been exploited to drain funds from production protocols.

children.title: Emitting Events for Off-Chain Consumers
children.description: Solidity events write indexed and unindexed data to transaction logs at a fraction of storage cost, and are the primary channel through which frontends and indexers learn that contract state has changed. Omitting events on critical state transitions makes a contract opaque to monitoring, breaks every off-chain integration, and makes historical queries impossible without re-replaying blocks.

children.title: require, revert, and Custom Errors
children.description: require validates external inputs and refunds remaining gas on failure, revert with a custom error provides structured diagnostics, and assert guards internal invariants that should never be violated. Using string messages in revert statements instead of custom errors wastes gas on every failed transaction and obscures diagnostic information from callers who want machine-readable errors.

children.title: Deployment, ABI, and Bytecode
children.description: Deploying a contract sends a transaction with empty to field and the constructor bytecode as data, returning the immutable contract address and runtime bytecode that can never change. The ABI is the off-chain specification of function signatures and types that ethers.js uses to encode calldata; deploying with a mismatched ABI silently calls wrong functions or sends malformed data.

children.title: Contract Interaction Patterns
children.description: Contracts interact with one another through high-level calls (typed ABI-encoded), low-level call/delegatecall/staticcall, and interface definitions that enforce a minimum required API. Using low-level call without checking the boolean return value is a critical vulnerability because failed ETH transfers and failed contract calls return false instead of reverting, allowing the caller to proceed as if the call succeeded.

children.title: Remix IDE for Rapid Iteration
children.description: Remix provides an in-browser Solidity compiler, debugger, and one-click deployment to a JavaScript VM or injected Web3 provider, making it the fastest way to test a contract idea without any local setup. Relying exclusively on Remix for multi-file projects is impractical — its limitations in import resolution and version control integration force most teams to migrate to Hardhat or Foundry for anything beyond prototypes.

children.title: Solidity Compiler Versioning and Pragmas
children.description: Pinning a pragma version like pragma solidity 0.8.24 ensures the contract is compiled with known semantics, because behavior and security properties have changed significantly across minor versions. Floating pragmas with the ^ caret symbol allow a newer breaking compiler to produce different bytecode than the one used in testing, making audits non-reproducible.

ethereum-dev.title: Connecting Apps with ethers.js
ethereum-dev.description: This layer connects browser and Node applications to Ethereum using ethers.js. Frontend integration turns contracts into usable products.
ethereum-dev.challenge: Build a web page that connects a wallet and lets users donate to your crowdfunding contract.

children.title: Providers: Reading Chain State
children.description: An ethers.js Provider wraps a JSON-RPC connection and exposes methods like getBalance, getBlock, and call to read Ethereum state without requiring a private key. Confusing a read-only Provider with a Signer causes cryptic errors when attempting to send transactions, and using an unauthenticated public RPC endpoint without rate limiting makes your application fragile under any meaningful load.

children.title: Signers: Signing and Broadcasting Transactions
children.description: A Signer holds or delegates access to a private key and can sign messages, typed data, and transactions before broadcasting them to the network. Using a plain Wallet object in production frontend code exposes the raw private key in JavaScript memory where browser extensions and XSS attacks can steal it — hardware wallets and injected providers should be used instead.

children.title: Contract Instances and ABI Binding
children.description: ethers.js Contract objects bind an ABI to an address and a provider or signer, generating typed JavaScript methods that encode calldata and decode return values automatically. Passing an outdated ABI to a Contract instance silently calls wrong function selectors or fails to decode return values, producing undefined or incorrectly typed data in the frontend.

children.title: Listening to Events and Querying Logs
children.description: ethers.js exposes contract.on and contract.queryFilter to subscribe to real-time events and fetch historical logs using an EventFilter derived from the ABI. Forgetting to remove listeners with contract.off or contract.removeAllListeners causes memory leaks and duplicate event processing in single-page applications that re-mount components.

children.title: Transaction Lifecycle: Pending to Confirmed
children.description: After broadcasting a transaction with signer.sendTransaction, you receive a TransactionResponse whose wait method polls until the transaction is mined and returns a TransactionReceipt with the actual gas used and event logs. Not awaiting the receipt before updating UI state causes race conditions where the frontend reflects a pending change that may still revert.

children.title: Handling JSON-RPC Errors Gracefully
children.description: Ethereum JSON-RPC errors carry numeric codes and provider-specific messages that must be parsed to distinguish user rejections, insufficient gas, contract reverts, and network failures. Catching all errors with a generic handler and displaying raw error objects exposes internal provider state to users and makes debugging nearly impossible when issues are reported in production.

children.title: parseEther, formatEther, and BigInt Arithmetic
children.description: ethers.js v6 represents on-chain integers as native JavaScript BigInt, and utilities like parseEther and formatUnits convert between human-readable strings and wei values. Mixing JavaScript number arithmetic with BigInt values produces NaN or truncated results because JavaScript floats cannot represent 18-decimal-place token amounts without loss of precision.

children.title: ABI Encoding and Decoding with ethers.js
children.description: ethers.js AbiCoder encodes and decodes raw calldata payloads independent of a contract instance, which is essential for building low-level multicall batches or verifying encoded data returned by proxy contracts. Using the wrong parameter types in an encode call produces calldata that silently reverts, and decoding with mismatched types produces corrupted values without throwing an error.

ethereum-dev.title: Running Nodes with Geth
ethereum-dev.description: This layer covers running an Ethereum execution client using Geth and querying it directly. Operating a node deepens understanding of the network and removes reliance on third parties.
ethereum-dev.challenge: Sync a Geth node on a testnet and query your crowdfunding contract through its JSON-RPC endpoint.

children.title: Execution Client vs Consensus Client
children.description: Since the Merge, Ethereum requires two cooperating processes: an execution client (like Geth) that manages the EVM and transaction pool, and a consensus client (like Lighthouse or Prysm) that manages the beacon chain and block finalization. Running only Geth without a paired consensus client produces a node that syncs historical data but cannot follow the chain head or submit attestations.

children.title: Syncing Modes: Full, Snap, and Archive
children.description: Geth supports snap sync (fast initial sync using state snapshots), full sync (validates every block), and archive mode (stores every historical state), and the choice determines both disk usage and which historical queries are available. Deploying a snap-synced node for a protocol that needs historical balance lookups causes eth_getBalance calls for old blocks to fail because the state is not retained.

children.title: Core eth_ Namespace Methods
children.description: The eth_ JSON-RPC namespace exposes methods like eth_call (simulate a transaction), eth_estimateGas (estimate gas cost), and eth_getLogs (fetch event logs) that form the foundation of all dApp backend queries. Calling eth_call without specifying a block number returns current state, which breaks historical simulations and causes non-deterministic test behavior when re-running against a live node.

children.title: Managing Accounts and Keystores
children.description: Geth stores account private keys in encrypted keystore files using a password-derived key and a JSON v3 format, separating key storage from the node's network-facing process. Storing keystore files on the same machine as a hot node exposes them to network-based attacks — production setups should use hardware signing modules or remote signing services instead.

children.title: Monitoring Node Sync Status
children.description: Geth exposes eth_syncing to report how many blocks remain until sync is complete, and eth_blockNumber to check the current head, both critical for health checks in automated deployments. Using a partially synced node for application queries silently returns stale data — blocks and state from hours or days ago — without any error, causing subtle but critical misinformation in production systems.

children.title: Running Geth on a Testnet
children.description: Connecting Geth to Sepolia or Holesky with the --sepolia or --holesky flag configures chain ID, genesis parameters, and peer discovery automatically, giving you a realistic network environment without risking real funds. Testing exclusively on a local simulated chain hides real-world RPC latency, block time variability, and mempool competition that affect user experience on mainnet.

children.title: Firewall and RPC Exposure Rules
children.description: Geth's HTTP RPC server should never be exposed on a public IP without authentication because it can be used to drain accounts managed by the node and to submit arbitrary transactions. Several high-profile thefts have occurred when developers left --http.addr 0.0.0.0 open while the node held funded accounts, making this one of the most costly misconfigurations in the ecosystem.

children.title: geth attach and the JavaScript Console
children.description: geth attach opens an interactive JavaScript console connected to a running node via IPC or HTTP, providing direct access to the web3 and eth namespaces for debugging and administrative tasks. The console has access to all node functions including account signing, so running arbitrary scripts from untrusted sources in an attached session can compromise all managed accounts instantly.

ethereum-dev.title: Token Standards and Contract Composition
ethereum-dev.description: This layer teaches the major token standards and how contracts call one another. Composability is what makes Ethereum a rich application platform.
ethereum-dev.challenge: Add an ERC20 reward token that your crowdfunding contract mints to contributors.

children.title: ERC20: The Fungible Token Interface
children.description: ERC20 defines the minimum interface — transfer, transferFrom, approve, allowance, balanceOf, totalSupply — that tokens must implement for wallets and DeFi protocols to interact with them without custom integration. Deviating from the standard by returning void instead of bool on transfer breaks every protocol that checks the return value, a real bug class that has caused millions in losses in non-compliant tokens.

children.title: ERC721: Non-Fungible Token Ownership
children.description: ERC721 assigns unique tokenIds to specific owner addresses and requires safeTransferFrom to call onERC721Received on the recipient contract, preventing tokens from being permanently locked inside contracts that cannot handle them. Ignoring the safeTransfer vs transfer distinction has permanently locked thousands of NFTs inside contracts that have no way to retrieve them.

children.title: Allowances and the Approve-TransferFrom Pattern
children.description: The allowance mechanism lets a third-party contract spend tokens on a user's behalf, which is how DEXes, lending protocols, and staking vaults pull funds from user wallets. Front-running the approve transaction allows a malicious spender to use the old allowance and then the new one in sequence — this is why increaseAllowance and permit (EIP-2612) exist as safer alternatives.

children.title: Reentrancy Awareness in Composable Calls
children.description: When a contract calls an external contract, execution transfers to the callee which can call back into the caller before the first call returns, enabling the classic reentrancy drain pattern if state is updated after the external call. Following the checks-effects-interactions pattern — updating all state variables before making any external call — is the primary defence and must become second nature before building any DeFi protocol.

children.title: Ownable and AccessControl
children.description: OpenZeppelin's Ownable provides a single privileged address with transferOwnership and renounceOwnership, while AccessControl supports multiple roles (MINTER_ROLE, PAUSER_ROLE) granted independently to different addresses. Rolling your own ownership logic introduces subtle bugs — missing a two-step transfer, making renounce irreversible, or checking msg.sender instead of _msgSender() in meta-transaction contexts — all of which have caused permanent loss of contract control.

children.title: Interfaces and Abstract Contracts for Composability
children.description: Interfaces define the minimum API a contract must expose for callers to interact with it in a type-safe way, while abstract contracts share implementation logic between children without being deployable on their own. Calling another contract without an interface casts it to address and uses low-level call, losing all type safety and forcing manual ABI encoding that is easy to get wrong under any schema change.

children.title: ERC1155 Multi-Token Standard
children.description: ERC1155 manages both fungible and non-fungible tokens in a single contract with a batched transfer API, reducing the number of transactions and contract deployments needed for games and multi-asset protocols. Treating ERC1155 IDs as independent ERC20 tokens and calling transfer instead of safeTransferFrom silently drops the receiver hook, locking tokens in contracts that have no withdrawal logic.

children.title: Using the OpenZeppelin Contracts Wizard
children.description: The OpenZeppelin Wizard generates audited, correctly wired boilerplate for ERC20, ERC721, and governance tokens with proper inheritance chains and selected extensions, saving hours of setup time. Treating the wizard output as a finished product rather than a starting point is dangerous — every generated line must be understood before deployment because the wizard cannot account for your specific business logic.

ethereum-dev.title: EVM Deep Dive
ethereum-dev.description: This layer dissects how Solidity compiles to bytecode and how the EVM executes opcodes. Low-level knowledge unlocks optimization and debugging skills.
ethereum-dev.challenge: Manually decode the function selector and calldata for a call to your reward token.

children.title: Stack, Memory, and Storage Data Locations
children.description: The EVM uses a 1024-element word stack for intermediate values, a byte-addressable memory region that expands quadratically in gas cost, and a persistent key-value storage mapped to 32-byte slots. Not knowing that memory expansion triggers increasing gas costs causes contracts that allocate large dynamic arrays in memory to silently exhaust gas on inputs that are only moderately large.

children.title: Key Opcodes: CALL, STATICCALL, DELEGATECALL
children.description: CALL executes another contract with a new context, STATICCALL forbids state changes during execution for read-only queries, and DELEGATECALL runs foreign code in the caller's own storage context — the mechanism underlying all proxy patterns. Misusing DELEGATECALL to run untrusted code in your own storage context allows the callee to overwrite your admin slot, ownership variable, or any other state with arbitrary values.

children.title: Function Selectors and Calldata Layout
children.description: Every contract call starts with a 4-byte function selector — the first four bytes of the keccak256 hash of the function signature — followed by ABI-encoded arguments in 32-byte padded slots. Two functions with different parameter types that hash to the same selector will collide, making one unreachable; this is an intentional attack vector in malicious contracts and an accidental bug in upgradeable systems.

children.title: Reading Bytecode and Decompiling Contracts
children.description: Runtime bytecode is what actually executes on the EVM — the constructor and immutables are resolved at deploy time and stripped — and being able to read disassembled opcodes lets you verify what a contract actually does, regardless of what its source claims. Closed-source or unverified contracts that your protocol must interact with can only be trusted if you have read their bytecode.

children.title: Writing Yul for Slot-Level Precision
children.description: Yul lets you write inline assembly inside Solidity using sload, sstore, and mload to bypass the language's abstraction layer and access exact storage slots and memory addresses. Yul removes all safety checks — an off-by-one slot address overwrites an adjacent variable, and wrong memory offsets produce silent data corruption with no revert, making it suitable only when you fully understand the layout you are manipulating.

children.title: Storage Slot Layout and keccak256 Mapping Addresses
children.description: Solidity packs state variables into 32-byte slots in declaration order, and mapping values are stored at keccak256(key . slot), a layout that must be reproduced exactly when writing Yul or when checking for storage collisions in proxy patterns. Reordering state variables in an upgrade shifts the slot of every subsequent variable, overwriting live data with values from adjacent slots that now have entirely different meanings.

children.title: Measuring Gas with Foundry Gas Reports
children.description: Foundry's built-in gas reports show min, mean, and max gas per function across the entire test suite, establishing a data-driven baseline before and after any optimization. Optimizing without measurement leads to premature micro-optimizations that make code unreadable while leaving the actual expensive hot paths — large loops, excessive storage writes — completely untouched.

children.title: SLOAD/SSTORE Costs and Caching Patterns
children.description: Reading a storage slot (SLOAD) costs 2,100 gas cold and 100 gas warm, and writing one costs 20,000 for the first write and 2,900 for subsequent updates within the same transaction. Reading the same storage variable inside a loop without caching it in a local memory variable multiplies the 2,100 cold cost by the loop count, which is often the single largest gas waste in naive contracts.

ethereum-dev.title: Indexing and Off-Chain Data
ethereum-dev.description: This layer shows how to index on-chain events for fast querying using The Graph. Efficient data access is essential for responsive dApps.
ethereum-dev.challenge: Build a subgraph that indexes contributions and display a live leaderboard in your dApp.

children.title: Event-Driven Indexing with Subgraph Manifests
children.description: A subgraph manifest (subgraph.yaml) declares which contract addresses and events to watch, which AssemblyScript mapping handlers to call on each event, and what GraphQL schema to expose, tying together the entire indexing pipeline. Pointing the manifest at the wrong contract address or ABI silently indexes no data, producing an empty subgraph with no error message that is hard to distinguish from a correctly deployed but unused contract.

children.title: Writing AssemblyScript Mapping Handlers
children.description: Mapping handlers are AssemblyScript functions that receive a generated event object and use the Graph node store API to create, update, and delete entities in the subgraph's database. Forgetting to call store.set after modifying an entity silently discards the change — the entity appears correct in memory but the updated version is never persisted to the indexed store.

children.title: Querying Subgraph Data with GraphQL
children.description: The Graph exposes indexed data via a GraphQL endpoint that supports filtering, sorting, and pagination using the where, orderBy, and first/skip parameters generated from the subgraph schema. Using skip-based pagination without ordering by a stable field returns inconsistent pages when new entities are indexed mid-query, causing duplicated or missing entries in the UI.

children.title: Pagination and Cursor-Based Queries
children.description: The Graph recommends using id_gt cursor-based pagination instead of skip/first for large datasets, because skip scans and discards N rows on every page request, making deep pages increasingly expensive as the dataset grows. Datasets with millions of entities such as DEX trade histories become completely unpageable with skip-based queries, causing timeouts and forcing costly subgraph re-architecture after launch.

children.title: Deploying Subgraphs to The Graph Network
children.description: Subgraphs are deployed to The Graph's hosted service or decentralized network using the Graph CLI (graph deploy), which compiles mappings and uploads the WASM bundle and manifest. Deploying without checking the subgraph health endpoint after deployment means indexing errors — schema mismatches, mapping panics — go unnoticed until users report missing data in production.

children.title: Subgraph Versioning and Re-Indexing
children.description: Deploying a new subgraph version triggers a full re-index from the startBlock, which can take hours for contracts with years of history, during which queries return stale data from the previous version. Choosing the correct startBlock (the contract deployment block) rather than block 0 reduces re-index time from days to hours for contracts deployed recently, and choosing too high a startBlock silently misses historical events.

children.title: Polling ethers.js Logs as a Lightweight Alternative
children.description: For small dApps where a full subgraph is overkill, ethers.js queryFilter fetches all logs for a given event and block range directly from an RPC node, which is adequate for a few hundred events but does not scale to thousands of blocks without pagination and retry logic. Not implementing log range pagination causes eth_getLogs calls to exceed the provider's 2,000-block or 10,000-event limits and fail silently or throw JSON-RPC range errors.

children.title: Caching Strategies for Off-Chain Data
children.description: Caching frequently queried subgraph responses in Redis or a CDN edge cache prevents repeated identical GraphQL requests from hammering the Graph node and reduces p99 latency from hundreds to single-digit milliseconds for popular queries. Not caching means every UI page load issues redundant GraphQL queries, and a spike in traffic overwhelms the indexer endpoint, causing degraded query response times for all users simultaneously.

ethereum-dev.title: Wallets, Signatures, and Security
ethereum-dev.description: This layer covers cryptographic signatures and secure user interactions. Signature handling enables gasless approvals and meta transactions while avoiding phishing risks.
ethereum-dev.challenge: Implement EIP-712 typed signatures so users can approve reward claims without an on-chain approval transaction.

children.title: ECDSA Signatures and ecrecover
children.description: Ethereum signatures are ECDSA signatures over the secp256k1 curve, producing a (v, r, s) tuple that can be verified on-chain with ecrecover to recover the signing address. Not salting signatures with a chain ID and contract address allows replay attacks where a valid signature for one contract or network is reused on another, which is how cross-chain replay exploits drained funds before EIP-155.

children.title: EIP-191 and Prefixed Message Signing
children.description: EIP-191 standardizes the prefix added to messages before signing — "\x19Ethereum Signed Message:\n" followed by the byte length — preventing users from accidentally signing a raw transaction payload when they think they are signing a readable message. Signing raw bytes without the prefix lets an attacker convince a user to sign something that is also a valid transaction, enabling arbitrary transaction submission with the user's key.

children.title: Structured Data Signing with EIP-712
children.description: EIP-712 defines a schema for signing structured on-chain data — domain separator, type hashes, and encoded fields — so wallets can display a human-readable breakdown of what the user is signing instead of an opaque hex blob. Using EIP-191 personal_sign for contract authorization data means wallets show users a raw hash with no field labels, making phishing attacks that swap the real data for malicious data undetectable.

children.title: Permit (EIP-2612) for Gasless Approvals
children.description: EIP-2612 extends ERC20 with a permit function that accepts an EIP-712 signature from the token holder, allowing a spender contract to set an allowance in the same transaction it uses it, removing the need for a separate approve transaction. Not implementing nonce management correctly in permit allows the same approval signature to be replayed after the nonce is consumed, enabling double-spending of gasless approval grants.

children.title: Nonce Management and Replay Attack Prevention
children.description: Every signed authorization must include a nonce — a value incremented on each use — that the contract validates and burns, ensuring the same signature cannot be submitted twice. Omitting nonce validation in off-chain authorization schemes allows any observer who captures a valid signature to replay it indefinitely, draining allowances or repeating privileged operations without the signer's consent.

children.title: Wallet Attack Vectors and Phishing Defense
children.description: Wallet phishing attacks use fake dApp domains, forged transaction previews, and malicious token approvals (approve(attacker, max_uint)) to steal funds without ever breaking cryptography. Building dApps that display clear human-readable summaries of every signature request, verify contract addresses against known ABIs before prompting users, and never request unlimited approvals are the minimum defences against the most common wallet attack class.

children.title: MetaMask Signing Methods Comparison
children.description: MetaMask exposes eth_sign (dangerous — signs raw 32-byte hashes), personal_sign (EIP-191 prefixed), and eth_signTypedData_v4 (EIP-712 structured), and choosing the wrong method either exposes users to phishing or displays an unreadable hex blob that users cannot verify. eth_sign is deprecated and should never be used because it allows the wallet to sign arbitrary 32-byte payloads that could represent a valid transaction.

children.title: Hardware Wallet Integration and EIP-712 Support
children.description: Hardware wallets like Ledger and Trezor display transaction details on a secure screen, making them the gold standard for signing high-value transactions because the signing key never touches a networked device. Not testing your dApp against hardware wallet limitations — Ledger's blind signing requirements, limited display rendering for complex EIP-712 structs — leads to a poor UX where power users cannot complete critical flows.

ethereum-dev.title: Testing and CI for dApps
ethereum-dev.description: This layer establishes automated testing and continuous integration for full stack dApps. Reliable pipelines catch regressions before they reach users.
ethereum-dev.challenge: Add a full test suite and a CI workflow that runs on every push for your crowdfunding dApp.

children.title: Unit and Integration Testing with Hardhat
children.description: Hardhat unit tests deploy contracts to an in-memory node and assert on return values and events using Mocha/Chai, while integration tests fork mainnet to run the same logic against real protocol state. Relying exclusively on unit tests with mock dependencies misses integration failures — wrong ABI versions, incorrect token decimals, unexpected revert messages from a real DEX — that only appear when the full system runs together.

children.title: Fuzz Testing with Foundry
children.description: Foundry fuzz tests accept typed function parameters and automatically generate hundreds of random inputs across the full value range, discovering edge cases and integer boundary bugs that no human would think to write as unit test cases. A contract with 100% line coverage from hand-written unit tests can still have a critical overflow or division-by-zero that a fuzzer finds in the first run with a random large value.

children.title: Hardhat Network Mainnet Forking
children.description: Hardhat Network forks mainnet at a pinned block, giving JavaScript tests access to real Uniswap pools, Chainlink oracles, and live token balances so you can test your contract against the real world without a deployment. Pinning the fork block ensures deterministic test results — using latest produces flaky tests because the state changes every 12 seconds as new blocks arrive.

children.title: Mocking Providers and Off-Chain Dependencies
children.description: Frontend tests should mock the Ethereum provider using libraries like mock-ethers or wagmi's test utilities to simulate wallet connection, transaction signing, and error states without a real node. Not mocking the provider makes frontend tests brittle and slow because they depend on network availability, and it makes it impossible to reproduce specific error states like user rejection or gas estimation failure in automated tests.

children.title: CI Pipeline with GitHub Actions
children.description: A GitHub Actions workflow that runs forge test and npx hardhat test on every pull request ensures that no regression merges to main, and the Foundry action (foundry-rs/foundry-toolchain) installs the toolchain without additional configuration. Running tests only locally and not in CI means a working-locally but broken-remotely change caused by a dependency version difference can silently merge and break production deployments.

children.title: Coverage Reporting in CI
children.description: Integrating forge coverage or solidity-coverage into CI and failing the pipeline below a coverage threshold prevents coverage from drifting downward as new code is added without tests. Coverage thresholds without branch coverage requirements are misleading — 80% line coverage can coexist with 20% branch coverage, leaving the majority of error-handling paths completely untested.

children.title: Testing React dApp Components with Vitest
children.description: Vitest with React Testing Library lets you test wallet-connected components in isolation by mocking the provider context, asserting on rendered transaction states, and simulating user interactions without opening a browser. Skipping component tests for wallet interactions means a provider API change or a redesigned connection modal breaks the user's ability to connect their wallet without any automated signal.

children.title: End-to-End Testing with Synpress
children.description: Synpress extends Playwright with MetaMask automation to write E2E tests that open a real browser, connect a wallet, sign transactions, and assert on the UI — the only way to catch bugs in the full user flow. Missing E2E tests for wallet interactions means launch-blocking bugs like the confirm button not appearing after sending a transaction are discovered by users during mainnet launch, not by developers during testing.

ethereum-dev.title: Mainnet Deployment and Operations
ethereum-dev.description: This final layer deploys the full dApp to mainnet and keeps it running safely. Operating in production requires monitoring, verification, and incident readiness.
topics: Gas and nonce management in production
ethereum-dev.challenge: Deploy and verify your complete crowdfunding dApp on a public network and wire up basic monitoring.

children.title: Deployment Scripts and Secret Management
children.description: Deployment scripts should read private keys from environment variables or encrypted keystores — never from hardcoded strings or committed .env files — and broadcast transactions in a single deterministic sequence that produces a known contract address. A single leaked private key in a public repository has drained protocol treasuries within minutes of discovery; secret scanning hooks and hardware signing modules are mandatory for any production deployment.

children.title: Contract Verification on Etherscan
children.description: Etherscan source verification submits constructor arguments, compiler settings, and source files so any user can read and audit the contract code directly from the explorer page. Deploying to mainnet without verifying source code makes the contract opaque to users, prevents security researchers from finding bugs pre-exploitation, and disqualifies the protocol from most DeFi integrations that require verified code.

children.title: Production Gas Pricing Strategy
children.description: Production deployments must query eth_maxFeePerGas and eth_maxPriorityFeePerGas from the network and add a buffer over the base fee to ensure timely inclusion, adapting to gas spikes that can increase fees tenfold within a single block. Using hardcoded gas prices in deployment scripts causes transactions to get stuck in the mempool for hours during congestion, blocking dependent deployments in a multi-contract system.

children.title: Nonce Management for Concurrent Transactions
children.description: Each Ethereum account has a monotonically increasing nonce, and transactions must be processed in strict nonce order — a gap caused by a failed or dropped transaction blocks all higher-nonce transactions from confirming. Automated deployment scripts that send multiple transactions concurrently must manage nonces manually to avoid collisions, and must handle the stuck-nonce scenario with a replacement transaction at the same nonce with higher gas.

children.title: On-Chain Monitoring with Tenderly or OpenZeppelin Defender
children.description: Tools like Tenderly Alerts and OpenZeppelin Defender Sentinels watch for specific contract events, function calls, or balance thresholds and send notifications the moment something anomalous happens — such as an unusually large withdrawal or a role-change event. Discovering an exploit from user complaints rather than automated alerts delays the response by hours, during which an attacker can drain remaining funds and exit through a mixer.

children.title: Incident Response and Emergency Pause
children.description: Every production protocol should have an emergency pause mechanism — a privileged pauseAll function using OpenZeppelin Pausable — that halts critical operations within one transaction of detecting an exploit, limiting the blast radius. Protocols without a pause mechanism have lost their entire TVL in a single block because they had no way to stop further exploitation after the initial attack was detected.

children.title: Upgrade Strategies: Proxy vs Immutable
children.description: Proxy upgrades allow bug fixes and feature additions but introduce governance risk and storage collision hazards, while immutable contracts provide trust guarantees at the cost of being unable to fix discovered vulnerabilities. Choosing a proxy pattern without implementing a timelock gives a compromised owner key the ability to upgrade to a malicious implementation in a single block, draining all user funds instantly.

children.title: Post-Deployment Operations and Bug Bounties
children.description: A responsible disclosure policy and a bug bounty program on platforms like Immunefi incentivize security researchers to report vulnerabilities privately rather than exploit or disclose them publicly, dramatically improving the protocol's security posture. Launching a protocol holding significant user funds without a bug bounty treats the entire user base as an unpaid security testing pool, with losses covered by users rather than the protocol.

solana-dev.title: Solana Architecture and Accounts
solana-dev.description: This layer introduces Solana's high-throughput design and its account-based programming model. Grasping accounts and rent is fundamental to everything on Solana.
solana-dev.challenge: Use the Solana CLI to create a keypair, airdrop test SOL, and inspect an account on devnet.

children.title: Accounts as the Universal Storage Unit
children.description: Every piece of state on Solana — wallet balances, program code, user data — lives in an account identified by a public key. Understanding that programs are stateless and only read or write accounts passed to them in each instruction is the mental model you need before writing a single line of on-chain code; without it you will misplace data and build programs that cannot scale.

children.title: Rent and Lamports
children.description: Accounts must maintain a minimum lamport balance proportional to their byte size to stay rent-exempt; accounts that fall below this threshold are purged by the runtime. Ignoring rent exemption when allocating accounts causes them to be deleted mid-operation, producing confusing instruction failures that are difficult to trace.

children.title: Instructions, Transactions, and Signers
children.description: A Solana transaction bundles one or more instructions, each targeting a program with a list of accounts and instruction data, and must be signed by every account marked as a signer. Misunderstanding which accounts require signatures causes authorization failures and makes it impossible to reason about transaction fees, which are charged per signature.

children.title: Proof of History and Throughput
children.description: Proof of History uses a verifiable delay function to encode the passage of time directly into the ledger, allowing validators to process thousands of transactions in parallel without waiting for global clock agreement. Without grasping PoH you will misunderstand why Solana's finality model and confirmation times differ fundamentally from Ethereum.

children.title: Solana CLI Setup and Keypair Management
