children.description: The Solana CLI lets you generate keypairs, airdrop test SOL on devnet, deploy programs, and inspect accounts from the terminal, making it the fastest way to interact with the network before writing any client code. Skipping the CLI and jumping straight to a framework means you lack the low-level intuition to debug program deployments or diagnose account state issues.

children.title: Devnet and Cluster Configuration
children.description: Solana provides devnet, testnet, and mainnet-beta clusters; setting the CLI to the correct cluster and using airdrops to fund test wallets is the very first step in any hands-on workflow. Accidentally targeting mainnet during development has caused real funds to be spent on unintended deployments.

children.title: Executable vs Non-Executable Accounts
children.description: Program accounts have their executable flag set and contain compiled BPF bytecode; data accounts are writable storage owned by a program that the program can freely modify. Confusing these two account types causes developers to attempt to write into program accounts or expect a data account to process instructions, both of which fail at runtime.

children.title: Ownership and Account Authority
children.description: Each account has an owner program that is the only entity allowed to modify its data or debit its lamports, enforcing a clear access-control boundary at the runtime level. Misunderstanding ownership means writing programs that try to mutate accounts they do not own, which fails silently or produces access-denied errors that are hard to trace without this mental model.

solana-dev.title: Rust for Solana
solana-dev.description: This layer builds the Rust foundations needed to write Solana programs. Comfort with ownership and borrowing is required to write safe on-chain code.
solana-dev.challenge: Write a small Rust program that serializes and deserializes an account struct with Borsh.

children.title: Ownership, Borrowing, and Lifetimes
children.description: Rust's ownership system enforces that each value has exactly one owner at a time and that references never outlive the data they point to, eliminating entire classes of memory bugs at compile time. Misunderstanding borrow rules is the number-one reason beginners cannot get Solana programs to compile; fighting the borrow checker instead of understanding it leads to excessive cloning that hurts performance.

children.title: Error Handling with Result and the ? Operator
children.description: Solana programs return ProgramResult, which is an alias for Result, and the ? operator propagates errors up the call stack automatically, making error handling explicit without verbose match blocks. Ignoring Result values or using unwrap in on-chain code causes programs to panic and abort the entire transaction, which burns compute units and gives users no actionable error information.

children.title: Structs and Enums for On-Chain Data
children.description: Structs model account state and instruction payloads in Solana programs; enums distinguish between instruction variants so a single program entrypoint can dispatch to multiple handlers. Poorly structured data types lead to accounts that are larger than necessary, wasting rent-exempt lamports across every user's account.

children.title: Traits and Generics for Reusable Code
children.description: Traits define shared behavior across types, and generics let you write one implementation that works for many concrete types, both of which are used extensively by the Solana SDK and Anchor macros. Without trait knowledge you cannot implement custom serialization, extend framework types, or understand the generated code produced by Anchor's derive macros.

children.title: Cargo Workspace and Dependency Management
children.description: Cargo manages Rust projects, resolves crate versions from crates.io, and compiles workspaces that contain multiple packages such as a program crate and a shared types crate. Mismatched crate versions between your program and your client libraries cause type mismatch errors that are confusing because both sides define what looks like the same struct.

children.title: Rustlings for Hands-On Practice
children.description: Rustlings provides small, focused exercises covering each Rust concept so you can build muscle memory for ownership, pattern matching, and iterators before applying them in a Solana context. Skipping structured exercises and jumping directly to Anchor programs means you will be fighting the language and the framework simultaneously, which doubles the time to first working program.

children.title: Borsh for Deterministic Serialization
children.description: Borsh is a binary serialization format designed for deterministic, canonical encoding, which is critical on-chain because every validator must produce identical byte representations. Using JSON or non-canonical serialization introduces non-determinism that breaks consensus and produces accounts that cannot be deserialized by other programs or clients.

children.title: BorshSerialize and BorshDeserialize Derives
children.description: Deriving BorshSerialize and BorshDeserialize on your account structs auto-generates the pack/unpack logic that reads from and writes to account data buffers. Writing manual serialization code instead of using derive macros introduces off-by-one bugs in byte offsets that silently corrupt account data on every write.

solana-dev.title: Native Solana Programs
solana-dev.description: This layer writes a Solana program without frameworks to understand the raw runtime. Knowing the native model demystifies what Anchor does for you.
topics: Building and deploying with the CLI
solana-dev.challenge: Write a native counter program that increments a value stored in a program owned account.

children.title: The process_instruction Entrypoint
children.description: Every native Solana program exposes a single process_instruction function that receives the program ID, a slice of AccountInfo objects, and raw instruction data bytes; all routing to specific handlers must be implemented manually. Without understanding this raw entrypoint you will not comprehend what Anchor generates for you, and you will be helpless when debugging programs that misbehave at the instruction dispatch level.

children.title: Deserializing Instruction Data
children.description: Instruction data arrives as a raw byte slice and must be deserialized by the program into a typed enum or struct that identifies which handler to call and what arguments to use. Failing to validate instruction data length before deserializing opens the program to malformed input that crashes the deserializer and causes unexpected transaction failures.

children.title: Manual Signer and Owner Checks
children.description: In native programs every security-sensitive account must be explicitly verified: is_signer must be checked to confirm authority and owner must match the expected program to prevent account substitution attacks. Missing these checks is the leading cause of Solana program exploits, allowing attackers to pass fake accounts that the program treats as legitimate.

children.title: AccountInfo Fields and Mutability
children.description: AccountInfo exposes key, lamports, data, owner, is_signer, is_writable, and executable; only accounts marked is_writable can have their data or lamports modified in a transaction. Attempting to write to an account not marked writable causes a runtime error, and neglecting to mark an account writable in the transaction prevents legitimate updates.

children.title: Deriving and Verifying PDAs
children.description: Program derived addresses are deterministic public keys computed from a program ID and seed bytes that intentionally fall off the elliptic curve so no private key exists for them; the program itself can sign for them via invoke_signed. Using random keypairs instead of PDAs for program-owned accounts makes it impossible for the program to autonomously sign transfers or CPIs without storing a private key on-chain, which is impossible and insecure.

children.title: invoke_signed for PDA Authority
children.description: invoke_signed passes the seeds used to derive a PDA to the runtime, which re-derives the address and verifies the match before granting signature authority, enabling programs to act as signers for accounts they own. Passing wrong seeds to invoke_signed causes the runtime to reject the PDA signature, making the CPI fail even though the program logically owns the account.

children.title: Building and Deploying with the Solana CLI
children.description: Native programs are compiled to BPF bytecode with cargo build-bpf and deployed to a cluster using solana program deploy, which uploads the bytecode to a buffer account and then links it to the program address. Misunderstanding the two-step buffer deployment process causes partial uploads to leave dangling buffer accounts that waste rent-exempt SOL.

children.title: Program Logs and solana logs
children.description: msg! macros in Solana programs emit log lines that can be streamed with solana logs or fetched via RPC getTransaction, providing the primary debugging mechanism for on-chain code. Without using program logs, diagnosing why an instruction fails requires guesswork because the runtime error codes are low-level integers without context.

solana-dev.title: Building with Anchor
solana-dev.description: This layer adopts the Anchor framework to remove boilerplate and add safety. Anchor is the standard way most teams build Solana programs.
solana-dev.challenge: Rebuild the counter as an Anchor program using account constraints and a PDA.

children.title: The #[program] Macro and Instruction Handlers
children.description: Anchor's #[program] attribute macro transforms a Rust module into a fully wired Solana program, automatically generating the entrypoint, discriminator-based dispatch, and error handling so you can focus on business logic. Not understanding what the macro generates means you cannot debug panics that happen inside generated code or diagnose why a handler is never reached.

children.title: Account Contexts and the #[derive(Accounts)] Macro
children.description: Every Anchor instruction handler receives a strongly typed context struct derived with #[derive(Accounts)] that declares every account the instruction needs, along with constraint annotations that Anchor validates before invoking your handler. Placing business logic directly in the Accounts struct instead of using proper constraint attributes produces code that is hard to audit and misses the automatic account validation Anchor provides.

children.title: seeds and bump Constraints for PDAs
children.description: Anchor's seeds and bump constraints in the #[account] attribute automatically derive and verify a PDA at the start of an instruction, ensuring the account passed by the client matches the one the program expects. Deriving PDAs manually inside the handler body instead of using constraints means the derivation happens after the account is already in use, opening a window for account substitution attacks.

children.title: init and init_if_needed for Account Initialization
children.description: Anchor's init constraint allocates and initializes a new account in a single instruction, setting its owner to the program and writing the discriminator, while init_if_needed handles idempotent creation. Using init_if_needed carelessly without a reinitialization guard allows attackers to reset existing account state by calling the same instruction again.

children.title: The Interface Definition Language (IDL)
children.description: Anchor generates a JSON IDL that describes every instruction, account, and error in your program, which the TypeScript client uses to produce type-safe function calls without any manual ABI encoding. Breaking IDL compatibility between your deployed program and your client library causes deserialization failures that surface as confusing runtime errors rather than compile-time type errors.

children.title: Custom Errors with #[error_code]
children.description: Anchor's #[error_code] enum lets you define named error variants with human-readable messages that are embedded in the IDL and surfaced to clients as typed errors rather than raw numeric codes. Using generic error codes without custom variants makes debugging program failures extremely tedious and gives end users no actionable feedback about what went wrong.

children.title: anchor build, deploy, and test Commands
children.description: The Anchor CLI wraps Cargo compilation, program deployment to a local validator, and the Mocha test runner into three commands, creating a reproducible develop-test-deploy loop. Setting up Anchor versions inconsistently across team members causes build failures due to feature flag and BPF toolchain mismatches that are time-consuming to diagnose.

children.title: Anchor.toml and Workspace Configuration
children.description: Anchor.toml declares all programs in a workspace with their program IDs per cluster, the test script location, and provider settings, serving as the single source of truth for the entire build and deployment pipeline. Hardcoding program IDs in source files instead of reading them from Anchor.toml causes deployments to different clusters to reuse stale addresses, breaking account derivation across environments.

solana-dev.title: SPL Tokens
solana-dev.description: This layer covers the SPL token standard and how to mint and transfer tokens. Tokens are central to most Solana applications.
solana-dev.challenge: Add an Anchor instruction that mints an SPL reward token to a user each time the counter increments.

children.title: Mint Accounts and Token Supply
children.description: A mint account defines a token's metadata — decimals, total supply, and the mint authority that can create new tokens — and is distinct from any account that holds a balance of that token. Confusing the mint account with a token account leads to passing the wrong account to SPL Token instructions, causing cryptic type mismatch errors at the program level.

children.title: Associated Token Accounts
children.description: An associated token account (ATA) is a PDA derived deterministically from a wallet address and a mint, providing a canonical, predictable address for each wallet-mint pair so applications and wallets can find token balances without storing a registry. Creating non-ATA token accounts leads to fragmented balances that wallets cannot discover automatically, confusing users who see zero balance despite holding tokens.

children.title: CPI to the Token Program for Minting
children.description: Anchor programs mint tokens by invoking the SPL Token program via cross-program invocation, passing a PDA as the mint authority signer so the program can autonomously reward users without a human key. Forgetting to add the token program to the accounts context and grant PDA signing authority causes mint CPIs to fail with a missing signer error.

children.title: Burning and Closing Token Accounts
children.description: Burning tokens reduces supply by invoking the burn instruction on the token program, and closing a token account reclaims its rent-exempt lamports back to the owner. Not closing empty token accounts after burning means users accumulate dozens of zero-balance accounts that clutter their wallets and waste lamports locked as rent.

children.title: Token Metadata with Metaplex
children.description: The Metaplex Token Metadata program extends SPL tokens with on-chain name, symbol, and URI fields plus creator royalty enforcement, making it the de-facto standard for both fungible tokens and NFTs on Solana. Storing metadata only off-chain without attaching a Metadata account means wallets cannot display your token's name or logo, severely reducing user trust and discoverability.

children.title: Token-2022 and Extensions
children.description: The Token-2022 program extends the original SPL Token with optional extensions such as transfer fees, interest-bearing tokens, and non-transferable mints baked directly into the mint account. Using the legacy token program for new projects means rebuilding extension logic in your own program, increasing code surface area and audit scope unnecessarily.

children.title: Token Transfers and Approval Delegates
children.description: SPL token transfers move balances between token accounts owned by the same mint, and the approve instruction grants a third-party program a spending allowance without transferring ownership, mirroring the ERC20 pattern but at the token account level. Setting an infinite or unexpired delegate allowance on a user's token account is a security risk that allows an approved program to drain the account at any time.

children.title: Freeze Authority and Permanent Delegates
children.description: Mint accounts can optionally hold a freeze authority that can pause all transfers for a token account, and Token-2022 adds permanent delegates that retain irrevocable spending rights. Granting freeze authority to a mutable keypair instead of a secure multisig or renouncing it after launch gives a centralized party the power to freeze user funds, which destroys community trust.

solana-dev.title: Client Integration with web3.js
solana-dev.description: This layer connects frontends to Solana programs using the JavaScript SDK and Anchor client. Client integration turns programs into real applications.
solana-dev.challenge: Build a web page that connects a wallet and calls your counter program with the Anchor client.

children.title: Connection, Commitment, and RPC Endpoints
children.description: The @solana/web3.js Connection class wraps all JSON-RPC calls to a Solana node, and the commitment level — processed, confirmed, or finalized — controls how many validator votes a transaction must accumulate before the SDK considers it settled. Using processed commitment in production causes your UI to show state that later gets rolled back on a fork, producing confusing inconsistencies between the displayed balance and the actual confirmed balance.

children.title: Building and Sending Transactions
children.description: Transactions in web3.js are assembled by creating a Transaction object, adding Instructions with the correct keys and data, signing with one or more Keypairs or wallet adapters, and then sending via sendAndConfirmTransaction. Omitting required accounts from an instruction's keys array causes the on-chain program to receive fewer accounts than it expects, producing an index-out-of-bounds panic.

children.title: Using the Anchor Provider and Program
children.description: The Anchor TypeScript client wraps web3.js with a Program class that reads the IDL and exposes every instruction as a typed async method, eliminating all manual account key assembly and instruction data encoding. Skipping the Anchor client and building raw transactions against Anchor programs means re-implementing the discriminator and borsh encoding logic that the IDL already describes.

children.title: Fetching and Subscribing to Account Data
children.description: The Anchor Program class exposes program.account.AccountType.fetch(address) to decode a single account and program.account.AccountType.all() to fetch all accounts of a type, and web3.js provides onAccountChange for real-time subscriptions. Polling for account data on a fixed timer instead of using websocket subscriptions causes UI lag, overloads the RPC provider, and increases costs.

children.title: Wallet Adapter for Browser Wallets
children.description: The @solana/wallet-adapter-react library standardizes connection to Phantom, Backpack, and other browser wallets through a unified interface, so your app does not need separate integration code for each wallet. Implementing a custom wallet connection flow from scratch for each wallet creates a maintenance burden and breaks whenever a wallet updates its extension API.

children.title: Signing Transactions with a Connected Wallet
children.description: Once a wallet is connected, the WalletContextState provides a signTransaction method that prompts the user's wallet extension for approval before returning a signed transaction, keeping private keys entirely inside the wallet. Collecting user signatures server-side and submitting transactions on their behalf requires users to trust your server with a signed blank check, creating a serious custody and phishing risk.

children.title: Handling Transaction Confirmations
children.description: sendAndConfirmTransaction blocks until the transaction reaches the requested commitment level and throws if the transaction expires or is rejected, while getSignatureStatus allows manual polling with timeout logic. Ignoring the return value of sendTransaction and not confirming transactions causes your frontend to display success before the transaction has actually landed on-chain.

children.title: Parsing and Displaying Transaction Errors
children.description: Solana transaction errors carry a logs array and an InstructionError code that must be parsed to surface meaningful messages; the Anchor client's translateError helper maps numeric error codes back to IDL-defined error names. Displaying raw numeric error codes to users creates a terrible developer experience and makes it impossible to provide actionable feedback.

solana-dev.title: Testing Solana Programs
solana-dev.description: This layer establishes testing practices for Anchor programs locally. Thorough tests catch account and constraint bugs before deployment.
solana-dev.challenge: Write an Anchor test suite that covers counter increments and reward minting including failure cases.

children.title: Local Validator Testing with anchor test
children.description: anchor test starts a local Solana test validator, deploys all workspace programs, runs the Mocha test suite, and tears everything down, giving you a clean isolated environment for every test run. Testing only on devnet instead of locally means tests are slow, non-deterministic due to network congestion, and can fail simply because the devnet airdrop faucet is rate-limited.

children.title: TypeScript Integration Tests with Mocha and Chai
children.description: Anchor's generated TypeScript tests use Mocha for test structure and Chai for assertions, invoking program instructions through the typed Anchor client and asserting on fetched account state. Writing tests that call instructions without asserting on the resulting account data only checks that the transaction did not revert, missing logic bugs that corrupt state silently.

children.title: In-Program Rust Unit Tests
children.description: Solana programs support standard Rust #[cfg(test)] modules that test pure functions and business logic without invoking the full runtime, enabling fast feedback on arithmetic, serialization, and state transition logic. Skipping Rust unit tests and relying exclusively on TypeScript integration tests makes the test suite slow and means a logic bug buried in a helper function requires a full deploy cycle to discover.

children.title: solana-program-test for Full Program Testing in Rust
children.description: The solana-program-test crate provides a BanksClient that processes transactions against a simulated on-chain runtime inside a Rust test, enabling end-to-end instruction testing without a running validator. Using only TypeScript tests means you cannot catch Rust-specific bugs like integer overflow or incorrect seed derivation until they reach an integration test environment.

children.title: Testing Expected Program Errors
children.description: Anchor tests can assert that an instruction throws a specific AnchorError code by wrapping the call in try/catch and inspecting error.error.errorCode.number, verifying that your constraint and validation logic fires correctly on bad inputs. Not testing failure paths means your access control, PDA verification, and arithmetic guards might have subtle mistakes that pass for the happy path but fail or are bypassable in edge cases.

children.title: Manipulating Clock and Slot in Tests
children.description: The solana-program-test crate exposes set_sysvar to override the clock sysvar's unix_timestamp and slot, enabling time-lock and epoch-sensitive logic to be tested without waiting for real time to pass. Testing time-dependent logic without clock manipulation requires sleeping in tests or deploying to devnet, both of which make the test suite impractically slow.

children.title: Reading Program Logs in Tests
children.description: Both the TypeScript test runner and solana-program-test capture program logs emitted by msg! calls, and inspecting these logs is the fastest way to trace which branch of your program executed and what values it saw. Programs without strategic msg! checkpoints are effectively black boxes during testing, turning every failed assertion into an extended debugging session.

children.title: Compute Unit Consumption in Tests
children.description: Each transaction is limited to 1.4 million compute units by default; exceeding the limit causes an immediate transaction failure. Checking compute usage during tests with ComputeBudget or log inspection lets you identify expensive instructions before they fail in production, especially after adding new CPI calls.

solana-dev.title: Advanced Program Design
solana-dev.description: This layer covers advanced patterns such as composability, account resizing, and compute budgets. These techniques are needed for complex production programs.
solana-dev.challenge: Extend your program with a second program that it invokes via CPI to record reward history.

children.title: CPI with invoke and invoke_signed
children.description: invoke calls another Solana program within the current transaction context, inheriting the caller's account permissions, while invoke_signed additionally passes PDA seeds so the runtime can grant signature authority to program-derived addresses. Mixing up invoke and invoke_signed when the callee requires a PDA signer produces a missing required signatures error that is difficult to trace without understanding which authority the callee checks.

children.title: CpiContext in Anchor for Type-Safe CPIs
children.description: Anchor's CpiContext bundles the target program, accounts struct, and optional PDA signer seeds into one typed object, and combined with the generated CPI modules it provides a compile-time-checked interface for every instruction of every dependency program. Writing raw invoke calls inside Anchor programs bypasses all account type checking, reintroducing the manual validation burden that Anchor is designed to eliminate.

children.title: Dynamic Account Reallocation with realloc
children.description: Anchor's realloc constraint resizes an existing account's data field on-chain without closing and reopening it, enabling programs to grow account state incrementally as more data is added. Allocating maximum account size upfront wastes the user's rent-exempt lamports for data that may never be written, while failing to realloc causes index-out-of-bounds panics when new fields are appended to a struct.

children.title: Compute Unit Budgeting with ComputeBudgetProgram
children.description: The ComputeBudgetProgram instruction allows a transaction to request up to 1.4 million compute units and set a compute unit price in micro-lamports, enabling priority fee bidding for time-sensitive transactions on congested networks. Failing to request extra compute units for CPI-heavy instructions causes programs to fail mid-execution with a compute exhaustion error that is indistinguishable from a logic bug without logs.

children.title: Versioned Transactions (v0)
children.description: Versioned transactions introduced a v0 message format that supports address lookup tables, allowing a single transaction to reference more accounts than the 35-account legacy limit by compressing repeated addresses into table indexes. Sticking with legacy transactions for complex multi-protocol calls hits the account limit and requires splitting work across multiple transactions, increasing costs and introducing atomicity risk.

children.title: Address Lookup Tables for Protocol Composability
children.description: Address lookup tables store frequently used account addresses on-chain and are referenced by index in v0 transactions, shrinking transaction size and enabling complex DeFi operations that touch many accounts in a single atomic call. Not using lookup tables for protocols that repeatedly reference the same programs and token accounts wastes transaction bytes and risks hitting size limits.

children.title: Anchor Program Upgrade with anchor upgrade
children.description: anchor upgrade writes new bytecode to a buffer account, then atomically swaps it into the live program account in a single transaction, preserving all existing account state because the program address never changes. Deploying a new program ID for every release instead of upgrading orphans all user accounts and forces every client to update hardcoded program addresses.

children.title: Upgrade Authority and Multisig Governance
children.description: The upgrade authority is the only keypair allowed to deploy new bytecode to a program; transferring it to a multisig like Squads or burning it entirely makes the program immutable, trading upgradeability for trustlessness. Leaving upgrade authority on a hot wallet in production gives a compromised key holder full control over every user's program interactions, which is equivalent to a full protocol takeover.

solana-dev.title: Security and Auditing on Solana
solana-dev.description: This layer focuses on common Solana vulnerabilities and how to defend against them. Account confusion attacks are a frequent source of exploits.
solana-dev.challenge: Audit your program for missing signer and owner checks and add constraints to close the gaps.

children.title: Missing Signer and Ownership Checks
children.description: Every privileged instruction must verify that the authority account is_signer and that program-owned accounts have the expected owner program ID; skipping either check lets attackers pass arbitrary accounts to gain unauthorized access. Real Solana exploits including multi-million dollar losses have stemmed from a single missing is_signer assertion on an admin instruction.

children.title: Account Substitution and Type Confusion
children.description: Without discriminator checks an attacker can pass a different account type that satisfies only the byte-size requirement, causing the program to deserialize one struct layout over data written in another layout and execute with garbage field values. Anchor's 8-byte discriminator prefix on every initialized account prevents this by rejecting accounts whose first bytes do not match the expected type.

children.title: Integer Overflow and Safe Math
children.description: Rust in release mode does not panic on integer overflow by default; it wraps around silently, which can turn a nearly-full reward pool balance into a near-zero value and allow a user to drain tokens for pennies. Using checked_add, checked_mul, and saturating arithmetic throughout financial calculations prevents silent wrapping that can break all economic invariants of a protocol.

children.title: PDA Seed Collisions
children.description: When multiple PDAs share the same seed set or seeds are derived from user-controlled inputs without a unique namespace, different logical accounts may resolve to the same address, allowing one account to be substituted for another. Adding a fixed program-specific prefix to every seed string isolates your PDA namespace from other programs and from attacker-controlled inputs.

children.title: Reinitialization Attacks
children.description: If an instruction that initializes account state can be called again on an already-initialized account, an attacker can reset owner fields or counters to an initial state, effectively hijacking the account. Anchor prevents this with the init constraint, which fails if the account already has a non-zero discriminator, but init_if_needed requires an explicit has_one or discriminator guard.

children.title: Safe Account Closing to Prevent Revival
children.description: Closing an account by zeroing its lamports and data in the same instruction is insufficient because another instruction in the same transaction can refund lamports before the transaction completes, leaving a zombie account. Anchor's close constraint zeroes the discriminator and uses a two-step lamport drain that prevents account revival within the same transaction.

children.title: Sealevel Attacks Reference
children.description: The coral-xyz/sealevel-attacks repository enumerates every known class of Solana program vulnerability with minimal reproducible examples, making it the definitive checklist to run through before requesting a professional audit. Skipping a structured vulnerability review and relying only on functional tests means security issues discovered by a paid auditor could have been caught for free.

children.title: Invariant Testing for Financial Safety
children.description: Beyond unit tests, property-based invariant tests assert that fundamental protocol guarantees — total tokens minted equals sum of all balances, no account can withdraw more than it deposited — hold after arbitrary sequences of instructions. Without invariant tests, a combination of individually-correct instructions can compose into a drain vector that no single-instruction test would catch.

solana-dev.title: Mainnet Deployment and Operations
solana-dev.description: This final layer ships an Anchor program to mainnet and manages it over time. Production deployment requires careful upgrade authority and monitoring.
solana-dev.challenge: Deploy your full program and client to devnet and rehearse a safe program upgrade.

children.title: Buffer Accounts and Safe Deployment
children.description: Solana program deployment uploads bytecode to a temporary buffer account first and then atomically swaps it into the live program account, allowing you to prepare and verify an upgrade before it goes live. Deploying directly without inspecting the buffer means a botched binary can go live before you have a chance to abort, causing immediate production outages.

children.title: Verifiable Builds with anchor verify
children.description: anchor verify rebuilds your program in a Docker container that matches the on-chain build environment and compares the resulting bytecode hash to the deployed program, proving that the published source code corresponds exactly to what is running on mainnet. Deploying unverifiable builds means users and auditors must trust your word that the source matches, destroying the transparency that makes decentralized protocols trustworthy.

children.title: Priority Fees for Transaction Landing
children.description: Solana validators order transactions within a block by their compute unit price, so during congestion a transaction with no priority fee can be dropped repeatedly until it expires. Computing an appropriate priority fee from recent fee percentiles via getRecentPrioritizationFees and attaching a ComputeBudgetProgram instruction is essential for time-sensitive operations like arbitrage, liquidations, and NFT mints.

children.title: Choosing and Configuring RPC Providers
children.description: Public Solana RPC endpoints are rate-limited and geographically distributed unevenly; using a dedicated provider such as Helius, QuickNode, or Triton gives you higher rate limits, websocket stability, and additional methods like getParsedAccountInfo that make client development easier. Building a production application on the public endpoint means users experience dropped connections, rate limit errors, and stale data during peak network load.

children.title: Monitoring Program Logs in Production
children.description: Streaming program logs via logsSubscribe websocket or indexing transactions with a tool like Helius webhooks lets you detect anomalous instruction patterns, failed transactions, and unexpected account mutations in real time. Running a protocol with no log monitoring means you learn about exploits from social media reports rather than automated alerts, giving you no time to respond before significant damage is done.

children.title: On-Chain Program Event Parsing
children.description: Anchor emits structured events via the emit! macro that encode typed Rust structs into transaction logs, which the TypeScript client can decode with program.addEventListener, enabling real-time frontend updates and off-chain indexing without polling account state. Relying only on account polling for UI updates produces laggy interfaces and misses the granular event data needed to build analytics dashboards.

children.title: Multisig Upgrade Authority with Squads
children.description: Squads Protocol provides on-chain multisig that wraps program upgrade authority behind an M-of-N approval threshold, so no single team member can unilaterally push a malicious upgrade. Single-key upgrade authority on a mainnet program is a governance risk accepted by users who must then trust the team's key management practices rather than a transparent on-chain process.

children.title: Rehearsing Upgrades on Devnet
children.description: Running the full upgrade procedure on devnet with the same toolchain, buffer deployment, and multisig approval flow used in production catches configuration mistakes before they are executed against real user funds. Skipping devnet rehearsal means the first time the team executes a live upgrade procedure is also the highest-stakes moment, when operator error is most costly.

blockchain-core.title: Distributed Systems Foundations
blockchain-core.description: This layer establishes the distributed systems and cryptography concepts underlying every blockchain. These fundamentals explain why blockchains are structured the way they are.
blockchain-core.challenge: Implement a Merkle tree in Go and verify inclusion proofs for a set of transactions.

children.title: Hash Functions
children.description: Hash functions are the building blocks of blockchain integrity — every block header, transaction ID, and Merkle root depends on collision resistance. Misunderstanding their properties leads to broken proof-of-work or forgeable data structures.

children.title: Public Key Cryptography
children.description: Elliptic-curve key pairs authenticate every transaction without revealing private keys. Without a firm grasp of signing and verification, you cannot reason about ownership or double-spend prevention.

resources.label: An Introduction to Elliptic Curve Cryptography

children.title: Failure and Partition Tolerance
children.description: Blockchains operate across unreliable networks where nodes crash and messages are delayed. Ignoring failure modes leads to designs that appear correct on paper but diverge silently in production.

children.title: Logical Clocks and Ordering
children.description: Without a global clock, distributed nodes need logical timestamps to order events. Mishandling event ordering causes state divergence and breaks replay determinism.

children.title: Merkle Tree Construction
children.description: Merkle trees allow efficient and tamper-evident verification of large datasets. Blockchains use them for transaction inclusion proofs; an incorrect implementation invalidates every audit trail.

children.title: Inclusion Proofs
children.description: Inclusion proofs let light clients verify a transaction exists in a block without downloading the whole chain. Misimplementing proofs breaks SPV security and enables fraudulent payment confirmations.

children.title: Go Concurrency Primitives
children.description: Goroutines and channels are essential for handling concurrent block validation and network I/O. Misusing them introduces race conditions that are notoriously hard to reproduce in blockchain nodes.

children.title: Go Interfaces and Testing
children.description: Interfaces allow pluggable consensus and storage backends, which is how production nodes stay modular. Weak test coverage at this layer makes later consensus bugs nearly impossible to isolate.

blockchain-core.title: Building a Minimal Blockchain
blockchain-core.description: This layer builds a simple blockchain data structure from scratch. Constructing blocks and chains by hand clarifies how state is linked and validated.
blockchain-core.challenge: Write a Go program that mines blocks with a simple proof of work and validates the chain.

children.title: Block Headers and Chaining
children.description: Each block header contains the hash of the previous block, creating an immutable chain. Any misunderstanding of this linkage leads to invalid chain construction and failed validation.

children.title: Genesis Block Design
children.description: The genesis block is the root of trust for every chain — its parameters determine initial supply, difficulty, and validator set. A misconfigured genesis cannot be patched without a hard fork.

children.title: Transaction Serialization
children.description: Consistent binary encoding of transactions is required for deterministic hashing and signature verification. Inconsistent serialization is a common source of consensus splits between node versions.

children.title: Validation Rules
children.description: Each node independently validates blocks and transactions against a canonical rule set. Diverging validation logic between nodes causes chain splits that are difficult to diagnose after the fact.

children.title: Mining and Difficulty Adjustment
children.description: Proof of work ties block production to real computational cost, making history rewriting expensive. Misunderstanding difficulty adjustment leads to unstable block times and miner incentive failures.

children.title: Nonce Search and Block Finalization
children.description: Finding a valid nonce that satisfies the difficulty target is the core loop of PoW mining. A faulty nonce search loop produces invalid blocks that peers immediately reject.

children.title: Fork Detection
children.description: Two valid blocks at the same height create a temporary fork that nodes must resolve deterministically. Without proper fork detection, nodes accumulate orphan blocks and waste resources.

children.title: Chain Selection Rules
children.description: Longest-chain or heaviest-chain rules determine which fork becomes canonical. Inconsistent chain selection causes nodes to follow different histories, splitting the network.

blockchain-core.title: Rust for Systems Programming
blockchain-core.description: This layer introduces Rust as a second core language for safe, performant node software. Many modern chains are written in Rust for its memory safety guarantees.
blockchain-core.challenge: Reimplement your block validator in Rust using async tasks for parallel verification.

children.title: Ownership and Borrowing
children.description: Rust's ownership system eliminates data races and use-after-free bugs at compile time, both critical in concurrent node software. Misunderstanding borrow rules causes cascading refactors when integrating networking and storage layers.

children.title: Lifetimes
children.description: Lifetimes let the compiler verify that references never outlive the data they point to. Ignoring lifetimes leads to subtle memory safety issues that can corrupt node state under high load.

children.title: Async/Await and the Tokio Runtime
children.description: Tokio provides an async runtime for thousands of concurrent network connections without blocking threads. Using synchronous code in hot paths stalls the entire event loop and degrades peer communication.

children.title: Tokio Channels and Tasks
children.description: Tokio's mpsc and broadcast channels coordinate work between async tasks safely. Misusing channels introduces deadlocks or message loss that can halt block propagation silently.

children.title: Traits for Pluggable Interfaces
children.description: Traits define shared behavior that allows swapping consensus or storage backends without rewriting core logic. Without trait abstractions, node components become tightly coupled and untestable.

children.title: Error Handling with Result
children.description: Propagating errors explicitly via Result prevents panics from crashing a live node on unexpected input. Silent error swallowing causes nodes to produce invalid state without any observable failure.

children.title: Cargo and Dependency Management
children.description: Cargo manages dependencies, build configurations, and feature flags for all major blockchain crates. Improper dependency pinning leads to subtle API breaks between node versions across a validator set.

children.title: Serialization with serde
children.description: serde provides zero-overhead serialization for block and transaction types across JSON, bincode, and RLP formats. Misconfigured derive macros produce silently wrong wire encodings that break interoperability.

blockchain-core.title: Peer-to-Peer Networking
blockchain-core.description: This layer covers how nodes discover and communicate over a peer-to-peer network. Networking is the backbone that propagates blocks and transactions.
blockchain-core.challenge: Build a small libp2p network that gossips new blocks between several local nodes.

children.title: Kademlia DHT
children.description: Kademlia is the distributed hash table protocol used by libp2p and Ethereum to discover peers without a central registry. A faulty discovery layer starves nodes of peers and causes them to fall behind the canonical chain.

children.title: Bootstrap Nodes and Seeding
children.description: Bootstrap nodes provide initial peer addresses to newly joining nodes; without them, a node cannot enter the network. Hardcoding stale bootstrap addresses is a common reason testnets fail to form.

children.title: Epidemic Broadcast
children.description: Gossip protocols propagate blocks and transactions across the network in O(log n) rounds without central coordination. A node that gossips too aggressively wastes bandwidth; one that is too conservative slows propagation and increases uncle rates.

children.title: Message Deduplication
children.description: Nodes must deduplicate gossip messages to prevent exponential amplification on large networks. Missing deduplication causes bandwidth storms that crash nodes under normal transaction volumes.

children.title: Transport and Multiplexing
children.description: libp2p abstracts TCP, QUIC, and WebSocket transports behind a unified interface and multiplexes streams over a single connection. Choosing the wrong transport or misconfiguring multiplexing causes connection resets under load.

children.title: Noise Protocol for Security
children.description: The Noise protocol provides authenticated encryption for all libp2p connections, preventing MITM attacks between peers. Skipping channel encryption exposes all gossip traffic and allows an attacker to inject forged blocks.

children.title: Hole Punching
children.description: Most nodes sit behind NAT; hole punching allows two NATted peers to establish a direct connection via a relay. Without it, a significant fraction of the network is unreachable and topology degrades to hub-and-spoke.

children.title: Circuit Relay
children.description: Circuit relays forward traffic for peers that cannot be directly reached, ensuring network connectivity as a fallback. Relying solely on relays introduces a single point of failure and a bandwidth bottleneck.

blockchain-core.title: Consensus Fundamentals
blockchain-core.description: This layer explains the consensus problem and classic algorithms for agreement. Consensus is what lets distrusting nodes agree on one history.
blockchain-core.challenge: Add a longest-chain fork choice rule to your network so nodes converge on one chain.

children.title: The Consensus Problem
children.description: Consensus requires all honest nodes to agree on the same value despite crashes and message delays. Confusing safety (nothing bad) with liveness (something good eventually) leads to protocols that halt under common network conditions.

children.title: Fork Choice Rules
children.description: Fork choice rules deterministically select the canonical chain when multiple valid branches exist. An ambiguous or non-deterministic fork choice can split validators across two competing chains indefinitely.

children.title: Nakamoto Consensus
children.description: Nakamoto consensus combines PoW with the longest-chain rule to achieve probabilistic finality without trusted coordinators. Misunderstanding probabilistic finality leads to accepting unconfirmed transactions that are later reversed.

children.title: 51% Attack Model
children.description: A miner controlling the majority of hash power can reorg the chain and double-spend. Understanding the cost model for attacks is essential when setting confirmation thresholds for high-value transactions.

children.title: Validator Sets and Staking
children.description: In PoS, validators lock up collateral to earn the right to propose and attest to blocks. Allowing validators with no stake reduces the cost of attacks; poor slashing design lets misbehavior go unpunished.

children.title: Finality in PoS
children.description: PoS chains achieve economic finality when reverting a block would require slashing a supermajority of the stake. Without finality gadgets, long-range attacks allow attackers to rewrite history from any checkpoint.

children.title: Probabilistic vs Economic Finality
children.description: PoW offers probabilistic finality that deepens with confirmations; BFT PoS offers economic finality after a single round. Choosing the wrong model for an application's trust assumptions is a fundamental design error.

children.title: Checkpointing
children.description: Checkpoints commit finalized state periodically so nodes can sync from a known-good point without replaying the full history. Missing or forged checkpoints allow sync attacks where new nodes are given a different history.

blockchain-core.title: Byzantine Fault Tolerant Consensus
blockchain-core.description: This layer studies BFT consensus protocols that deliver instant finality. BFT algorithms power many proof of stake chains today.
blockchain-core.challenge: Implement a simplified two-phase BFT round among your nodes that finalizes a block on a quorum.

children.title: PBFT Three-Phase Protocol
children.description: PBFT achieves Byzantine fault tolerance through pre-prepare, prepare, and commit phases requiring 2f+1 votes for safety. Skipping any phase breaks safety guarantees and allows Byzantine nodes to fork the committee.

children.title: View Changes
children.description: View changes replace a faulty primary leader without halting the protocol. A broken view change allows a Byzantine leader to stall the chain by withholding proposals indefinitely.

children.title: Tendermint Round Structure
children.description: Tendermint uses propose, prevote, and precommit steps with a rotating proposer and 2/3 majority thresholds. Miscounting votes or mishandling nil votes causes liveness failures under a Byzantine minority.

children.title: Quorum Certificates
children.description: A quorum certificate (QC) aggregates 2/3 of validator votes into a single verifiable proof of consensus. Without QC aggregation, vote messages scale quadratically with validator count and flood the network.

children.title: Voting Power and Stake Weighting
children.description: Voting power proportional to stake ensures the cost of a 33% attack equals one-third of total stake value. Flat or miscalculated voting power breaks the economic security model entirely.

children.title: Slashing Conditions
children.description: Slashing penalizes validators for equivocation (double signing) and downtime, aligning incentives with honest behavior. Overly aggressive slashing drives validators offline; lenient slashing makes attacks cost-free.

children.title: Cosmos SDK and CometBFT
children.description: Cosmos SDK provides a production BFT stack (CometBFT) with pluggable application logic via ABCI. Understanding how ABCI separates consensus from execution prevents race conditions in state machine updates.

children.title: Substrate BABE and GRANDPA
children.description: Substrate separates block production (BABE) from finality (GRANDPA), giving each concern its own protocol. Confusing the two layers leads to forks where blocks are produced but never finalized.

blockchain-core.title: State and Storage Engines
blockchain-core.description: This layer covers how chains persist state efficiently using tries and key-value stores. Storage design directly affects sync speed and node hardware needs.
blockchain-core.challenge: Back your node's state with an embedded key-value store and add a state snapshot feature.

children.title: UTXO Model
children.description: The UTXO model represents balance as a set of unspent outputs that are consumed and created atomically. Misunderstanding UTXO selection leads to unspendable dust outputs and incorrect fee estimation.

children.title: Account Model
children.description: The account model tracks balance and nonce directly per address, simplifying smart contract state management. Replay attacks are possible if nonces are not strictly enforced, allowing the same transaction to execute twice.

children.title: Trie Structure and State Root
children.description: Ethereum stores all account state in a Merkle Patricia Trie whose root hash is committed in each block header. A bug in trie updates causes state root mismatches that make the node permanently invalid to its peers.

children.title: State Proofs
children.description: State proofs allow light clients and bridges to verify account balances against a committed root without a full node. Weak proof verification logic enables fraudulent state assertions that drain cross-chain bridges.

children.title: LevelDB and RocksDB
children.description: LevelDB and RocksDB provide the embedded key-value stores that back most blockchain state databases. Choosing an inappropriate compaction strategy causes write amplification that saturates I/O during chain sync.

children.title: Write-Ahead Logging
children.description: WAL ensures that storage operations survive crashes by logging changes before applying them. Disabling WAL for performance gains risks corrupted state after an unexpected node restart.

children.title: Historical State Pruning
children.description: Pruning removes historical trie nodes that are no longer needed for current state, keeping disk usage manageable. Aggressive pruning breaks archive nodes and prevents proving historical state for light clients.

children.title: Snapshots for Fast Sync
children.description: State snapshots capture a complete world state at a block height, allowing new nodes to sync in hours rather than weeks. A snapshot without integrity verification is a trust vector that can give new nodes fraudulent state.

blockchain-core.title: Transaction Pool and Execution
blockchain-core.description: This layer builds the mempool and the execution pipeline that applies transactions to state. Correct ordering and execution are vital for deterministic results.
blockchain-core.challenge: Add a fee-prioritized mempool and a block producer that executes transactions deterministically.

children.title: Fee Prioritization
children.description: The mempool ranks pending transactions by fee rate so block producers include the most profitable ones first. Incorrect priority queues result in low-fee spam filling blocks and high-fee transactions being stuck.

children.title: Nonce and Replay Protection
children.description: Monotonically increasing nonces prevent the same transaction from being included twice. A mempool that accepts out-of-order nonces allows attackers to replay transactions and drain accounts.

children.title: Transaction Selection and Ordering
children.description: Block producers select and order transactions, directly affecting user experience and MEV extraction. Non-deterministic ordering allows validators to manipulate transaction sequence for profit at users' expense.

children.title: Deterministic Execution
children.description: All nodes must apply the same transactions in the same order and reach identical state. Any source of non-determinism — floating-point math, uninitialized memory, or random ordering — causes consensus failures.

children.title: Gas Metering
children.description: Gas assigns a cost to every EVM opcode, bounding computation per block and preventing DoS through infinite loops. An incorrectly priced opcode can be abused to make blocks extremely expensive to validate.

children.title: EIP-1559 Fee Market
children.description: EIP-1559 introduced a base fee that adjusts per block, reducing fee volatility for users. Misimplementing the base fee update rule causes unpredictable fee spikes that break user wallets.

children.title: EVM Architecture
children.description: The EVM is a stack-based virtual machine that executes smart contract bytecode deterministically across all nodes. A divergence in EVM semantics between client implementations causes silent consensus splits.

children.title: WASM as Execution Environment
children.description: WebAssembly runtimes like those in Substrate offer portable, near-native execution for smart contracts. An unsafe WASM host function can break isolation and allow contracts to corrupt node state.

blockchain-core.title: Security and Resilience
blockchain-core.description: This layer hardens the node against attacks on consensus and networking. Resilience is what keeps a live network safe under adversarial conditions.
blockchain-core.challenge: Add peer rate limiting and equivocation detection, then stress test your network against a misbehaving node.

children.title: Eclipse Attacks
children.description: An eclipse attack monopolizes all of a node's peer connections, isolating it from the honest network. An eclipsed node accepts a forked chain and may confirm double-spend transactions as final.

children.title: Sybil Attacks
children.description: A Sybil attack floods the peer-to-peer network with fake identities to manipulate routing tables or gossip. Without identity cost or staking, Sybil nodes can censor transactions or eclipse specific targets.

children.title: Long-Range Attacks
children.description: Long-range attacks exploit the cheap history rewrite possible when old validator keys are compromised in PoS. Weak subjectivity checkpoints are the primary defense; ignoring them makes the chain vulnerable to history revision.

children.title: Equivocation Detection
children.description: Equivocation is signing two conflicting messages at the same height, which is the primary Byzantine misbehavior in BFT chains. Without automated detection and slashing, a Byzantine validator can fork the committee without penalty.

children.title: Peer Rate Limiting
children.description: Rate limiting caps the message volume accepted from individual peers, preventing bandwidth exhaustion from a single bad actor. Without rate limits, one malicious peer can saturate the node's I/O and halt block processing.

children.title: Transaction Validation at Ingress
children.description: Rejecting syntactically invalid transactions before they enter the mempool prevents cheap DoS through malformed inputs. Deferring validation to execution means a single bad transaction can exhaust worker threads.

children.title: Fuzzing Node Inputs
children.description: Fuzzing generates malformed blocks, transactions, and peer messages to find crash bugs before deployment. Untested edge cases in deserialization are the most common source of remote crashes in production nodes.

children.title: Network Chaos Testing
children.description: Chaos testing introduces latency, packet loss, and Byzantine peers to validate that the node handles adversarial conditions gracefully. Skipping chaos tests means the first production incident reveals unknown failure modes.

blockchain-core.title: Testnet Launch and Operations
blockchain-core.description: This final layer launches a multi-node testnet and operates it like a real network. Running a live testnet validates the entire stack end to end.
blockchain-core.challenge: Launch a multi-validator testnet of your node, monitor it with metrics, and perform a coordinated upgrade.

children.title: Genesis File Design
children.description: The genesis file encodes the initial validator set, token supply, and chain parameters that every node must agree on before producing a single block. A genesis mismatch between validators prevents the network from ever starting.

children.title: Validator Onboarding
children.description: Validator onboarding defines how new nodes join the active set with staked collateral. An open onboarding process without stake requirements allows Sybil validators to flood and destabilize the testnet.

children.title: On-Chain Governance and Upgrades
children.description: Coordinated upgrades require all validators to switch to new software at the same block height. An uncoordinated upgrade splits the validator set across two incompatible versions, causing a chain halt.

children.title: Hard Forks vs Soft Forks
children.description: Hard forks require all nodes to upgrade simultaneously; soft forks are backward-compatible and allow gradual rollout. Misclassifying a breaking change as a soft fork causes old nodes to follow an invalid chain silently.

children.title: Metrics and Telemetry
children.description: Exposing Prometheus metrics for block time, peer count, mempool size, and consensus rounds is essential for diagnosing liveness issues. A node without metrics is a black box; operators cannot distinguish a stuck consensus from normal finality latency.

children.title: Structured Logging
children.description: Structured logs with consistent fields allow operators to correlate events across multiple nodes during an incident. Unstructured logs make post-mortem analysis of consensus failures extremely time-consuming.

children.title: Chain Halt Recovery
children.description: A chain halt occurs when no block is produced for an extended period, usually caused by a validator supermajority going offline. Having a documented recovery runbook prevents chaotic manual interventions that can cause permanent state loss.

children.title: Emergency Governance
children.description: Emergency governance mechanisms allow validators to push critical patches without waiting for normal proposal timelines. Without them, a critical security vulnerability can persist for days while governance processes run their course.

blockchain-security.title: Smart Contract Security Mindset
blockchain-security.description: This layer introduces the adversarial mindset needed to review smart contracts. Thinking like an attacker is the core skill of every auditor.
blockchain-security.challenge: Review a small intentionally vulnerable contract and write up every issue you can find.

children.title: Threat Modeling for Smart Contracts
children.description: Threat modeling forces you to enumerate every actor, asset, and trust boundary before a single line of code is read. Without it, auditors miss whole categories of risk and produce incomplete reviews.

children.title: Severity Classification
children.description: Classifying findings by severity and likelihood determines which bugs get fixed first and how a protocol prioritizes remediation. Incorrect classification misleads teams and can leave critical issues unaddressed.

children.title: Trust Assumptions
children.description: Every contract rests on assumptions about who can call what and when. Failing to document these assumptions makes it impossible to identify when they are violated by an attacker.

children.title: Reading Code Critically
children.description: Critical reading means questioning every assumption in a codebase rather than accepting that it works as written. Auditors who read too charitably overlook the subtle logic errors that lead to exploits.

children.title: Common Vulnerability Classes
children.description: A shared vocabulary of vulnerability classes lets auditors communicate findings precisely and match patterns against known exploits. Gaps in this knowledge cause real bugs to be filed under the wrong category or missed entirely.

children.title: Security Mindset vs. Development Mindset
children.description: Developers optimize for features while auditors optimize for failure modes — these mindsets are fundamentally different. Switching into adversarial mode is a learned skill that makes the difference between a superficial review and a thorough one.

children.title: Scoping an Engagement
children.description: Defining scope clearly prevents wasted effort on out-of-scope code and sets expectations for both auditor and client. Poor scoping leads to incomplete reports and disputes over whether a bug was covered.

children.title: Choosing the Right Tools
children.description: Combining manual review with static analysis and fuzzing creates layered coverage that no single technique achieves alone. Relying on one method leaves entire classes of bugs undetected.

blockchain-security.title: Solidity Internals for Auditors
blockchain-security.description: This layer deepens Solidity and EVM knowledge from a security angle. Many bugs hide in low-level behavior that auditors must understand.
blockchain-security.challenge: Trace how a delegatecall changes storage context in a proxy and document the risk.

children.title: Storage Layout
children.description: The EVM packs state variables into 32-byte slots in a deterministic but non-obvious way. Misunderstanding layout leads to storage collision bugs in proxy contracts, which have caused multi-million dollar losses.

children.title: Delegatecall and Context
children.description: Delegatecall executes foreign code in the caller's storage context, making it a powerful but dangerous primitive. Confused context bugs in proxy patterns regularly result in ownership takeovers and fund drains.

children.title: Function Selectors and Calldata
children.description: The first four bytes of calldata route every external call, and selector collisions in proxy contracts silently redirect execution to unintended functions. Auditors must verify selector uniqueness across implementation and proxy ABIs.

children.title: Integer Behavior and Overflow
children.description: Solidity 0.8+ adds automatic overflow checks, but older code and unchecked blocks still allow arithmetic wrapping. A missed overflow in a balance calculation is enough to mint or drain arbitrary tokens.

children.title: Visibility Pitfalls
children.description: Default visibility in early Solidity was public, and even today developers misconfigure internal functions that should be private. A single incorrectly visible function can bypass all authorization logic.

children.title: Low-Level Calls and Return Values
children.description: Raw call, send, and transfer behave differently around return values and gas stipends, and ignoring the boolean return from a low-level call swallows failures silently. This pattern enables denial-of-service and fund-locking bugs.

children.title: Assembly Auditing
children.description: Inline assembly bypasses Solidity's safety guarantees entirely, making audits of Yul blocks require opcode-level reasoning. Missing a single mstore offset error in assembly can corrupt adjacent storage slots.

children.title: Cyfrin Updraft EVM Internals
children.description: A structured deep-dive into the EVM execution model accelerates the pattern recognition that underlies every manual audit. Auditors who skip this layer spend far more time on each engagement tracing basic behaviors.

blockchain-security.title: Common Vulnerability Patterns
blockchain-security.description: This layer catalogs the most frequent exploit patterns with concrete examples. Recognizing these patterns quickly is what makes reviews efficient.
blockchain-security.challenge: Exploit a reentrancy bug in a sample vault on a local fork and then patch it.

children.title: Reentrancy Attacks
children.description: Reentrancy lets a malicious contract call back into the victim before state is updated, allowing repeated withdrawals from a single balance entry. The original DAO hack drained $60 million using this exact pattern and it remains a top finding today.

children.title: Checks-Effects-Interactions Pattern
children.description: CEI mandates that state updates happen before any external call, eliminating the window that reentrancy exploits. Deviating from CEI even once in a contract is enough to introduce the vulnerability regardless of other mitigations.

children.title: Missing Access Control
children.description: Functions that modify critical state without authorization checks allow any caller to act as an admin. Access control bugs are consistently among the top three causes of DeFi losses by dollar value.

children.title: tx.origin Authentication
children.description: Using tx.origin instead of msg.sender for authentication allows phishing contracts to impersonate users by being called in the same transaction chain. Any contract that checks tx.origin is vulnerable to a simple forwarding attack.

children.title: Front-Running Vulnerabilities
children.description: Mempool visibility lets searchers observe pending transactions and insert their own with higher gas to execute first, stealing arbitrage or manipulating state. DEX trades, NFT mints, and oracle updates are all common targets for front-running.

children.title: Denial of Service Patterns
children.description: DoS bugs prevent legitimate users from calling functions, often by exploiting gas limits or forcing a revert inside a loop. A contract that lets an attacker brick core functionality can permanently lock user funds.

children.title: Oracle Manipulation
children.description: On-chain price oracles derived from spot DEX reserves can be moved by a large trade in the same block, enabling attackers to borrow against inflated collateral. Protocols that use manipulable oracles for critical calculations are prime targets for flash loan attacks.

children.title: Unchecked External Calls
children.description: An unchecked return value from a low-level call allows silent failure to pass unnoticed, so a contract may believe an action succeeded when it did not. This leads to state inconsistency and potential fund loss.

blockchain-security.title: Static Analysis with Slither
blockchain-security.description: This layer automates vulnerability detection using the Slither static analyzer. Tooling lets auditors cover more ground and catch obvious issues fast.
blockchain-security.challenge: Run Slither on a mid-sized protocol and triage every finding into real or false positive.

children.title: Slither Detectors
children.description: Slither ships over 90 built-in detectors that flag reentrancy, unsafe arithmetic, dangerous delegatecall usage, and more in seconds. Skipping this step means finding manually what automation could have surfaced in a single command.

children.title: Filtering False Positives
children.description: Slither produces false positives, and triaging them correctly separates real risks from noise. Over-reporting false positives wastes client time and erodes trust in the tooling-assisted review.

children.title: Integrating Slither into CI
children.description: Running Slither on every pull request creates a safety net that catches regressions before they reach mainnet. Teams that skip CI integration often discover that a known vulnerability pattern was re-introduced weeks after being fixed.

children.title: Inheritance and Call Graphs
children.description: Slither generates call and inheritance graphs that reveal which functions are reachable from external entry points. Complex inheritance chains hide unexpected override behaviors that manual reading frequently misses.

children.title: Writing Custom Slither Detectors
children.description: Custom detectors let auditors encode project-specific invariants and search the entire codebase for their violation automatically. Without this capability, protocol-specific anti-patterns must be hunted manually on every engagement.

children.title: Mythril Symbolic Analysis
children.description: Mythril complements Slither by symbolically executing the bytecode rather than analyzing source, catching bugs that manifest only at the EVM level. Using both tools together improves coverage significantly over either alone.

children.title: Static Analysis Limitations
children.description: Static analysis cannot reason about runtime values or economic incentives, so it misses whole classes of logic bugs and economic exploits. Understanding these limits prevents auditors from treating a clean Slither run as a clean bill of health.

children.title: Combining Static and Dynamic Analysis
children.description: Layering static analysis with fuzzing and manual review creates defense-in-depth that catches different bug classes at each layer. Teams relying on a single technique consistently miss the vulnerabilities that the other techniques would catch.

blockchain-security.title: Fuzzing with Echidna
blockchain-security.description: This layer uses property-based fuzzing to find edge cases automatically. Fuzzers discover bugs that manual review and unit tests miss.
blockchain-security.challenge: Write invariants for the sample vault and run Echidna until it breaks an assumption.

children.title: Writing Invariants
children.description: Invariants express properties that must hold for every possible sequence of transactions, forcing you to articulate what correct behavior actually means. Without written invariants, a fuzzer has no oracle and cannot detect correctness violations, only crashes.

children.title: Configuring Echidna
children.description: Echidna's configuration controls campaign length, corpus seeding, and assertion mode, each of which dramatically affects what bugs get found. A default configuration on a complex DeFi protocol explores only a tiny fraction of the reachable state space.

children.title: Foundry Invariant Tests
children.description: Foundry's built-in invariant testing integrates fuzzing directly into the existing test suite, lowering the barrier to entry for teams already using Forge. Protocols without invariant tests regularly ship with accounting bugs that a 10-line property would have caught.

children.title: Corpus and Coverage
children.description: The fuzzer corpus accumulates interesting inputs over time, and coverage metrics show which code paths have been exercised. Auditors who ignore corpus quality may run a long campaign that repeatedly hits the same narrow slice of behavior.

children.title: Minimizing Counterexamples
children.description: When Echidna breaks an invariant it produces a transaction sequence that can be long and noisy; shrinking it to the minimal reproducer is essential for diagnosing the root cause. Unminimized counterexamples waste hours of investigation time.

children.title: Fuzzing vs. Unit Tests
children.description: Unit tests check specific known scenarios while fuzzers explore the unknown, making them complementary rather than competing. A test suite consisting only of unit tests will never break invariants it was not written to test.

children.title: When to Stop Fuzzing
children.description: No fuzzing campaign can prove the absence of bugs, so auditors must decide when coverage and campaign length are sufficient for their risk tolerance. Running a fuzzer for too short a time provides false assurance; running it indefinitely is economically impractical.

children.title: Stateful vs. Stateless Fuzzing
children.description: Stateless fuzzers test individual functions in isolation while stateful campaigns chain multiple calls to reach deep states. Many DeFi bugs only manifest after a specific sequence of operations, making stateful fuzzing essential.

blockchain-security.title: Manual Review Methodology
blockchain-security.description: This layer formalizes a repeatable manual review process. A systematic methodology produces consistent and defensible audit results.
blockchain-security.challenge: Perform a structured manual review of a DeFi contract and produce findings with proofs of concept.

children.title: Reading Specs and Invariants
children.description: The specification is the ground truth against which code should be measured, and deviations between spec and implementation are findings regardless of whether code compiles. Auditors who skip spec review miss the entire class of logic bugs where code does something technically valid but wrong.

children.title: Note Taking and Finding Tracking
children.description: A disciplined note-taking system ensures no finding is lost between discovery and the final report and makes re-review efficient. Auditors without a tracking system routinely rediscover the same issues or forget promising leads mid-review.

children.title: Cross-Function and Cross-Contract Analysis
children.description: Vulnerabilities often span multiple functions or contracts, requiring the auditor to trace state changes across the entire call graph. Narrow function-by-function reading misses bugs that only emerge when two functions interact unexpectedly.

children.title: Proof of Concept Writing
children.description: A working PoC that reproduces the bug on a mainnet fork transforms a theoretical finding into indisputable evidence and accelerates triage. Findings without PoCs are frequently downgraded or disputed by development teams.

children.title: Severity and Likelihood Scoring
children.description: A finding's severity combines the maximum possible impact with the realistic probability of exploitation. Misjudging severity misleads developers about priorities and can leave critical issues unpatched while low-impact issues consume engineering time.

children.title: Recommended Fixes
children.description: Every finding should include a concrete recommended fix so that developers do not have to guess how to remediate. Vague recommendations without code snippets or pattern guidance are difficult to implement correctly under time pressure.

children.title: Automated Checklist Integration
children.description: Systematic checklists ensure every known vulnerability class is considered on every engagement, preventing the human tendency to overlook familiar territory. A checklist-less review is only as good as the auditor's memory on that particular day.

children.title: Tooling Stack for Reviews
children.description: The combination of VS Code with Solidity extensions, Foundry, Slither, and a local fork creates an environment where auditors can read, run, and exploit code within minutes. A slow or incomplete tooling setup burns a significant portion of a time-boxed audit.

blockchain-security.title: DeFi-Specific Attack Vectors
blockchain-security.description: This layer focuses on attacks unique to decentralized finance protocols. DeFi handles large value and presents complex economic risks.
blockchain-security.challenge: Build a flash loan proof of concept that manipulates a vulnerable price source and drains a pool.

children.title: Flash Loan Mechanics
children.description: Flash loans allow borrowing unlimited liquidity within a single transaction with no collateral, making them the primary capital source for DeFi exploits. Any protocol that does not account for single-block balance changes is vulnerable to flash-loan-amplified attacks.
