children.title: Price Oracle Manipulation
children.description: AMM spot prices can be moved within one block using flash loans, allowing attackers to borrow against inflated collateral or liquidate healthy positions. Protocols using TWAP oracles or Chainlink feeds are significantly more resistant to this class of attack.

children.title: Governance Attack Vectors
children.description: Governance systems that allow token borrowing for voting enable attackers to acquire quorum temporarily and pass malicious proposals. A flash-loaned governance attack can drain a protocol treasury in a single transaction if quorum thresholds are set incorrectly.

children.title: Reward Accounting Bugs
children.description: Staking and yield protocols must track per-user reward snapshots precisely, as rounding errors and race conditions compound over millions of users. A one-wei rounding error in reward distribution can be exploited at scale to drain the reward pool.

children.title: Slippage and Sandwich Attacks
children.description: Sandwich attacks front-run a user's swap to move the price and back-run it to capture the arbitrage profit, extracting value from every large trade with loose slippage settings. DeFi protocols that omit minimum output checks enable extraction from every user.

children.title: Liquidation Edge Cases
children.description: Lending protocols must handle scenarios where collateral prices move faster than liquidators can act, and edge cases in the liquidation math can leave the protocol insolvent. Insufficient testing of liquidation boundaries is a common audit finding in money market protocols.

children.title: Learning from DeFi Post-Mortems
children.description: Reading detailed post-mortems of real DeFi exploits trains the pattern recognition that makes auditors effective on novel protocols. Ignoring this history means rediscovering attack patterns that the community has already catalogued.

children.title: Economic Security Design
children.description: DeFi protocols are economic systems and must be analyzed as such, considering incentive alignment, adversarial actors, and game-theoretic stability. Bugs in tokenomics and incentive design can be as damaging as code-level vulnerabilities.

blockchain-security.title: Formal Verification and Symbolic Execution
blockchain-security.description: This layer applies symbolic execution and formal methods to prove properties. These techniques give the strongest assurances about contract correctness.
blockchain-security.challenge: Use a symbolic tool to formally prove a key invariant of the sample vault holds for all inputs.

children.title: Symbolic Execution Basics
children.description: Symbolic execution explores all possible execution paths simultaneously by treating inputs as mathematical symbols rather than concrete values. It can prove the absence of certain bug classes rather than just finding instances, providing a level of assurance fuzzing cannot match.

children.title: Using Halmos
children.description: Halmos applies bounded symbolic execution to Solidity tests, allowing formal proofs to be written in the same framework as regular unit tests. Protocols that achieve Halmos-verified invariants have a measurably higher security baseline than those relying solely on dynamic testing.

children.title: Writing Formal Specifications
children.description: A formal specification mathematically defines what a contract must do and is machine-checkable, unlike prose documentation. Writing one forces protocol designers to confront ambiguities before they become bugs in code.

children.title: Bounded Model Checking
children.description: Bounded model checking verifies properties hold for all executions up to a specified number of steps, catching bugs that manifest in short interaction sequences. It is far more exhaustive than unit tests but faster than full formal proofs.

children.title: Fuzzing vs. Formal Proofs
children.description: Fuzzing is probabilistic and fast while formal verification is exhaustive within stated bounds but expensive to set up. Choosing the wrong technique for the risk level leads to either over-spending on low-value contracts or under-investing in high-value ones.

children.title: Limits of Formal Verification
children.description: Formal verification only covers what the specification captures, so an incorrect spec produces a false sense of correctness. Economic and game-theoretic risks are rarely captured by formal models, meaning verification and adversarial economic analysis must both be applied.

children.title: Certora Prover
children.description: The Certora Prover is a commercial formal verification tool used by leading DeFi protocols to verify critical invariants across entire contract systems. Its rule language (CVL) is purpose-built for Solidity and covers property classes that open-source tools do not.

children.title: SMT Solvers in Security
children.description: Tools like Z3 underpin most symbolic execution engines and can be queried directly to check arithmetic constraints that code-level tools struggle with. Understanding SMT basics helps auditors tune tools and interpret counterexamples accurately.

blockchain-security.title: Reporting and Disclosure
blockchain-security.description: This layer covers writing clear audit reports and handling responsible disclosure. Communication is as important as finding the bug itself.
blockchain-security.challenge: Compile your vault findings into a professional audit report with severities and remediation advice.

children.title: Report Structure and Clarity
children.description: A well-structured audit report makes findings actionable by presenting context, impact, proof of concept, and remediation in a consistent format. Poor report structure causes development teams to misunderstand severity or skip remediation entirely.

children.title: Risk Ratings
children.description: Standardized risk ratings (Critical, High, Medium, Low, Informational) create a common language between auditor and client and set triage priorities. Inconsistent or undefined rating criteria confuse stakeholders and result in critical bugs receiving insufficient urgency.

children.title: Responsible Disclosure Process
children.description: Responsible disclosure gives the protocol time to patch before vulnerability details become public, protecting users and maintaining the auditor's professional reputation. Premature or incomplete disclosure has led to exploits of protocols that were mid-remediation.

children.title: Bug Bounty Workflows
children.description: Bug bounty programs formalize the relationship between white-hat researchers and protocols, defining scope, payout tiers, and disclosure timelines. Researchers who bypass the bounty process or disclose without a response often forfeit payment and damage their reputation.

children.title: Writing Clear Findings
children.description: Each finding must describe the root cause, the attack scenario, and the impact in plain language that both developers and non-technical stakeholders can understand. Jargon-heavy or ambiguous findings are routinely marked as won't-fix due to unclear impact.

children.title: Recommended Mitigations
children.description: Every finding should conclude with one or more concrete remediation options, ideally with code, so the development team can act immediately. Findings without actionable mitigations require additional back-and-forth that delays the fix and extends risk exposure.

children.title: Remediation Verification
children.description: A follow-up review confirms that fixes actually address the root cause and do not introduce new vulnerabilities. Patches that are not re-reviewed have introduced critical regressions in multiple well-known protocols.

children.title: Public Audit Reports as Reference
children.description: Published audit reports from leading firms are the best training data for understanding how professional findings are structured and what issues matter. Aspiring auditors who skip this corpus reinvent methodology from scratch rather than building on the field's accumulated knowledge.

blockchain-security.title: Competitive Auditing and Continuous Monitoring
blockchain-security.description: This final layer prepares you for real engagements through competitive audits and on-chain monitoring. Ongoing vigilance protects protocols after launch.
blockchain-security.challenge: Enter a public competitive audit, submit at least one finding, and set up on-chain monitoring for a deployed contract.

children.title: Competitive Audit Platforms
children.description: Platforms like Code4rena and Sherlock host public audit competitions where many researchers review the same codebase simultaneously, dramatically increasing coverage. Participating in competitions is the fastest path to building a verifiable on-chain reputation as a security researcher.

children.title: Triage Under Time Pressure
children.description: Competitive audits are time-boxed, requiring auditors to prioritize the highest-impact attack surfaces and leave cosmetic issues for later. Poor triage discipline leads to spending hours on informational findings while critical bugs go unfound.

children.title: Immunefi and Bug Bounty Programs
children.description: Immunefi is the largest Web3 bug bounty platform, hosting programs with payouts up to $10 million for critical vulnerabilities in live protocols. Bug bounty participation provides real-world practice on production code with direct financial incentives aligned to impact.

children.title: Building an Audit Portfolio
children.description: A public portfolio of submitted findings, contest rankings, and disclosed bug bounties is the primary credential for landing paid audit engagements. Auditors without a verifiable track record cannot demonstrate competence to clients regardless of their actual skill level.

children.title: On-Chain Alerting
children.description: Monitoring tools like Forta and OpenZeppelin Defender watch deployed contracts for suspicious transaction patterns and alert teams in real time. Protocols without monitoring discover exploits from Twitter hours after funds have left, with no time to respond.

children.title: Incident Response Playbooks
children.description: A pre-written incident response playbook defines who does what in the first minutes after an exploit is detected, preventing the paralysis that costs protocols additional losses. Teams without playbooks waste critical time on communication and authorization instead of halting the attack.

children.title: Security CTFs
children.description: Capture-the-flag challenges like Ethernaut and Damn Vulnerable DeFi provide safe environments to practice exploitation of known vulnerability patterns. CTF practice is the most time-efficient way to build the muscle memory needed to recognize vulnerabilities quickly during real audits.

children.title: Staying Current with the Threat Landscape
children.description: New vulnerability classes emerge regularly as DeFi protocols adopt novel mechanisms, making continuous learning mandatory for practicing auditors. Researchers who stop following exploit disclosures quickly fall behind the attacker's evolving toolkit.

chain-architect.title: Application-Specific Blockchain Concepts
chain-architect.description: This layer introduces the idea of purpose-built blockchains and the major frameworks for building them. Understanding the design space guides every architectural decision.
chain-architect.challenge: Write a design brief comparing Cosmos SDK and Substrate for a hypothetical application chain.

children.title: Monolithic Chain Design
children.description: A monolithic blockchain bundles execution, consensus, and data availability into one layer. Misunderstanding this limits your ability to identify scalability bottlenecks or reason about trade-offs when choosing a framework.

resources.label: Celestia: What is a Modular Blockchain?

children.title: Application-Specific Chains
children.description: App-chains dedicate all block space and governance to a single application, removing contention with unrelated protocols. Without grasping this, architects default to shared chains and inherit their throughput ceilings and governance conflicts.

children.title: Sovereignty vs Shared Security
children.description: Sovereign chains control their own validator set and upgrade path, while shared-security models inherit protection from a parent chain. Mixing these concepts leads to misconfigured validator incentives and unexpected governance constraints.

children.title: State Machine Replication
children.description: Every blockchain is a replicated state machine: all nodes apply the same ordered transactions to reach the same state. A shaky understanding of this model produces inconsistent state across validators and unexplained forks.

children.title: Cosmos SDK Architecture
children.description: Cosmos SDK provides a Go-based modular framework where each module owns its own state store and message handlers. Without knowing this composability model, teams build tightly coupled logic that becomes unmaintainable at scale.

children.title: Substrate Framework Overview
children.description: Substrate separates the node client from the runtime, which is compiled to WebAssembly and can be upgraded on-chain without a hard fork. Overlooking this design means architects treat Substrate like a traditional chain and miss its greatest operational advantage.

children.title: Native Token Design
children.description: The native token funds transaction fees, secures the network through staking, and drives governance weight. Poor token design at genesis creates inflation spirals or validator centralization that cannot be fixed without a contentious hard fork.

children.title: On-Chain Governance Basics
children.description: On-chain governance lets token holders vote to change parameters or approve upgrades without relying on a central team. Ignoring governance at the architecture stage forces bolt-on solutions that rarely achieve meaningful participation.

chain-architect.title: Consensus and Tendermint
chain-architect.description: This layer studies the Tendermint BFT engine that drives many app chains. Consensus choice determines finality, throughput, and security properties.
chain-architect.challenge: Run a local Tendermint network and observe how validators reach finality on each block.

children.title: Byzantine Fault Tolerance
children.description: BFT consensus guarantees safety as long as fewer than one-third of validators are faulty or malicious. Underestimating this threshold leads architects to trust validator sets that can actually collude to produce conflicting blocks.

children.title: Consensus Phases: Propose, Prevote, Precommit
children.description: Tendermint requires two rounds of voting (prevote and precommit) before a block is finalized, providing immediate single-block finality. Skipping this detail leads to incorrect assumptions about transaction confirmation time that break bridge and relayer logic.

children.title: Validator Set Management
children.description: Voting power is proportional to bonded stake, so a small set of large validators can dominate block production. Misconfiguring the active validator cap creates centralization risks that undermine the chain's censorship-resistance guarantees.

children.title: Slashing and Fork Accountability
children.description: Slashing penalizes validators for double-signing or extended downtime, aligning economic incentives with honest behavior. Without slashing parameters that match your validator economics, the punishment cost falls below the profit from an attack.

children.title: ABCI: Separating Consensus from Application
children.description: ABCI (Application BlockChain Interface) decouples the consensus engine from application logic, so the same Tendermint core can drive any state machine. Misunderstanding ABCI means embedding consensus logic inside business code, creating fragile chains that cannot be upgraded independently.

children.title: Block Lifecycle: BeginBlock to Commit
children.description: Each block passes through BeginBlock, DeliverTx, EndBlock, and Commit phases where modules can inject logic. Ignoring this lifecycle causes side effects like reward distribution or governance tallying to run at the wrong point, producing incorrect state.

children.title: Instant Finality vs Probabilistic Finality
children.description: Tendermint provides instant, deterministic finality after each block, unlike PoW chains where finality is probabilistic. Building bridges or relayers without this distinction causes unnecessary confirmation delays or, worse, accepting blocks that are later reversed.

resources.label: Vitalik: Proof of Stake Design Philosophy

children.title: Validator Incentive Structures
children.description: Block rewards, transaction fees, and commission rates determine whether running a validator is economically rational. Poorly tuned incentives cause validator churn, which destabilizes consensus and drives stake toward centralized operators.

chain-architect.title: Building Modules with Cosmos SDK
chain-architect.description: This layer builds custom state machine modules using the Cosmos SDK. Modules are where application logic lives in a Cosmos chain.
chain-architect.challenge: Scaffold a Cosmos chain and add a custom module that stores and retrieves a simple record.

children.title: Keepers and State Management
children.description: Keepers are the only authorized objects that can read and write a module's state store, enforcing access control at compile time. Bypassing keepers by accessing stores directly breaks module isolation and introduces hard-to-detect state corruption bugs.

children.title: Messages and Handlers
children.description: Messages define the transaction payload and route to handlers that validate and execute state changes. Confusing message validation with handler logic leads to state changes that bypass important invariant checks, opening attack surfaces.

children.title: Queries and gRPC Gateway
children.description: Cosmos SDK exposes module state through gRPC services that are also proxied over HTTP via gRPC-Gateway. Skipping proper query design forces clients to parse raw blockchain events, creating brittle integrations that break on every module upgrade.

children.title: Genesis State and Parameters
children.description: Genesis defines the initial state for every module, including parameters that govern chain behavior. Incorrect genesis configuration means the chain starts with wrong inflation, unbonding periods, or max validators, requiring a hard fork to correct.

children.title: App Wiring with Depinject
children.description: Cosmos SDK v0.47+ introduces a dependency injection system (depinject) to wire modules together declaratively. Not understanding this system causes incompatible module initialization order and missing keeper dependencies that surface only at runtime.

children.title: Ignite CLI: Chain Scaffolding
children.description: Ignite CLI scaffolds a fully wired Cosmos SDK chain in seconds and generates CRUD modules, gRPC, and frontend clients. Ignoring it leads to hours of boilerplate that introduces subtle ordering bugs already solved by the scaffold generator.

children.title: Unit Testing Keepers
children.description: Cosmos modules are tested by creating an in-memory context and calling keeper methods directly without spinning up a full node. Relying only on integration tests makes feedback loops painfully slow and misses edge cases in individual keeper logic.

children.title: Simulation Testing
children.description: The Cosmos SDK simulation framework applies random operations to find invariant violations that unit tests cannot reach. Skipping simulation means shipping modules with subtle state inconsistencies that only manifest under unusual transaction orderings in production.

chain-architect.title: Building Pallets with Substrate
chain-architect.description: This layer builds runtime logic using Substrate and the FRAME framework in Rust. Substrate offers a different, upgrade-friendly approach to chain design.
chain-architect.challenge: Build a Substrate pallet that mirrors your record module and trigger a forkless runtime upgrade.

children.title: Runtime and FRAME Architecture
children.description: Substrate runtimes are compiled to Wasm, stored on-chain, and executed deterministically by every node. Treating the runtime like ordinary native code causes confusion around host functions, Wasm size limits, and the strict no-std constraint.

children.title: Writing a Pallet
children.description: A pallet uses the #[pallet] macro to declare storage, calls, events, and errors in a single Rust module. Misusing the macro or omitting the correct pallet attribute order causes cryptic compile errors that obscure the actual logic mistake.

children.title: Storage Items and Maps
children.description: Substrate provides StorageValue, StorageMap, and StorageDoubleMap as typed abstractions over the Merkle trie. Choosing the wrong storage type causes excessive proof sizes or unbounded iteration that can halt block production under load.

children.title: Extrinsics and Weight System
children.description: Extrinsics (Substrate's transactions) must declare a weight that accurately predicts their execution cost to prevent block-filling attacks. Underestimating weights lets attackers submit cheap transactions that consume far more computation than the fee covers.

children.title: On-Chain Wasm Upgrades
children.description: Substrate's runtime is stored as a Wasm blob in chain state, so an on-chain governance vote can replace it without restarting nodes. Misunderstanding migration logic causes the new runtime to read old storage layouts incorrectly, silently corrupting state.

children.title: Storage Migration Patterns
children.description: When a pallet's storage layout changes, a migration hook must transform old data into the new format before the new runtime logic runs. Shipping a runtime upgrade without a migration leaves the chain in an inconsistent state that can halt finalization.

children.title: Events and Errors
children.description: Pallet events notify off-chain services about state changes, while typed errors give callers machine-readable failure reasons. Omitting events forces indexers to parse raw storage diffs, and untyped errors make debugging production incidents extremely difficult.

children.title: Pallet Unit Testing
children.description: Substrate pallets are tested with a mock runtime that wires only the pallets under test, keeping tests fast and focused. Skipping mock runtimes causes tests to drag in unrelated pallets and makes the failing assertion impossible to isolate.

chain-architect.title: Tokenomics and Governance Design
chain-architect.description: This layer designs the economic and governance systems of a chain. Incentives and on-chain governance shape long-term security and decentralization.
chain-architect.challenge: Design and configure staking and governance parameters for your Cosmos chain and submit a test proposal.

children.title: Staking Mechanics and Inflation Rate
children.description: Staking locks tokens as collateral to secure consensus, and the inflation rate determines new token emissions split between stakers and the community pool. Getting this wrong produces either hyperinflation that dilutes holders or too-low rewards that leave the chain under-secured.

children.title: Delegation and Commission
children.description: Token holders delegate stake to validators who take a commission percentage, aligning both parties' incentives toward honest behavior. Poorly calibrated commission caps concentrate stake on the cheapest validators, creating centralization without technical safeguards.

children.title: Governance Proposal Lifecycle
children.description: Proposals pass through deposit, voting, and tallying phases before any parameter or upgrade change takes effect. Ignoring quorum and threshold settings means either governance paralysis (nothing passes) or capture (a small coalition controls all decisions).

children.title: Polkadot OpenGov and Referenda
children.description: OpenGov replaces a single council with multiple tracks that have different thresholds, allowing low-risk changes to pass quickly while high-risk ones require supermajority approval over longer periods. Treating all proposals equally creates bottlenecks for routine maintenance and risks for critical changes.

children.title: On-Chain Treasury Design
children.description: A treasury funded by a portion of transaction fees and inflation creates a sustainable source for ecosystem grants without relying on a foundation. Skipping treasury design forces every funding decision off-chain, reducing transparency and excluding community oversight.

children.title: Parameter Change Proposals
children.description: Governance modules let token holders change chain parameters (inflation, unbonding period, max validators) without a code upgrade. Without understanding parameter change proposals, teams hard-code values that cannot adapt to evolving market conditions.

children.title: Economic Security: Cost of Attack
children.description: A chain's economic security equals the cost to acquire one-third of staked tokens, which must exceed the value an attacker could extract. Chains that launch with low market cap and high staking rewards quickly become profitable to attack despite passing BFT thresholds.

children.title: Slashing Parameters and Jail Conditions
children.description: Slashing fractions and jail durations must be calibrated so that the expected penalty deters misbehavior without punishing honest validators for transient downtime. Overly aggressive slashing drives validators away; too lenient slashing makes double-signing risk-free.

chain-architect.title: Cross-Chain Communication
chain-architect.description: This layer enables chains to talk to each other through interoperability protocols. Cross-chain messaging unlocks shared liquidity and composability across ecosystems.
chain-architect.challenge: Connect two local Cosmos chains over IBC and transfer a token between them with a relayer.

children.title: IBC: Light Clients and Proofs
children.description: IBC verifies cross-chain messages by running a light client of the counterpart chain and checking Merkle proofs against its latest consensus state. Without this, an attacker can forge incoming packets and drain the destination chain's escrowed tokens.

children.title: Channels, Ports, and Packets
children.description: IBC communication is scoped to channels between specific ports on two chains, and packets carry the actual application data with sequence numbers guaranteeing ordering. Misunderstanding channel ordering (ordered vs unordered) causes packets to be silently dropped or processed out of sequence.

children.title: ICS-20 Token Transfers
children.description: ICS-20 defines how tokens are escrowed on the source chain and minted as vouchers on the destination chain, with a denom trace tracking the full path. Mishandling denom traces creates phantom tokens that appear real but can never be redeemed back to their origin.

children.title: Relayers: Hermes and Go Relayer
children.description: Relayers are off-chain processes that watch both chains and submit packet proofs, acting as the physical couriers of IBC messages. A chain without active relayers is effectively cut off from the interchain, so relayer incentives and liveness must be designed upfront.

children.title: Polkadot XCM: Cross-Consensus Messages
children.description: XCM is Polkadot's messaging format for expressing arbitrary instructions between parachains, relay chains, and external networks. Treating XCM like a simple token bridge misses its generality and leads to over-engineered custom solutions for problems XCM already solves natively.

children.title: Interoperability Security Trade-offs
children.description: Every bridge inherits the security of its weakest chain, meaning a high-value bridge to a low-security chain widens the overall attack surface. Ignoring this leads to catastrophic bridge exploits where attacking the smaller chain lets the attacker drain the larger one.

children.title: Interchain Accounts (ICS-27)
children.description: ICS-27 lets a controller chain send transactions to be executed on a host chain as if a locally controlled account signed them. Without understanding the controller-host separation, developers build fragile bespoke cross-chain execution flows that ICS-27 already handles securely.

children.title: Interchain Queries (ICQ)
children.description: ICQ allows a chain to trustlessly read the state of another chain by verifying Merkle proofs without sending a transaction. Polling state off-chain instead introduces a trust assumption on an RPC provider and defeats the trustless nature of the cross-chain design.

chain-architect.title: Modular Architecture and Data Availability
chain-architect.description: This layer explores separating execution, settlement, and data availability into distinct layers. Modular designs improve scalability and let teams specialize.
chain-architect.challenge: Diagram a modular architecture for your chain that uses a separate data availability layer.

children.title: Execution, Settlement, and Data Availability
children.description: Modular blockchains decompose a monolithic chain into specialized layers: execution produces state transitions, settlement resolves disputes, and data availability ensures anyone can verify data was published. Blurring these boundaries leads to architectures that cannot scale independently or that create hidden centralization in one of the layers.

children.title: Data Availability Sampling
children.description: Data availability sampling lets light nodes verify that block data was published by downloading random small chunks rather than the full block. Without DAS, chains must trust full nodes to check data availability, creating a bottleneck that limits block size and breaks decentralization.

children.title: Sovereign Rollups
children.description: Sovereign rollups post transaction data to a DA layer for ordering and availability but use their own fork-choice rules for settlement. Confusing sovereign rollups with smart-contract rollups causes architects to implement unnecessary fraud proof or ZK proof infrastructure for a chain that settles its own disputes.

children.title: Optimistic vs ZK Rollups
children.description: Optimistic rollups assume transactions are valid and rely on fraud proofs during a challenge window, while ZK rollups generate a validity proof for every batch. Choosing the wrong type locks a project into a finality latency or proving cost that cannot be changed without rebuilding the settlement layer.

resources.label: Vitalik: An Incomplete Guide to Rollups

children.title: Cosmos Interchain Security
children.description: Interchain Security lets consumer chains borrow the Cosmos Hub's validator set, gaining security without bootstrapping their own token and staking. Without understanding provider-consumer separation, teams either over-invest in solo validator bootstrapping or create governance dependencies they did not intend.

children.title: Polkadot Parachains and Relay Chain
children.description: Parachains lease a slot on the Polkadot relay chain, which validates their state transitions and provides finality backed by the full DOT validator set. Misunderstanding slot auctions and lease periods causes budgeting errors that leave a parachain unable to retain its slot after the first lease expires.

children.title: Sequencer Design and Centralization
children.description: Most rollups use a single sequencer for fast pre-confirmation, creating a centralization point that can censor transactions or cause liveness failures. Architects who ignore sequencer decentralization paths ship systems that achieve throughput goals but fail the credible neutrality requirement.

children.title: Trade-offs of Modularity
children.description: Modular designs reduce per-layer complexity but add cross-layer coordination overhead, latency at layer boundaries, and operational complexity for node operators. Presenting modularity as purely positive to stakeholders creates unrealistic expectations that damage trust when the coordination costs materialize.

chain-architect.title: Performance and Scalability
chain-architect.description: This layer tunes the chain for higher throughput and lower latency. Performance engineering is essential for chains expecting real usage.
chain-architect.challenge: Benchmark your chain under load and tune block parameters to improve throughput.

children.title: Block Size and Block Time Trade-offs
children.description: Larger blocks increase throughput but raise propagation latency, which pushes toward validator centralization as only high-bandwidth nodes can keep up. Setting these parameters without a propagation model causes a chain that meets TPS goals on a testnet but fragments under real-world network conditions.

children.title: Mempool Design and Priority Lanes
children.description: The mempool orders pending transactions before they are included in a block; poorly designed mempools enable MEV extraction and front-running that harm ordinary users. Cosmos SDK v0.47 introduced priority lanes to allow time-sensitive transactions (like oracle updates) to bypass spam without a fee auction.

children.title: State Growth and Pruning
children.description: Unbounded state growth forces validators to store ever-larger tries, increasing disk requirements and slowing query times until participation becomes economically irrational. Implementing state expiry or aggressive pruning at design time avoids a future hard fork to retrofit these mechanisms.

children.title: Benchmarking Pallet Weight
children.description: Substrate's benchmarking framework runs dispatchable calls with worst-case inputs to measure actual execution time and derive accurate weights. Without benchmarking, weights are guesswork: too high and fees are extortionate, too low and attackers can exhaust block capacity for pennies.

children.title: Parallel Transaction Execution
children.description: Transactions that access disjoint state can be executed concurrently, dramatically increasing throughput on multi-core hardware. Ignoring dependency analysis causes either incorrect parallel execution (data races in state) or an overly conservative serial fallback that negates the performance benefit.

children.title: IAVL and Alternative State Backends
children.description: Cosmos chains use IAVL, an immutable AVL tree, for state storage; at large state sizes its rebalancing and hashing overhead becomes a throughput bottleneck. Migrating to alternatives like IAVL v2 or SMT requires careful storage migration planning to avoid breaking existing proofs.

children.title: Validator Hardware Requirements
children.description: Block time and block size together determine the minimum CPU, memory, and disk bandwidth a validator needs to stay in sync. Specifying hardware requirements too loosely lets under-resourced validators join the active set, causing cascading timeouts that destabilize consensus.

children.title: Load Testing with Simulation Tools
children.description: Tools like gauntlet and locust let teams saturate a testnet with synthetic transactions to find latency cliffs before launch. Skipping load tests means encountering block time degradation or mempool explosion for the first time during a high-profile mainnet event.

chain-architect.title: Security and Validator Operations
chain-architect.description: This layer secures the chain and defines safe validator operations. Operational security protects the network from downtime and slashing.
chain-architect.challenge: Set up a sentry node architecture with double-sign protection for a validator on your testnet.

children.title: Validator Key Management and HSM
children.description: The consensus key that signs blocks must be kept online, making it a high-value target; hardware security modules (HSMs) and remote signers like Horcrux reduce the blast radius of a server compromise. Storing the consensus key directly on the validator machine means a single server breach results in slashing and potential double-sign jailing.

children.title: Sentry Node Architecture
children.description: Sentry nodes sit between the validator and the public internet, proxying all peer-to-peer traffic so the validator's IP address is never exposed. Exposing the validator directly makes it trivial to DDoS the node, stalling block signing and triggering downtime slashing.

children.title: Double-Sign Detection and Prevention
children.description: Signing two different blocks at the same height and round is the most severe validator offense, resulting in a large stake slash and permanent jailing. Running redundant validator processes without a shared signing coordinator almost guarantees a double-sign when both processes believe they are the primary.

children.title: High Availability Without Double-Sign
children.description: Active-passive failover for validators must ensure the passive node never signs while the active node is alive; distributed threshold signers like Horcrux provide HA without that risk. Naive active-active setups eliminate downtime risk but introduce a near-certain double-sign scenario.

children.title: Validator Monitoring with Prometheus
children.description: CometBFT and Cosmos SDK expose Prometheus metrics for block height, missed votes, peer count, and mempool size that must be alerted on in real time. Operating validators without dashboards means discovering downtime from slashing events rather than from an alert.

children.title: DDoS Mitigation for Validators
children.description: Validators are publicly identifiable targets; traffic shaping, rate limiting, and sentry-node IP rotation limit the effectiveness of volumetric attacks. Without a layered DDoS defense, a basic ICMP flood can take down block signing and trigger downtime slashing within the jailing window.

children.title: State Snapshots and Recovery
children.description: Periodic state snapshots allow a crashed validator to resync from a trusted snapshot in minutes rather than replaying the full chain history from genesis. Without snapshot infrastructure, every validator outage risks a hours-long resync that keeps the node out of the active set past the jailing threshold.

children.title: Incident Response Runbooks
children.description: Pre-written runbooks for scenarios like validator jailing, chain halt, or network split allow the operations team to respond in seconds instead of minutes. Improvising during a live incident while under stakeholder pressure consistently produces the wrong remediation and extends downtime.

chain-architect.title: Mainnet Launch and Upgrades
chain-architect.description: This final layer coordinates a mainnet launch and ongoing chain upgrades. Launching a sovereign chain demands careful planning and community coordination.
chain-architect.challenge: Run a genesis ceremony for a multi-validator testnet and execute a governance-approved upgrade.

children.title: Genesis File and Ceremony Coordination
children.description: The genesis file defines all initial state including accounts, validators, parameters, and module configurations; a genesis ceremony collects gentx submissions from all founding validators before deriving the final file. Errors in the genesis file cannot be corrected without restarting the chain from scratch, since the first block must be based on a valid genesis.

children.title: Validator Onboarding and Gentx
children.description: Each genesis validator submits a gentx (genesis transaction) that bonds stake and sets their commission rate before the chain starts. Accepting gentxs without validating minimum self-delegation or commission range creates a validator set that either centralizes immediately or charges unsustainable rates.

children.title: Cosmovisor: Automated Upgrade Manager
children.description: Cosmovisor watches for on-chain upgrade proposals and automatically swaps the node binary at the correct upgrade height without manual intervention. Requiring validators to manually swap binaries introduces a wide window where different nodes run different software, causing consensus failures if the timing is not perfectly coordinated.

children.title: Governance-Approved Chain Upgrades
children.description: Software upgrades in Cosmos require passing a governance proposal that includes the upgrade name and height, ensuring community buy-in before any node binary is swapped. Bypassing governance for urgent upgrades destroys validator and community trust and sets a precedent that undermines the chain's credible neutrality.

children.title: Chain Halt Detection and Recovery
children.description: A chain halts when more than one-third of voting power goes offline; detecting this quickly and coordinating a restart with an emergency proposal is the only recovery path. Without pre-established communication channels and runbooks, a two-thirds quorum may never be reached again, making the halt permanent.

children.title: Monitoring Block Production and Liveness
children.description: Tracking block height, voting power online, and per-validator missed-block counters in real time allows operators to preemptively reach out to lagging validators before the jailing threshold is hit. Reactive monitoring that only alerts after jailing means the chain has already degraded before any corrective action begins.

children.title: Post-Mortem Culture and Blameless Review
children.description: Publishing transparent post-mortems after incidents demonstrates accountability to the validator and delegator community and surfaces systemic improvements that prevent recurrence. Chains that silently patch issues or attribute blame to individuals erode trust and repeat the same class of outage on a longer cycle.

children.title: Upgrade Testing on Staging Networks
children.description: Running every upgrade proposal on a replica staging network with the same validator set composition catches migration bugs before they halt the mainnet. Deploying upgrades directly to mainnet without staging validation is the leading cause of production chain halts in the Cosmos ecosystem.

bitcoin-dev.title: Bitcoin Fundamentals
bitcoin-dev.description: This layer explains how Bitcoin works as a peer-to-peer electronic cash system. The UTXO model and proof of work underpin everything built on Bitcoin.
bitcoin-dev.challenge: Use a testnet wallet to send a transaction and trace its inputs and outputs on an explorer.

children.title: Unspent Transaction Outputs
children.description: Bitcoin tracks balances not as account totals but as a set of unspent outputs. Misunderstanding this leads to fee calculation errors and incorrect change address handling in applications.

resources.label: UTXO Explained — Learn Me a Bitcoin

children.title: Proof of Work and Mining
children.description: PoW is the Sybil-resistance mechanism that anchors transaction ordering in thermodynamic cost. Without understanding it, developers misread confirmation semantics and finality guarantees.

children.title: Mempool Dynamics
children.description: Unconfirmed transactions compete in the mempool by fee rate; ignoring this causes wallets and services to use stale fee estimates and get transactions stuck for hours.

children.title: Confirmations and Finality
children.description: Bitcoin achieves probabilistic finality through accumulated proof of work; treating zero-confirmation transactions as final exposes services to double-spend attacks.

children.title: Public Key Cryptography on secp256k1
children.description: Bitcoin keys are points on the secp256k1 elliptic curve; a mistake in scalar multiplication or point encoding produces invalid addresses that silently lose funds.

resources.label: Keys and Addresses — Learn Me a Bitcoin

children.title: Hash Functions in Bitcoin
children.description: SHA-256 and RIPEMD-160 are composed throughout the protocol for addresses, Merkle trees, and PoW; conflating their roles causes address generation bugs and broken block validation.

resources.label: Hash Functions — Learn Me a Bitcoin

children.title: P2P Network and Node Roles
children.description: Full nodes independently validate every rule; applications that rely on light clients or third-party APIs inherit their trust assumptions and censorship surface.

children.title: Block Structure and Merkle Trees
children.description: Blocks chain together via header hashes and commits to transactions through a Merkle tree; misreading the header fields breaks custom block parsers and SPV proofs.

bitcoin-dev.title: Keys, Addresses, and Wallets
bitcoin-dev.description: This layer covers Bitcoin's cryptography and hierarchical deterministic wallets. Correct key handling is the foundation of custody and security.
bitcoin-dev.challenge: Generate an HD wallet from a mnemonic and derive several addresses across derivation paths.

children.title: BIP32 Hierarchical Deterministic Wallets
children.description: BIP32 derives an entire tree of keys from a single seed using HMAC-SHA512; an incorrect child key derivation silently produces keys the user cannot recover, causing permanent fund loss.

resources.label: HD Wallets — Learn Me a Bitcoin

children.title: BIP39 Mnemonic Phrases
children.description: BIP39 encodes a random seed as a human-readable word list with a checksum; skipping checksum validation or stretching the phrase incorrectly produces a wallet that opens without error but holds different keys.

children.title: BIP44 Derivation Paths
children.description: BIP44 standardises the m/purpose/coin/account/change/index hierarchy so that wallets can deterministically rediscover funds; deviating from the path means imported seeds find no balance.

children.title: SegWit and Taproot Addresses
children.description: Bech32 (P2WPKH) and Bech32m (P2TR) addresses encode witness programs; sending to a Bech32m address with old Bech32 encoding corrupts the checksum and burns the funds.

children.title: Secure Entropy Generation
children.description: All Bitcoin security reduces to the unpredictability of the initial seed; using a weak PRNG or a predictable source makes every derived key brute-forceable.

children.title: Extended Public Keys and Watch-Only Wallets
children.description: An xpub can derive all public addresses without ever exposing the private key, enabling watch-only wallets; leaking an xpub alongside any child private key exposes the entire branch.

resources.label: Extended Keys — Learn Me a Bitcoin

children.title: bitcoinjs-lib Key Handling
children.description: bitcoinjs-lib wraps secp256k1 and BIP32/39 in JavaScript; misusing its ECPair or BIP32 API — such as reusing a signer object across networks — produces cross-network address collisions.

children.title: python-bitcoinlib
children.description: python-bitcoinlib gives low-level access to script, transactions, and key operations in Python; its manual serialization requires strict byte-order awareness or payloads fail consensus validation.

bitcoin-dev.title: Bitcoin Script
bitcoin-dev.description: This layer teaches Bitcoin's stack-based scripting language for locking and unlocking coins. Script defines the conditions under which bitcoin can be spent.
bitcoin-dev.challenge: Construct and spend a multisignature script on testnet that requires two of three keys.

children.title: Stack-Based Execution Model
children.description: Bitcoin Script is an intentionally non-Turing-complete stack machine; misunderstanding push/pop semantics leads to scripts that compile but fail at spend-time, permanently locking the output.

children.title: Standard Output Script Templates
children.description: P2PK, P2PKH, P2SH, P2WPKH, and P2WSH are the relay-standard templates; using a non-standard locking script means miners may relay the transaction but standard nodes will not propagate it.

children.title: Multisignature Scripts
children.description: OP_CHECKMULTISIG requires an extra dummy stack item due to an original implementation bug; omitting it causes an SCRIPT_ERR_SIG_NULLFAIL error that looks like a key problem but is purely a stack issue.

resources.label: P2SH Multisig — Learn Me a Bitcoin

children.title: Timelocks — CLTV and CSV
children.description: OP_CHECKLOCKTIMEVERIFY and OP_CHECKSEQUENCEVERIFY enforce absolute and relative time constraints at the script layer; mixing nLockTime semantics with CSV leads to channels or escrows that can never be spent.

children.title: P2SH Redeem Scripts
children.description: P2SH hides complex scripts behind a 20-byte hash and defers fee cost to the spender; supplying a redeem script that does not hash to the locking script results in an immediate script failure at spend time.

children.title: Segregated Witness Scripts
children.description: SegWit moves the witness data outside the txid commitment, fixing transaction malleability that broke payment channels; signing the wrong sighash version silently produces a valid-looking but unspendable output.

children.title: Script Debugging Tools
children.description: Interactive debuggers let you step through stack execution opcode by opcode; skipping manual tracing means subtle off-by-one errors in custom scripts go undetected until mainnet fund loss.

children.title: Miniscript
children.description: Miniscript is a structured language that compiles to Bitcoin Script and provides composable, analysable spending policies; hand-writing complex scripts without it creates unauditable locking conditions.

bitcoin-dev.title: Transactions and PSBT
bitcoin-dev.description: This layer builds raw transactions and works with partially signed Bitcoin transactions. PSBT is the standard for collaborative and hardware wallet signing.
bitcoin-dev.challenge: Create a PSBT, sign it with two parties, and broadcast the finalized transaction on testnet.

children.title: Transaction Serialization Format
children.description: Raw Bitcoin transactions follow a strict binary layout for version, inputs, outputs, and locktime; a single byte-order inversion in a txid reference creates a transaction that nodes reject at parse time.

children.title: Sighash Types
children.description: Sighash flags control which parts of a transaction the signature commits to; using SIGHASH_NONE accidentally allows anyone to rewrite the outputs and redirect funds after broadcast.

resources.label: Sighash Types — Learn Me a Bitcoin

children.title: Fee Estimation and RBF
children.description: Fees are implicitly the difference between input and output values; failing to account for change outputs inflates fees to miner windfall levels, while too-low fees strand transactions in the mempool.

children.title: CPFP — Child Pays for Parent
children.description: CPFP lets a recipient fee-bump a stuck parent transaction by spending its unconfirmed output with a high-fee child; ignoring this mechanism leaves no recourse when fee estimation was wrong.

children.title: PSBT Structure and Roles
children.description: BIP174 defines a portable container for unsigned or partially signed transactions shared between creators, updaters, signers, and finalizers; skipping the finalizer step leaves a PSBT that can never be broadcast.

children.title: Hardware Wallet Signing with PSBT
children.description: PSBTs are the standard interchange format for hardware wallets, allowing an air-gapped device to sign without seeing private keys online; a malformed global xpub map causes most hardware wallets to refuse the PSBT entirely.

children.title: Multi-Party Transaction Workflows
children.description: CoinJoin and collaborative custody require assembling inputs from multiple parties before anyone signs; broadcasting a partially constructed PSBT exposes all participants' UTXOs without achieving the privacy goal.

children.title: Output Script Descriptors
children.description: Descriptors encode full wallet policy — keys, derivation paths, and script templates — in a single portable string, replacing ad-hoc xpub sharing that loses track of script type at recovery time.

bitcoin-dev.title: Running Bitcoin Core
bitcoin-dev.description: This layer runs and queries a full Bitcoin Core node. Operating a node gives trustless access to the chain and its RPC interface.
bitcoin-dev.challenge: Run Bitcoin Core on signet and script a wallet workflow entirely through its RPC interface.

children.title: Initial Block Download and Pruning
children.description: IBD replays the entire chain from genesis to validate every rule; starting an application against a node still syncing produces stale UTXO responses that cause double-spend vulnerabilities.

resources.label: Bitcoin Core — Running a Full Node

children.title: Configuration Options
children.description: bitcoin.conf controls network, RPC, wallet, and indexing behaviour; enabling txindex after a partial sync without reindexing leaves the index incomplete and causes intermittent RPC failures.

children.title: Wallet and Chain RPC Methods
children.description: Bitcoin Core exposes hundreds of JSON-RPC methods for wallet management, chain queries, and mempool inspection; calling gettransaction on a wallet-unrelated txid silently returns an error that looks like a missing transaction.

children.title: ZMQ Notifications
children.description: Bitcoin Core publishes block and transaction events over ZeroMQ; polling the RPC instead of subscribing to ZMQ introduces latency and missed events under load.

children.title: Signet — Controlled Test Network
children.description: Signet produces blocks on demand with a signature, eliminating the hash-power instability of testnet3; using testnet3 for integration tests exposes applications to multi-hour block droughts that mask real timing bugs.

children.title: Regtest for Local Development
children.description: Regtest generates blocks instantly on command with no real PoW, enabling deterministic test flows; forgetting to mine blocks after broadcasting a transaction makes the UTXO appear unconfirmed indefinitely.

children.title: Transaction Index (txindex)
children.description: txindex lets Bitcoin Core look up any historical transaction by txid without a block hint; without it, getrawtransaction fails for transactions not in the wallet, breaking block explorers and audit tools.

children.title: Compact Block Filters (BIP157/158)
children.description: Neutrino uses compact block filters for light-client UTXO scanning without trusting a server; implementing a custom wallet scanner without filter-based sampling downloads the full block chain unnecessarily.

bitcoin-dev.title: Bitcoin Core Internals in C++
bitcoin-dev.description: This layer explores the Bitcoin Core codebase and its consensus rules in C++. Reading the reference implementation reveals how the protocol is truly enforced.
bitcoin-dev.challenge: Build Bitcoin Core from source and add a small functional test that exercises a wallet RPC.

children.title: Bitcoin Core Directory Structure
children.description: The src/ tree is divided into consensus, net, wallet, and RPC layers with strict dependency rules; patching validation code from the wallet layer bypasses the consensus boundary and risks introducing non-determinism.

children.title: Consensus vs Policy Rules
children.description: Consensus rules are enforced by all full nodes and cannot be changed without a hard fork; policy rules are local relay defaults that miners can override, and confusing them leads to transactions that are relayed but not mined.

children.title: Mempool Data Structures
children.description: The mempool indexes transactions by fee rate, ancestor chains, and conflict sets; not understanding package limits explains why a CPFP child sometimes fails to bump the parent above the effective minimum.

children.title: Package Relay
children.description: Package relay allows a parent-child transaction bundle to be evaluated together for fee rate, enabling CPFP fee-bumping of zero-fee parents; applications that do not use package relay cannot reliably unstick stuck transactions.

children.title: Functional and Unit Tests
children.description: Bitcoin Core ships a Python functional test framework in test/functional/; skipping tests before submitting a patch means CI catches regressions only after a full build cycle, slowing review.

children.title: Fuzzing Bitcoin Core
children.description: Bitcoin Core uses libFuzzer and AFL to fuzz-test parsers and cryptographic primitives; uninstrumented code changes in serialization paths are the most common source of CVE-level crash bugs.

children.title: P2P Message Protocol
children.description: Bitcoin's wire protocol uses a fixed-size 24-byte header and variable-length payload; an off-by-one in message length parsing causes the receiving peer to desync and drop the connection.

children.title: Compact Block Relay (BIP152)
children.description: BIP152 lets nodes reconstruct blocks from short transaction IDs they likely already have, reducing latency; nodes that do not advertise sendcmpct fall back to full-block download and increase propagation time.

bitcoin-dev.title: Taproot and Advanced Scripting
bitcoin-dev.description: This layer covers Taproot, Schnorr signatures, and modern scripting capabilities. These upgrades improve privacy and flexibility for complex spending conditions.
topics: Key path and script path spends
bitcoin-dev.challenge: Create a Taproot output with both a key path and a script path spend and test both on signet.

children.title: Schnorr Signature Scheme (BIP340)
children.description: BIP340 defines 64-byte Schnorr signatures over secp256k1 with linear homomorphism, enabling key and signature aggregation; using the legacy ECDSA signing path for Taproot key-path spends produces an invalid witness that fails immediately.

children.title: MuSig2 Key Aggregation
children.description: MuSig2 allows n-of-n signers to produce a single Schnorr signature that looks like any other key-path spend, providing both efficiency and privacy; naively combining keys without the MuSig2 protocol exposes all signers to rogue-key attacks.

children.title: Taproot Internal Key and Tweak (BIP341)
children.description: A Taproot output commits to both a key-path internal key and an optional Merkle root of scripts; tweaking the internal key incorrectly with the wrong hash makes the key-path spend produce an invalid signature every time.

children.title: Tapscript (BIP342)
children.description: Tapscript updates several opcodes for Taproot script-path spends, including replacing OP_CHECKMULTISIG with OP_CHECKSIGADD; using the old multisig opcode inside a Tapscript leaf returns an immediate script failure.

children.title: Taproot Merkle Branch Proofs
children.description: To spend via a script path, the witness must include the leaf script, the control block with the internal key, and the Merkle proof; supplying siblings in wrong order or omitting the parity bit in the control block invalidates the proof.

children.title: Key-Path vs Script-Path Privacy
children.description: Key-path spends look identical to single-key P2TR outputs on-chain, hiding that scripts exist; using script-path when key-path is possible unnecessarily reveals the script tree and degrades the privacy of the UTXO set.

children.title: FROST Threshold Signatures
children.description: FROST extends Schnorr to t-of-n threshold signing with a single aggregated signature; implementing a naive Shamir-based threshold that signs independently produces n separate signatures rather than one aggregated output.

children.title: Adaptor Signatures
children.description: Adaptor signatures atomically link payment to disclosure of a secret, enabling cross-chain atomic swaps and point time-lock contracts; a missing nonce commitment in the adaptor allows a counterparty to extract the secret without completing payment.

bitcoin-dev.title: Lightning Network Fundamentals
bitcoin-dev.description: This layer introduces the Lightning Network for fast off-chain payments. Lightning scales Bitcoin payments to high volume and low fees.
bitcoin-dev.challenge: Open a Lightning channel on testnet, route a payment, and then cooperatively close the channel.

children.title: Channel Funding and Commitment Transactions
children.description: A Lightning channel is a 2-of-2 multisig UTXO with a series of off-chain commitment transactions that represent the current balance split; broadcasting a revoked commitment exposes the broadcaster to a penalty transaction that forfeits their entire channel balance.

children.title: Hash Time-Lock Contracts (HTLCs)
children.description: HTLCs enable trustless multi-hop routing by conditioning payment on revealing a preimage before a timeout; setting the HTLC expiry too short relative to the downstream hop gives the sender insufficient time to claim funds on-chain if the channel goes dark.

children.title: Onion Routing and Source-Routing
children.description: Lightning uses Sphinx onion packets so intermediate nodes learn only their predecessor and successor; reusing the same payment hash across attempts lets a routing node correlate probes and deanonymise the sender.

children.title: Gossip Protocol and Channel Announcements
children.description: Nodes learn the network graph through gossip messages that commit to on-chain UTXOs; failing to refresh channel_update messages causes routing nodes to drop the channel from their graph and route around it.

children.title: Cooperative and Force-Close
children.description: A cooperative close produces a clean on-chain output immediately; a force-close requires waiting out CSV timelocks on the local output, and failing to monitor for the other party's force-close allows them to steal using a revoked state.

children.title: Watchtowers
children.description: Watchtowers monitor the chain for revoked commitment broadcasts and automatically publish the penalty transaction on behalf of the victim; without a watchtower, a node that goes offline even briefly can lose its entire channel balance.

children.title: Inbound and Outbound Liquidity
children.description: A channel can only route payments in the direction where the sender holds funds; a node with all outbound but no inbound liquidity can send but will fail to receive, blocking merchant use cases entirely.

children.title: Channel Rebalancing
children.description: Circular rebalancing routes a payment through the network back to the originating node to redistribute liquidity; aggressive circular rebalancing at high fee rates costs more than the routing revenue it enables.

bitcoin-dev.title: Building Lightning Applications
bitcoin-dev.description: This layer builds applications on top of a Lightning node using its API. Integrating Lightning enables instant payments inside real products.
bitcoin-dev.challenge: Build a small app that generates invoices and accepts Lightning payments through a node API.

children.title: LND gRPC and REST API
children.description: LND exposes its full functionality over gRPC and a REST gateway; using the REST gateway for high-frequency invoice polling introduces latency that gRPC streaming subscriptions eliminate.

children.title: CLN JSON-RPC and Plugins
children.description: Core Lightning uses a Unix-domain JSON-RPC socket and a plugin system for extensions; blocking a plugin's main loop delays all other RPC calls because CLN's plugin host is single-threaded per plugin by default.

children.title: BOLT11 Invoice Encoding
children.description: BOLT11 invoices encode amount, payment hash, expiry, and routing hints in a bech32 string; an invoice with a zero-amount field is valid but lets the payer choose the amount, creating under-payment vulnerabilities in e-commerce integrations.

children.title: BOLT12 Offers
children.description: BOLT12 offers are static, reusable payment codes that support recurring payments and payer authentication unlike one-time BOLT11 invoices; implementing tip jars with BOLT11 requires generating a fresh invoice per visitor, creating server-side state for every page load.

children.title: Keysend Spontaneous Payments
children.description: Keysend embeds the payment preimage in a TLV onion record, enabling payments without a prior invoice; the recipient must have keysend enabled or the payment fails at the final hop with an unknown preimage error.

children.title: TLV Custom Records
children.description: Lightning's onion and payment fields support arbitrary TLV records for application-layer metadata; using odd TLV type numbers is mandatory for forward-compatible extensions — an even unknown type causes the receiving node to fail the payment.

children.title: Autopilot and Channel Opening Strategy
children.description: Autopilot heuristics choose peers by graph centrality and capacity; opening too many small channels fragments liquidity below the minimum HTLC size and results in channels that cannot route even small payments.

children.title: MPP — Multi-Part Payments
children.description: MPP splits a large payment across multiple paths, overcoming single-channel capacity limits; a node that does not advertise the MPP feature bit will receive MPP shards as individual failed payments rather than holding them until complete.

bitcoin-dev.title: Production Deployment and Security
bitcoin-dev.description: This final layer deploys Bitcoin and Lightning infrastructure securely for production. Custody and uptime demand rigorous operational security.
bitcoin-dev.challenge: Deploy a hardened Bitcoin and Lightning node setup with secure backups and uptime monitoring.

children.title: Securing the Bitcoin Node Host
children.description: Bitcoin Core holds the wallet and signs transactions; running it on an internet-facing server with a weak RPC password exposes funds to trivial RPC-over-HTTP theft.

children.title: Lightning Node Security
children.description: LND and CLN store the channel state database on disk in plaintext by default; a compromised host can drain hot wallet funds and steal channel balances by replaying old states or extracting seed files.

children.title: Multisig Cold Storage
children.description: Cold storage using hardware wallets and multisig eliminates single points of failure; a 2-of-3 scheme where all three keys share the same geolocation provides false diversification that a single physical breach defeats.

children.title: Hot Wallet Limits and Sweep Policies
children.description: Production services keep only working capital in hot wallets and sweep excess to cold storage; failing to set withdrawal limits means a single application-layer compromise drains the entire operational reserve.

children.title: Bitcoin Core Wallet Backups
children.description: Bitcoin Core's wallet.dat contains keys and metadata; because HD wallets derive new addresses after each transaction, a backup taken before a gap fill may miss recently used keys and appear to have lost funds.

children.title: Lightning Channel Backups (SCB)
children.description: LND's Static Channel Backup records the channel funding outpoints and peer pubkeys; without an SCB, a node that loses its channel state database cannot cooperatively close channels and the funds are stuck until the peer force-closes.

children.title: Node Health Metrics
children.description: Critical metrics include peer count, mempool size, block tip age, and channel balance ratios; an unmonitored node that loses all peers processes no transactions and silently falls behind chain tip.

children.title: Fee Bump Automation
children.description: Long-lived pending HTLCs must be fee-bumped before their on-chain timeout expires; services without automated CPFP or RBF logic for commitment transactions risk time-locked outputs expiring and funds being swept by the counterparty.

cosmos-dev.title: Cosmos Ecosystem Fundamentals
cosmos-dev.description: This layer introduces the Cosmos vision of an internet of blockchains and its core stack. Understanding the ecosystem clarifies why app chains and IBC matter.
cosmos-dev.challenge: Install the toolchain and spin up a single-node Cosmos chain locally to send a test transaction.

children.title: Internet of Blockchains
children.description: Cosmos proposes that every application should own its sovereign chain rather than compete on a shared one. Misunderstanding this leads to over-relying on general-purpose chains and inheriting their congestion and governance constraints.

children.title: App Chains vs Shared Chains
children.description: App chains give teams full control over consensus parameters, fee tokens, and upgrade schedules. Without grasping this trade-off you will either over-engineer a module that belongs in a contract or under-engineer a contract that needs its own chain.

children.title: The ABCI Interface
children.description: ABCI is the boundary between the consensus engine and your application logic. Confusing ABCI calls causes silent state divergence between nodes, which is one of the hardest bugs to diagnose in a live chain.

children.title: Accounts and Keys
children.description: Cosmos accounts use HD wallets and secp256k1 or ed25519 keys with bech32 addresses. Getting address derivation wrong means funds sent to a user can be unrecoverable or attributed to the wrong party.

children.title: Interfaces and Structs
children.description: The Cosmos SDK is built on Go interfaces for every keeper and codec boundary. Implementing an interface incorrectly compiles fine but produces nil-pointer panics or missing functionality that only surfaces under specific transactions.

children.title: Error Handling in Go
children.description: Cosmos SDK handlers must return typed SDK errors so the ABCI layer encodes them correctly for clients. Swallowing or misformatting errors breaks CLI output and prevents relayers from detecting failures.

children.title: Protobuf Type Definitions
children.description: Every message, query, and state object in the Cosmos SDK is defined in .proto files and generated with buf. Editing generated files directly or mismatching field numbers breaks binary encoding across nodes and breaks state import/export.

children.title: Code Generation with buf
children.description: buf enforces lint rules and breaking-change detection on your proto definitions. Skipping this tooling allows silent API breaks between chain versions that crash IBC counterparties or relayers.

cosmos-dev.title: Go for Cosmos Development
cosmos-dev.description: This layer builds the Go skills required to work effectively in the Cosmos SDK. Comfort with Go and protobuf is essential for module development.
cosmos-dev.challenge: Write a Go package with protobuf-defined types and generate the corresponding Go code.

children.title: Go Module System
children.description: Cosmos SDK versioning is tightly coupled to Go module paths, and major version upgrades change the import path. Confusing module versioning leads to mixed SDK versions in a single binary, causing unpredictable runtime panics.

children.title: Testing in Go
children.description: Go's table-driven testing and testify suite underpin all Cosmos SDK unit tests. Without mastering these patterns your module tests will be brittle and miss edge cases that cause funds to be locked or burned.

children.title: Writing .proto Files
children.description: Protobuf field numbers are permanent binary identifiers — changing them corrupts stored state when the chain is upgraded. Understanding this contract before writing your first message type prevents irreversible data loss.

children.title: Generating Go Code
children.description: The Cosmos SDK uses buf with protoc-gen-gocosmos and protoc-gen-grpc-gateway to emit both gRPC server stubs and REST gateway wrappers. Generating only half the stack leaves either the CLI or the REST API broken.

children.title: Ignite CLI Scaffolding
children.description: Ignite CLI generates boilerplate chains, modules, and types in seconds, saving days of repetitive wiring. Misusing scaffold commands without reading the output leads to duplicate registrations that prevent the chain from compiling.

children.title: Local Chain Startup
children.description: Running a single-node devnet with a known genesis lets you iterate on module logic before writing any tests. Skipping this step means bugs are first discovered under multi-node network conditions, which makes root-cause analysis exponentially harder.

children.title: Goroutines and Channels
children.description: Background workers in Cosmos nodes (relayers, event listeners) rely on goroutines and channels for safe message passing. Race conditions introduced here produce non-deterministic node behavior that consensus engines will reject.

children.title: Context Propagation
children.description: The Cosmos SDK passes sdk.Context through every keeper call to carry block height, gas meter, and event manager. Dropping or copying the context incorrectly silently discards emitted events or charges gas against the wrong meter.

cosmos-dev.title: Cosmos SDK Architecture
cosmos-dev.description: This layer dissects how a Cosmos application is structured around modules and the baseapp. Knowing the architecture lets you place logic in the right component.
cosmos-dev.challenge: Scaffold a chain and trace a transaction from the ante handler through to state changes.

children.title: BaseApp Request Routing
children.description: BaseApp routes every transaction message to the correct module handler via the message router. Registering a handler under the wrong type URL means transactions silently succeed with no state change, a hard-to-detect correctness bug.

children.title: Begin and End Block Hooks
children.description: BeginBlock and EndBlock give modules a chance to run logic at block boundaries, such as distributing rewards or expiring orders. Running expensive operations in these hooks without gas bounding can halt the chain by exceeding block time limits.

children.title: Keeper Pattern
children.description: Keepers are the only sanctioned way for one module to access another module's state. Bypassing keeper interfaces by reading raw store keys breaks module encapsulation and creates hidden state coupling that causes upgrade migrations to fail.

children.title: Dependency Injection Wiring
children.description: The Cosmos SDK v0.47+ uses depinject to wire module dependencies at compile time. Failing to declare a dependency correctly produces a runtime panic at startup rather than a compile-time error, which is confusing for new developers.

children.title: IAVL Merkle Store
children.description: Cosmos chains store state in an IAVL Merkle tree which produces a per-block app hash committed to consensus. Writing to the wrong store prefix or missing a commit causes the node to diverge from the network and be rejected by validators.

children.title: AnteHandlers and Gas
children.description: AnteHandlers run before any message handler to authenticate, deduct fees, and enforce invariants. A misconfigured ante chain allows unauthenticated messages or double-spend attacks to reach application state.

children.title: Transaction Encoding
children.description: Cosmos SDK transactions use protobuf-encoded Tx envelopes with sign docs that include chain-ID and sequence number. Getting any field wrong causes signature verification to fail, making all transactions from that account permanently rejected.

children.title: Events and Indexing
children.description: Every message handler should emit typed events so off-chain indexers and relayers can track state changes without scanning blocks. Omitting events forces consumers to re-index the entire chain history when they need to reconstruct state.

cosmos-dev.title: Building Custom Modules
cosmos-dev.description: This layer builds a custom SDK module with messages, state, and queries. Custom modules are where your application's unique logic lives.
cosmos-dev.challenge: Build a custom module that lets users create and query named items on your chain.

children.title: Defining SDK Messages
children.description: SDK messages are protobuf types that implement sdk.Msg and carry their own ValidateBasic check. Missing or weak validation allows malformed inputs to reach keeper logic and corrupt store state in ways that cannot be undone without a hard fork.

children.title: Keeper State and Stores
children.description: Keepers expose typed get/set/delete helpers that abstract raw key-value store operations. Writing raw bytes without a stable key schema causes silent data collisions when two objects hash to the same prefix.

children.title: Genesis Import and Export
children.description: Every module must implement InitGenesis and ExportGenesis to participate in chain snapshots and restarts. A module that skips these functions loses all its state when a node restarts from a genesis file, breaking the chain for anyone who joins late.

children.title: Module Parameters
children.description: Module parameters are on-chain governance-controlled values stored in the params subspace. Hard-coding values that should be parameters prevents the community from adjusting them without a code release, stifling chain evolution.

children.title: gRPC Query Handlers
children.description: Cosmos SDK queries are served over gRPC and automatically exposed via a REST gateway. Forgetting to register the query server means the module's state is write-only from a client perspective, breaking explorers and wallets.

children.title: Pagination in Queries
children.description: Store iterations without pagination can return unbounded result sets that exhaust node memory and time out clients. The SDK's query.PageRequest type provides cursor-based pagination that keeps queries safe under large state sizes.

children.title: Keeper Unit Tests
children.description: Keeper tests use an in-memory store with a mocked context to validate state transitions in isolation. Skipping keeper tests means bugs in state logic are only caught by slow end-to-end tests or, worse, on a live chain.

children.title: CLI Integration Tests
children.description: The Cosmos SDK provides a network.Network test harness for spinning up a local chain inside a test. Without integration tests your message handlers may pass in isolation but fail when fees, ante handlers, and multi-message batching interact.

cosmos-dev.title: Tokens, Staking, and Governance
cosmos-dev.description: This layer integrates the standard bank, staking, and governance modules. These modules provide the economic backbone of any Cosmos chain.
cosmos-dev.challenge: Wire staking and governance into your chain and pass a parameter change proposal by vote.

children.title: Bank Module Token Transfers
children.description: The bank module is the single authority over coin balances and must be called through its keeper interface. Minting or burning tokens by writing directly to the account store bypasses supply tracking and produces an inconsistent total supply.

children.title: Staking and Delegation
children.description: The staking module manages validator power and delegates the right to propose and sign blocks. Misconfiguring unbonding periods or commission caps creates economic attack surfaces that can drain delegators without recourse.

children.title: Distribution Rewards
children.description: The distribution module lazily computes validator and delegator rewards using historical reference points. Calling distribution keeper methods out of order or before rewards are withdrawn corrupts the internal accounting and locks staked funds.

children.title: Module Account Permissions
