children.description: Module accounts require explicit minter, burner, or staking permission flags registered in the account keeper. Omitting a required permission causes the bank keeper to panic when the module tries to mint tokens, halting block processing.

children.title: Governance Proposal Lifecycle
children.description: Cosmos governance proposals go through deposit, voting, and tally phases before any handler executes. Triggering upgrades or parameter changes outside governance removes validator community oversight and breaks the social contract of the chain.

children.title: Parameter Change Proposals
children.description: Parameter change proposals are the standard mechanism for adjusting on-chain constants without deploying new code. Hard-coding what should be a parameter means every tuning exercise requires a full software upgrade and validator coordination.

children.title: Token Issuance and Supply
children.description: Choosing an inflation model and initial distribution in genesis is permanent without a governance migration. Misaligned tokenomics produces either hyperinflation that devalues stakers or an undersupplied chain that cannot attract validators.

children.title: Slashing Parameters
children.description: Slashing fractions and downtime thresholds determine validator economic risk and directly influence decentralization. Setting slash fractions too high drives validators to over-bond expensive infrastructure; too low removes the security incentive entirely.

cosmos-dev.title: Inter-Blockchain Communication
cosmos-dev.description: This layer connects your chain to others using the IBC protocol. IBC is the defining feature that lets Cosmos chains exchange value and data.
cosmos-dev.challenge: Establish an IBC channel between two local chains and transfer tokens across it with a relayer.

children.title: Light Clients and Connections
children.description: IBC light clients track the consensus state of a counterparty chain without trusting a third party. A misconfigured or expired light client freezes all channels that depend on it, blocking cross-chain token transfers until governance unfreezes it.

children.title: Channels and Packet Lifecycle
children.description: IBC packets go through send, receive, acknowledge, and timeout phases, each requiring a relayer submission. Failing to handle timeouts means packets hang indefinitely and escrowed funds on the source chain are never released.

children.title: ICS-20 Transfer Protocol
children.description: ICS-20 defines how fungible tokens are escrowed on the source and minted as vouchers on the destination. Misunderstanding the denom trace path causes wallets and DEXes to treat the same asset as different tokens, fragmenting liquidity.

children.title: Denom Trace and IBC Hashes
children.description: IBC token denominations are SHA-256 hashes of the full channel path, not human-readable names. Displaying raw IBC hashes to users without resolving the trace causes confusion and trust issues that reduce adoption of cross-chain features.

children.title: Hermes Relayer Setup
children.description: Hermes is the production-grade IBC relayer that submits client updates, connection handshakes, and packet messages on behalf of both chains. A misconfigured key or insufficient gas settings causes relayer transactions to fail silently, stalling all packet flows.

children.title: Relayer Economics and Monitoring
children.description: Relayers pay gas on both chains and must be funded continuously to avoid channel liveness failures. Without monitoring wallet balances and packet queues, a drained relayer causes user transactions to time out and escrowed funds to be refunded.

children.title: Interchain Accounts (ICS-27)
children.description: Interchain Accounts allow a chain to control an account on another chain via IBC, enabling cross-chain DeFi without trust assumptions. Incorrectly sequencing ICA transactions can result in funds sent to an uncontrolled address with no recovery path.

children.title: IBC Fee Middleware
children.description: IBC fee middleware allows senders to incentivize relayers by attaching fees to packets. Deploying cross-chain applications without fee incentives means relayers have no economic reason to relay your packets, leading to indefinite delays.

cosmos-dev.title: CosmWasm Smart Contracts
cosmos-dev.description: This layer adds WebAssembly smart contracts to a Cosmos chain using CosmWasm. CosmWasm enables flexible contract logic without forking the chain.
cosmos-dev.challenge: Write and deploy a CosmWasm contract that stores a counter and exposes execute and query messages.

children.title: Instantiate, Execute, and Query Entry Points
children.description: Every CosmWasm contract exposes three mandatory entry points that map to contract creation, state mutation, and read-only access. Confusing execute and query semantics by writing state inside a query handler causes non-deterministic results that will be rejected by the wasm VM.

children.title: Contract Storage with cw-storage-plus
children.description: cw-storage-plus provides typed Map, Item, and IndexedMap helpers that manage key namespacing automatically. Using raw storage keys manually risks prefix collisions between storage structures, corrupting unrelated data on every contract call.

children.title: Ownership and Borrowing
children.description: CosmWasm contracts are compiled to Wasm from Rust, where the borrow checker enforces memory safety at compile time. Misunderstanding ownership leads to excessive cloning of large state objects, inflating gas costs and slowing contract execution.

children.title: Error Handling with thiserror
children.description: CosmWasm contracts use thiserror-derived enums to produce descriptive error messages that are surfaced through the SDK. Returning generic string errors makes debugging in production nearly impossible because the error context is lost at the ABCI boundary.

children.title: Uploading and Instantiating
children.description: CosmWasm separates code upload (storing Wasm bytecode) from instantiation (creating a contract instance with state). Conflating these steps causes teams to re-upload identical bytecode on every deployment, wasting gas and cluttering the code store.

children.title: Contract Migration
children.description: CosmWasm supports on-chain contract migration so bugs can be fixed without deploying a new address. Omitting a migrate entry point locks the contract to its initial code forever, preventing critical security patches from being applied.

children.title: SubMessages and Replies
children.description: CosmWasm sub-messages allow a contract to dispatch messages to other contracts and optionally handle their replies. Ignoring reply handling after a sub-message that transfers funds means failed transfers are silently swallowed and the sending contract's state becomes inconsistent.

children.title: IBC-Enabled Contracts
children.description: CosmWasm contracts can act as IBC apps by implementing the IBC entry points, enabling cross-chain contract calls. Implementing these entry points without understanding the packet lifecycle results in permanently locked cross-chain escrow balances.

cosmos-dev.title: Clients, Tooling, and Frontends
cosmos-dev.description: This layer connects frontends and services to a Cosmos chain using CosmJS. Client tooling turns your chain into a usable application.
cosmos-dev.challenge: Build a web frontend with Keplr that creates and lists items from your custom module.

children.title: Connecting and Signing
children.description: CosmJS provides SigningStargateClient which handles transaction encoding, fee estimation, and broadcasting. Using a read-only client for signing operations produces confusing errors that appear as network failures but are actually API misuse.

children.title: Keplr Wallet Integration
children.description: Keplr is the standard browser wallet for Cosmos chains and exposes an offlineSigner interface consumed by CosmJS. Requesting signing before suggesting the chain to Keplr causes a silent failure where the transaction is never constructed.

children.title: StargateClient Query Methods
children.description: StargateClient exposes typed query methods that map directly to the gRPC query services registered on-chain. Using the REST fallback instead of gRPC-web introduces an extra encoding layer that loses type information and slows response times.

children.title: Indexing Events for Frontends
children.description: Block explorers and frontends must subscribe to tendermint WebSocket event streams or use an indexer like Mintscan to avoid polling every block. Polling without an indexer creates unbearable latency and will be rate-limited by public RPC nodes.

children.title: Building a Cosmos dApp
children.description: A Cosmos frontend connects via CosmJS, manages wallet state with React context, and displays chain data from a node or indexer. Skipping wallet state management causes the UI to lose the signer on page refresh, forcing users to reconnect on every action.

children.title: Fee Estimation
children.description: Cosmos transactions require a gas limit and fee amount that must be estimated before signing. Under-estimating gas causes transactions to fail mid-execution, which can leave partial state changes that break user-facing invariants.

children.title: Chain CLI (gaiad / chaind)
children.description: Every Cosmos chain exposes a CLI binary for sending transactions, querying state, and managing keys. Not mastering the CLI makes debugging production issues slower because the CLI is the fastest path to inspecting live chain state.

children.title: Tendermint RPC
children.description: The CometBFT RPC exposes block, transaction, and consensus data independently of the application query layer. Understanding which data lives in the Tendermint RPC versus the app gRPC prevents building clients that miss critical consensus-level information.

cosmos-dev.title: Testing and Upgrades
cosmos-dev.description: This layer establishes testing and in-place upgrade workflows for a chain. Reliable upgrades let a live chain evolve without halting.
cosmos-dev.challenge: Write tests for your module and perform a governance-gated upgrade with a state migration on a testnet.

children.title: Simulation Testing
children.description: The Cosmos SDK simulation framework generates random valid transactions and checks invariants after each block. Skipping simulation means subtle state corruption bugs survive unit tests and only surface as a halted chain under real user traffic.

children.title: Invariant Checks
children.description: Module invariants are assertions registered with the crisis module that can be triggered on-chain to halt the chain if state is corrupt. Not writing invariants removes the last safety net before a bug causes silent, undetected fund loss.

children.title: x/upgrade and Plan Handlers
children.description: The x/upgrade module coordinates a chain-wide halt at a specific block height so validators can swap binaries atomically. Deploying a new binary without registering an upgrade plan leaves validators on different software versions, causing a consensus fork.

children.title: State Migration Handlers
children.description: Migration handlers run once during an upgrade to transform old state structures into the new schema. A migration that reads the wrong store key or skips a data type leaves orphaned state that causes keepers to panic on the first post-upgrade transaction.

children.title: Coordinated Upgrade on Testnet
children.description: A testnet upgrade rehearsal validates that the upgrade plan, cosmovisor configuration, and migration handler all work together before risking mainnet funds. Skipping the rehearsal means the upgrade team discovers validator coordination issues live, often causing extended chain halts.

children.title: IBC Compatibility Testing
children.description: A chain upgrade that changes IBC light client parameters or consensus state format must be tested against all connected chains before mainnet deployment. An incompatible upgrade will freeze IBC channels to every connected chain, cutting off cross-chain liquidity.

children.title: Software Upgrade Proposals
children.description: Upgrades in production Cosmos chains are submitted as governance proposals that set the upgrade height and binary info. Bypassing governance to upgrade out-of-band breaks validator trust, violates the chain's social contract, and may constitute a contentious hard fork.

children.title: Cosmovisor Auto-Download
children.description: Cosmovisor can automatically download and verify binaries when an upgrade plan includes a download URL. Distributing the upgrade binary only through manual channels causes stragglers to miss the upgrade height, fragmenting the validator set.

cosmos-dev.title: Mainnet Launch and Operations
cosmos-dev.description: This final layer launches a Cosmos chain to production and operates it reliably. A successful launch requires validator coordination and strong monitoring.
cosmos-dev.challenge: Launch a multi-validator testnet of your chain with IBC relaying and full monitoring in place.

children.title: Genesis File Construction
children.description: The genesis file sets every module's initial state including validator keys, token allocations, and consensus parameters. An incorrect genesis hash causes the entire validator set to refuse to start, requiring a new genesis ceremony that delays launch.

children.title: Validator Onboarding
children.description: Validators must submit create-validator transactions with precise commission rates and self-delegation before the genesis height. Misconfigured commission caps lock validators into unfavorable economics permanently because the cap cannot be raised after it is set.

children.title: Prometheus Metrics
children.description: CometBFT and the Cosmos SDK expose Prometheus metrics for consensus rounds, block times, mempool size, and gRPC latency. Launching without a monitoring dashboard means operator teams are blind to performance degradation until users report transaction failures.

children.title: Alerting on Chain Health
children.description: Alerting on missing block signatures and voting power changes gives on-call engineers advance warning before jailing or slashing occurs. Without automated alerts a validator can go offline for hours before anyone notices, resulting in slash penalties and delegator losses.

children.title: IBC Channel Management
children.description: Production chains require multiple funded relayer accounts and redundant relayer processes to guarantee liveness. A single relayer is a single point of failure; if it crashes during a packet timeout window, escrowed user funds become temporarily inaccessible.

children.title: Coordinated Upgrades in Production
children.description: Mainnet upgrades require validator coordination via governance, pre-announced binary hashes, and a rehearsed rollback plan. An uncoordinated upgrade causes the chain to fork at the upgrade height, splitting token balances between two incompatible chains.

children.title: Chain Halt Recovery
children.description: A chain halt due to consensus failure or panic requires a coordinated binary patch and restart with an emergency governance proposal. Teams without a documented runbook take hours longer to recover, during which all IBC channels accumulate unrelayed packets.

children.title: Security Patch Coordination
children.description: Security vulnerabilities in the Cosmos SDK or CometBFT are disclosed under embargo to validator operators before public release. Operators who are not subscribed to security advisories patch late, leaving the chain exposed until a sufficient voting power threshold has upgraded.

move-developer.title: Move Language Foundations
move-developer.description: This layer introduces Move, a resource-oriented language built for safe asset handling. Move's design prevents whole classes of bugs common in other smart contract languages.
move-developer.challenge: Write and run a simple Move module that defines a basic struct and a function that returns it.

children.title: Resource-Oriented Programming
children.description: Move treats assets as first-class resources that cannot be copied or silently discarded. Misunderstanding this mental model leads to patterns that compile but violate asset-safety guarantees at runtime.

children.title: Modules and Scripts
children.description: Modules are the deployment unit in Move and define reusable logic; scripts are one-off transaction entry points. Confusing their roles causes incorrect visibility, unreachable code, or broken upgrade paths.

resources.label: The Move Book — Modules and Scripts

children.title: Primitive Types and Functions
children.description: Move's integer types, booleans, addresses, and vectors underpin every program. Getting integer widths or address representations wrong silently corrupts arithmetic and storage keys.

children.title: Function Visibility
children.description: Public, friend, and private visibility control which callers can invoke a function. Over-exposing internal helpers breaks encapsulation and opens upgrade-safety holes.

children.title: The Move Execution Model
children.description: Move's bytecode verifier enforces safety properties before code even runs, eliminating entire vulnerability classes. Skipping this mental model leads developers to add redundant checks or miss the ones the verifier cannot catch.

children.title: Aptos vs Sui at a Glance
children.description: Aptos uses a global account-storage model while Sui uses owned objects; choosing the wrong chain for a use case causes fundamental architectural rework later. Early exposure to both execution environments sharpens design decisions.

children.title: Move CLI and Package Manager
children.description: The Move CLI compiles, tests, and publishes packages; misunderstanding its project layout breaks builds before any code logic is wrong. A working local toolchain is the prerequisite for every subsequent step.

children.title: Hello World in Move
children.description: Writing and running a minimal module cements the compile-test-publish loop and surfaces environment issues early. Developers who skip this step often discover toolchain problems mid-project when they are harder to isolate.

move-developer.title: Resources and Abilities
move-developer.description: This layer explores Move's core innovation of resources and the ability system. Abilities control how values can be copied, dropped, and stored, enforcing asset safety.
move-developer.challenge: Define a coin-like resource with the right abilities so it cannot be copied or silently dropped.

children.title: The Four Abilities: copy, drop, store, key
children.description: Each ability gates a different operation on a value; attaching the wrong subset to an asset struct allows duplication or silent destruction. Getting abilities right is the single most important correctness decision in a Move module.

children.title: Resources vs Copyable Types
children.description: Resources lack copy and drop, forcing explicit handling at every use site; copyable types flow freely like ordinary data. Treating a resource as copyable data destroys the asset-safety guarantee Move was built around.

children.title: Ownership and Move Semantics
children.description: Values in Move are moved, not copied, unless the copy ability is present; ignoring this causes use-after-move compiler errors or unintended resource consumption. A firm grasp of move semantics is the prerequisite for safe resource passing.

children.title: References and Borrowing
children.description: Move allows immutable and mutable references that never outlive their owner, preventing dangling-pointer bugs at compile time. Misusing references inside loops or across function calls produces borrow-check failures that are confusing without a solid mental model.

children.title: Designing Safe Structs
children.description: Struct field visibility and ability combinations determine what external callers can do with a value; leaking mutable access to internal fields undermines invariants. Deliberate struct design is the foundation of correct module interfaces.

children.title: Preventing Duplication with Abilities
children.description: Omitting copy and drop from a token struct makes accidental duplication a compile error rather than a runtime exploit. This is the mechanism that lets Move eliminate double-spend bugs without runtime checks.

children.title: Modeling a Coin-Like Resource
children.description: Building a simple coin struct with the correct ability set ingrains the rules viscerally before tackling real token standards. Skipping this exercise leaves developers cargo-culting ability annotations without understanding the consequences.

children.title: Borrow Check Exercises
children.description: Intentionally triggering and resolving borrow-check errors builds intuition for the reference rules faster than reading docs alone. Developers who have not done this often ship code that works in testing but fails under concurrent borrow scenarios.

move-developer.title: Building on Aptos
move-developer.description: This layer builds and deploys Move modules on the Aptos blockchain. Aptos uses a global storage model that shapes how state is organized.
move-developer.challenge: Publish a Move module to Aptos testnet that stores a resource under a user account.

children.title: Global Storage and move_to / move_from
children.description: Aptos stores resources under account addresses using explicit storage operations; using the wrong address or operation order corrupts global state irreversibly. Mastering move_to, move_from, borrow_global, and borrow_global_mut is the core of all Aptos state management.

children.title: Entry Functions and Transaction Flow
children.description: Entry functions are the public callable surface of an Aptos module; their signature determines what transaction payloads look like on-chain. Incorrect entry function design leads to unusable modules that cannot be called from the CLI or SDK.

children.title: Aptos CLI: compile, test, publish
children.description: The Aptos CLI drives the entire development workflow from package init through testnet publish; not understanding its flags causes failed deployments and hard-to-debug state inconsistencies. A confident CLI workflow is the minimum viable skill for Aptos development.

children.title: Using the Aptos Framework
children.description: The Aptos framework provides battle-tested modules for coins, tokens, accounts, and tables; reinventing these increases audit surface and introduces bugs that the framework already handles. Knowing what the framework offers prevents unnecessary duplication.

children.title: Module Upgradeability on Aptos
children.description: Aptos supports compatible module upgrades but enforces strict compatibility rules; breaking a published interface locks users out of their funds or data. Understanding the upgrade lifecycle from day one avoids irreversible deployment mistakes.

children.title: Aptos Indexer and Events
children.description: Aptos events and the indexer enable off-chain applications to react to on-chain state changes without constant polling. Applications that ignore the event system are brittle and expensive to operate at scale.

children.title: Resource Accounts
children.description: Resource accounts let modules hold assets without a human signer, enabling autonomous protocols like DEXes and vaults. Developers who do not know resource accounts use signer-based workarounds that create centralization and key-management risks.

children.title: Tables and Dynamic Storage
children.description: Aptos Tables provide O(1) key-value storage that avoids loading entire collections into memory on every transaction. Using vectors where tables belong causes gas costs that scale linearly and transactions that eventually exceed limits.

move-developer.title: Building on Sui
move-developer.description: This layer builds Move modules on Sui, which uses an object-centric model. Sui's objects and parallel execution differ significantly from account-based chains.
move-developer.challenge: Build a Sui module that mints an object and transfers ownership to another address on testnet.

children.title: Owned vs Shared Objects
children.description: Sui's consensus model treats owned-object transactions as fastpath and shared-object transactions as slower consensus-path; choosing the wrong ownership type adds latency and cost. This distinction shapes every Sui protocol design decision.

children.title: Object Ownership Transfer
children.description: Transferring an object in Sui changes its ownership record on-chain; using transfer::transfer vs transfer::share_object incorrectly creates objects that cannot be accessed by the intended party. Correct transfer semantics are essential for any multi-user protocol.

children.title: Programmable Transaction Blocks
children.description: PTBs let clients compose multiple Move calls and object operations in a single atomic transaction, enabling complex workflows without custom coordinator contracts. Developers who build one-call-per-transaction workflows miss this composability and create worse UX with higher fees.

children.title: Entry Functions on Sui
children.description: Sui entry functions receive objects by value or reference from the transaction context; using the wrong parameter form causes the object to be consumed or locked when it should remain accessible. Mismatched entry signatures are among the most common Sui beginner mistakes.

children.title: Sui CLI: build, test, publish
children.description: The Sui CLI manages package compilation, unit tests, and testnet/mainnet publishing from a single tool; ignoring its output during publish often hides dependency or address-override errors. A reliable publish workflow is the first milestone of any Sui project.

children.title: TxContext and Sui-Specific APIs
children.description: TxContext provides the transaction digest, sender address, and object-ID generation; omitting it prevents creating new objects or checking the caller. Every non-trivial Sui module needs to understand when and how to use TxContext.

children.title: Dynamic Fields and Dynamic Object Fields
children.description: Sui's dynamic fields let objects grow collections of heterogeneous children without compile-time schema changes, enabling extensible protocols. Developers unfamiliar with dynamic fields create monolithic structs that cannot be evolved after deployment.

children.title: Object Wrapping
children.description: Wrapping an object inside another removes it from global storage, making it inaccessible until explicitly unwrapped; misusing wrapping permanently traps user assets. Understanding wrap/unwrap semantics is critical before building any composable protocol on Sui.

move-developer.title: Tokens and Digital Assets
move-developer.description: This layer implements fungible and non-fungible assets using Move standards. Tokens are the most common building block of Move applications.
move-developer.challenge: Create a fungible token and a simple NFT collection on your chosen chain and mint examples.

children.title: Aptos Fungible Asset Standard
children.description: Aptos v2 introduces a unified Fungible Asset standard that supersedes the older Coin module; using the deprecated module creates interoperability gaps with wallets and DEXes built on the new standard. Understanding the FA standard is required for any production token on Aptos.

children.title: Sui Coin and Balance
children.description: Sui represents fungible tokens as Coin<T> objects owned by addresses; splitting and merging coins incorrectly leads to dust objects that clog wallets and complicate UX. The split/merge model is fundamentally different from allowance-based ERC-20 patterns.

children.title: Aptos Digital Asset Standard
children.description: The Aptos Digital Asset standard defines collections and token objects with extensible metadata; building NFTs outside this standard breaks marketplace and wallet integration. Familiarity with the DA standard accelerates composable NFT development.

children.title: Sui NFTs and Collections
children.description: On Sui, NFTs are ordinary objects with metadata fields and can be extended with display objects for rich wallet presentation; omitting the Display standard causes NFTs to appear as raw hex in every wallet. The Display object is an ergonomic requirement, not an optional nicety.

children.title: Minting and Burning
children.description: Mint and burn authority must be controlled by a capability or admin-only entry function; leaving these operations open creates infinite-mint vulnerabilities that drain protocol value instantly. Capability-gated mint/burn is the baseline security requirement for any token.

children.title: Asset Metadata and On-chain Storage
children.description: Token metadata (name, symbol, decimals, URI) is consumed by wallets, explorers, and DeFi protocols; incorrect or mutable metadata breaks integrations in hard-to-debug ways after launch. Immutable metadata should be set at creation and verified before mainnet deploy.

children.title: Safe Transfer Patterns
children.description: Transfers must validate receiver existence and custody rules to prevent lost assets; on Aptos this means checking account registration, and on Sui it means understanding owned vs shared object semantics. Ignoring chain-specific transfer rules is one of the top causes of mainnet asset loss.

children.title: Collections and Batch Operations
children.description: Token collections require careful supply accounting and access control on both Aptos and Sui; off-by-one errors in counters or missing checks allow over-minting that breaks collection rarity guarantees. Thorough collection design before launch prevents post-mint reputation damage.

move-developer.title: Testing and the Move Prover
move-developer.description: This layer covers unit testing and formal verification with the Move Prover. The prover can mathematically guarantee key properties of your modules.
move-developer.challenge: Write unit tests and a Move Prover specification proving your token cannot be minted beyond a cap.

children.title: Writing and Running Unit Tests
children.description: Move's built-in test framework lets developers run tests inside the module using #[test] attributes without deploying to a network; skipping unit tests means bugs are discovered on testnet where they are slower and more expensive to fix. A comprehensive test suite is the primary safety net before formal verification.

children.title: Test-Only Code and Expected Failures
children.description: The #[test_only] attribute and #[expected_failure] annotation allow testing error paths and helper scaffolding without polluting production code; missing these leads to either untested abort paths or bloated deployed bytecode. Proper test isolation keeps modules lean and coverage complete.

children.title: Move Specification Language
children.description: The Move Specification Language (MSL) lets developers write preconditions, postconditions, and invariants that the Move Prover checks mathematically; modules without specifications can pass all unit tests and still harbor logical exploits. MSL is the primary tool that elevates Move security above other smart contract languages.

children.title: Running the Move Prover
children.description: The prover requires Boogie and Z3 as backend solvers and can take minutes on complex modules; not setting it up locally means security properties are never actually verified. Integrating prover runs into CI catches regressions before they reach review.

children.title: Invariants and Aborts Specs
children.description: Invariants assert global properties that must hold after every public function call, while aborts_if specs document when a function is allowed to fail; missing invariants allow subtle state corruption that unit tests rarely expose. Writing invariants first, before implementation, is the most effective specification methodology.

children.title: Supply-Cap Proofs
children.description: Proving that a token's total supply never exceeds a cap requires specifying the invariant and having the prover check every code path that modifies supply; this is the canonical demonstrating use case for the Move Prover. Developers who have proven a supply cap understand how to apply the same technique to any safety-critical property.

children.title: Aptos Move Test Framework
children.description: Aptos provides a dedicated test framework with helpers for mocking signer accounts and simulating chain state; using raw unit tests without these helpers produces tests that pass locally but fail to reflect realistic on-chain conditions. Investing in realistic test scaffolding reduces the gap between tests and production behavior.

children.title: Sui Testing Patterns
children.description: Sui's test_scenario module lets tests simulate multi-transaction sequences with different senders, which is essential for testing object transfer and multi-party protocols. Protocols tested only with single-sender scenarios regularly have authorization bugs that only appear in multi-user production usage.

move-developer.title: Composability and Generics
move-developer.description: This layer builds reusable, composable modules using generics and capabilities. Composability lets protocols build on each other safely.
move-developer.challenge: Refactor your token into a generic module and add a capability that gates the mint function.

children.title: Generic Types and Functions
children.description: Generics allow a single Move module to operate on many types, eliminating code duplication across token denominations or asset types. Developers who write non-generic modules end up with dozens of near-identical modules that are impossible to maintain consistently.

children.title: Phantom Type Parameters
children.description: Phantom types create distinct type identities at the type-system level without carrying runtime data, enabling safe nominal typing for tokens like Coin<USD> vs Coin<EUR>. Confusing phantom and non-phantom parameters causes compile errors or unintended type unification.

children.title: Capability and Witness Patterns
children.description: Capabilities are resource objects whose possession proves authorization; the witness pattern passes a zero-size type to prove the caller owns a particular module. Without these patterns, access control devolves to fragile address comparisons that are easily forged or misconfigured.

children.title: Cross-Module Calls and Friend Modules
children.description: Friend declarations grant limited elevated access to a sibling module, enabling tightly-coupled components without making internals fully public. Over-using friend relationships or skipping them in favor of public functions erodes module encapsulation and makes future refactoring unsafe.

children.title: Dynamic Fields for Extensibility on Sui
children.description: Dynamic fields let a Sui object accumulate heterogeneous child data at runtime without modifying the parent struct definition, enabling composable protocol extensions. Protocols without dynamic fields must redeploy every time a new data field is needed, breaking existing object instances.

children.title: Designing Composable Interfaces
children.description: A composable module exports a minimal set of typed capabilities and resource types that other modules can build upon without depending on implementation details. Tightly coupled modules that expose internal state make it impossible to upgrade one without breaking all dependents.

children.title: Building on Existing Protocols
children.description: Composing with deployed DEX, lending, or oracle modules requires understanding their published interfaces and capability requirements; calling them incorrectly can silently transfer funds or leave positions in broken states. Reading a protocol's source and test suite before integrating is the minimum due diligence.

children.title: Hot Potato Pattern
children.description: The hot potato pattern uses a resource without drop or store to force callers to complete a multi-step operation within the same transaction, enabling flash loans and atomic multi-step protocols. Omitting this pattern requires trust assumptions between modules that defeat the purpose of trustless protocols.

move-developer.title: Frontend and SDK Integration
move-developer.description: This layer connects applications to Move modules using the TypeScript SDKs. Client integration turns modules into real user-facing products.
move-developer.challenge: Build a web page with wallet connection that mints and displays your token via the TypeScript SDK.

children.title: TypeScript SDK Setup for Aptos
children.description: The Aptos TypeScript SDK provides typed interfaces for building, signing, and submitting transactions; using raw REST calls instead leads to brittle code that breaks with every API version change. A correctly configured SDK client is the foundation of every reliable Aptos frontend.

children.title: TypeScript SDK Setup for Sui
children.description: The Sui TypeScript SDK wraps the JSON-RPC API and provides object query, transaction building, and wallet-adapter interfaces; bypassing the SDK causes type mismatches in object IDs and BCS-encoded payloads. SDK fluency is the fastest path from a working Move module to a working frontend.

children.title: Querying Resources and Objects
children.description: Frontends need to read module resources on Aptos and objects on Sui to display balances, metadata, and protocol state; using the wrong query method returns stale data or silently returns null for missing resources. Understanding the query API deeply prevents subtle data-freshness bugs in production UIs.

children.title: Handling On-Chain Events
children.description: Events emitted by Move modules allow frontends to react to state changes without polling every block; missing event subscriptions creates UIs that require manual refresh and miss transactions in real time. Event-driven frontends are the production standard for all mature Move dApps.

children.title: Wallet Connection on Aptos
children.description: The Aptos Wallet Adapter provides a unified interface for connecting Petra, Martian, and other wallets; building direct wallet integrations creates maintenance burden and breaks when wallets update their APIs. Standardized wallet adapter usage is the only scalable approach for production Aptos apps.

children.title: Wallet Connection on Sui
children.description: Sui's dApp Kit provides React hooks for wallet connection, transaction signing, and object queries; using the lower-level JSON-RPC directly in a React app produces brittle, hard-to-test component logic. dApp Kit is the recommended integration path for all Sui web applications.

children.title: Building and Submitting Transactions
children.description: Transactions must be correctly serialized in BCS format with accurate gas estimates before submission; underestimating gas causes silent transaction failures that are confusing to end users. Gas simulation before submission is the professional standard for reliable dApp UX.

children.title: Confirming and Error Handling
children.description: Waiting for transaction finality requires polling or websocket callbacks with proper timeout and retry logic; ignoring confirmation status leads to UIs that show success before on-chain inclusion, causing user confusion during network congestion. Robust confirmation handling separates prototype apps from production-quality dApps.

move-developer.title: Security and Auditing in Move
move-developer.description: This layer examines Move-specific security concerns and review techniques. Even with strong safety guarantees, logic and access bugs remain possible.
move-developer.challenge: Audit your token module for capability leakage and access flaws, then close any gaps you find.

children.title: Access Control and Signer Verification
children.description: Move does not automatically restrict entry function callers; every privileged function must explicitly verify the signer against a stored admin address or capability. Missing signer checks on critical functions is the most common high-severity Move vulnerability found in audits.

children.title: Capability Leakage
children.description: A leaked capability object grants permanent elevated access to anyone who holds it; storing capabilities in publicly readable global storage or passing them through public functions are the most common leakage vectors. Capability storage and transfer paths must be audited before every release.

children.title: Integer Overflow and Abort Handling
children.description: Move aborts on integer overflow rather than wrapping, which is safe but can be weaponized as a denial-of-service vector if abort paths are not audited. Every arithmetic operation on user-supplied inputs must be validated with appropriate bounds checks or use the checked math helpers.

children.title: Reentrancy Considerations in Move
children.description: Move's type system prevents classic EVM reentrancy because re-entrant calls cannot modify resources held by an active borrow, but flash-loan-style control flow can still create unexpected state. Understanding exactly which reentrancy vectors exist and which are prevented avoids both false confidence and unnecessary defensive code.

children.title: Upgrade Safety and Compatibility
children.description: Module upgrades on Aptos must preserve existing struct layouts and public function signatures; breaking these creates incompatibilities that corrupt in-flight transactions and strand user assets under the old layout. Running the compatibility checker before every upgrade is a non-negotiable safety step.

children.title: Audit Checklists and Review Process
children.description: A structured audit checklist covering access control, arithmetic, storage, and upgrade safety catches the majority of common vulnerabilities before engaging an external auditor. Teams that skip checklists spend audit budget on findings they could have caught internally, leaving time for deeper logic review.

children.title: Applying the Move Prover for Security
children.description: Writing prover specifications for invariants identified during a manual audit provides machine-checked assurance that the invariant holds across all possible inputs. Security-critical modules without prover specifications rely entirely on test coverage, which cannot exhaustively verify all input combinations.

children.title: Known Move Vulnerability Patterns
children.description: Documented Move vulnerability classes include admin-key compromise, unchecked global storage existence, and event spoofing; knowing these patterns allows both proactive prevention and efficient review. Developers unfamiliar with Move-specific vulnerability taxonomy focus their reviews on EVM patterns that do not apply.

move-developer.title: Mainnet Deployment and Upgrades
move-developer.description: This final layer deploys Move modules to mainnet and manages upgrades safely. Production deployment requires careful upgrade policy and monitoring.
move-developer.challenge: Deploy your full token application to testnet and rehearse a compatible module upgrade.

children.title: Publishing to Mainnet
children.description: Mainnet publication is irreversible on the same address; a botched deployment requires re-deploying at a new address, migrating all user state, and updating every integration. Rehearsing the exact publish command on testnet with production configuration eliminates this risk.

children.title: Upgrade Policies and Authority Management
children.description: Choosing an upgrade policy (compatible, additive, or immutable) at publish time determines what future changes are allowed; selecting immutable by mistake prevents all bug fixes permanently. Managing upgrade authority through multi-sig or governance prevents single points of failure.

children.title: Running the Compatibility Checker
children.description: Both Aptos and Sui CLIs include compatibility check commands that compare a new module version against the published one before deployment; skipping this step is the primary cause of failed upgrades that brick live contracts. Every upgrade should pass compatibility checks on a fork before being applied to mainnet.

children.title: Safe Migration Patterns
children.description: Migrating state from an old module version to a new one requires careful sequencing of schema additions, data migration transactions, and deprecation of old entry points. Skipping a migration plan causes old and new state to coexist in incompatible forms, breaking dependent applications.

children.title: Monitoring On-Chain Activity
children.description: Production modules must be monitored for unusual transaction patterns, unexpected abort codes, and large asset movements that indicate an ongoing exploit. Teams without monitoring discover incidents hours after they start, maximizing damage and complicating post-mortems.

children.title: Indexer Integration for Operations
children.description: The Aptos Indexer and Sui GraphQL indexer allow efficient historical queries and real-time event subscriptions that the chain RPC alone cannot support at scale. Operators who rely only on the RPC for monitoring face rate limits and incomplete data during high traffic periods.

children.title: Incident Response Runbooks
children.description: A pre-written runbook covering pause mechanisms, upgrade rollback procedures, and emergency contact chains enables a fast, coordinated response when an exploit is detected. Teams without runbooks improvise under pressure, making cascading mistakes that worsen the impact.

children.title: Post-Mortem and Upgrade Workflow
children.description: After an incident, a structured post-mortem identifies the root cause, documents the fix, and verifies the patch with formal proof before re-deployment; rushing a fix without this process often introduces new vulnerabilities. The upgrade workflow is the final test of whether a team's development process is mature.

layer2-dev.title: Scaling and Layer 2 Foundations
layer2-dev.description: This layer explains why Ethereum needs scaling and how layer 2 solutions work. Understanding the scaling landscape frames every later design decision.
layer2-dev.challenge: Write a comparison of optimistic and zk rollups covering their trust and cost trade-offs.

children.title: The Scaling Trilemma
children.description: The scaling trilemma states that a blockchain can optimize for at most two of three properties — decentralization, security, and scalability — at the same time. Grasping this constraint explains every architectural trade-off in Layer 2 design and prevents you from naively expecting a free lunch when evaluating solutions.

children.title: Layer 1 vs Layer 2 Architecture
children.description: Layer 1 is the base consensus chain that provides security and settlement finality; Layer 2 is a system that inherits that security while executing transactions off-chain. Conflating the two leads to incorrect assumptions about finality, censorship resistance, and where trust actually lives in a cross-layer system.

children.title: Optimistic vs ZK Rollups
children.description: Optimistic rollups assume transactions are valid and rely on fraud proofs to dispute bad state transitions, while ZK rollups generate cryptographic validity proofs for every batch. Choosing the wrong model for your latency or cost requirements leads to poor UX or infrastructure over-spend.

children.title: Sequencers and Data Availability
children.description: A sequencer orders and batches transactions before publishing them to L1; data availability ensures the raw transaction data is accessible for verification. Without understanding both roles, you cannot reason about censorship resistance, liveness failures, or the cost structure of your rollup.

children.title: EVM Equivalence
children.description: EVM-equivalent rollups execute the same bytecode and support the same toolchain as Ethereum mainnet, meaning Hardhat, Foundry, and existing Solidity contracts need no changes. Misunderstanding the degree of equivalence causes silent bugs when opcodes like SELFDESTRUCT or PUSH0 behave differently on a given L2.

children.title: Gas Differences on L2
children.description: L2 gas costs have two components — execution gas on L2 and an L1 data fee proportional to calldata size. Ignoring the L1 fee component leads to incorrect cost estimates, failed transactions when users set low gas limits, and suboptimal contract encodings.

children.title: Foundry for L2 Deployments
children.description: Foundry's forge and cast tools let you compile, test, and deploy contracts to any EVM-compatible L2 using the same workflow as mainnet. Skipping L2-specific network configuration in foundry.toml results in deployments going to the wrong chain or failed RPC calls during verification.

children.title: Reading L2 Block Explorers
children.description: L2 explorers such as Optimistic Etherscan and Arbiscan expose L2-specific fields like L1 submission batch index and the derivation status of each transaction. Overlooking these fields makes it impossible to debug delayed transactions or confirm that a batch was correctly posted to L1.

layer2-dev.title: Deploying to Layer 2 Networks
layer2-dev.description: This layer deploys existing Solidity contracts to layer 2 networks. EVM-equivalent rollups let you reuse familiar tooling with lower fees.
layer2-dev.challenge: Deploy a sample ERC20 contract to an Optimism testnet and interact with it through an explorer.

children.title: Connecting to L2 Testnets
children.description: Setting up RPC endpoints for Optimism Sepolia or Arbitrum Sepolia is the first practical step toward L2 deployment. Incorrect chain IDs or mismatched RPC URLs cause transactions to silently go to the wrong network, wasting test funds and delaying debugging.

children.title: Bridging Test Funds
children.description: Before deploying you must move test ETH from L1 to your L2 testnet via a native bridge or faucet. Skipping this step leaves you unable to pay gas for deployment transactions and delays the entire development feedback loop.

children.title: Verifying Source Code on L2 Explorers
children.description: Verifying contracts on L2 explorers exposes your ABI to frontend libraries and allows users to read and call your contract through a UI. Unverified contracts are opaque to integrators, slow down audits, and prevent automatic ABI inference in tools like Etherscan.

children.title: ERC20 Deployment Workflow
children.description: Deploying a standard ERC20 is the canonical smoke test for a new L2 environment because it exercises compilation, deployment, and interaction in a familiar contract shape. Rushing past this step without verifying allowance and transfer behavior on L2 leaves chain-specific opcode differences undiscovered.

children.title: Multi-Network Foundry Configuration
children.description: Foundry's foundry.toml supports named network profiles so you can target Optimism, Arbitrum, or a local Anvil fork with a single flag. Without proper profiles, developers hard-code RPC URLs in scripts, causing accidental mainnet deployments or lost environment-specific configurations.

children.title: Ethers.js Provider Setup for L2
children.description: Connecting ethers.js to an L2 requires setting the correct provider URL and chain ID so that fee estimation and transaction signing target the right network. Misconfigured providers produce confusing errors where transactions are built for one chain but signed for another.

children.title: Transaction Lifecycle on L2
children.description: An L2 transaction goes through soft confirmation by the sequencer, inclusion in a batch posted to L1, and finally L1 finality — each stage having different security guarantees. Applications that treat soft confirmation as final are vulnerable to sequencer reorgs during the window before L1 inclusion.

children.title: Debugging Failed L2 Transactions
children.description: L2 failures can happen at the sequencer level, during batch submission, or at the EVM execution level, and each requires different debugging tools. Treating all failures as simple revert errors causes developers to miss sequencer downtime, batch submission failures, or L1 data fee under-pricing.

layer2-dev.title: Optimistic Rollups in Depth
layer2-dev.description: This layer dives into how optimistic rollups achieve security through fraud proofs. The optimistic model trades a challenge window for low compute cost.
layer2-dev.challenge: Trace a withdrawal from an optimistic rollup to L1 and document each step of the challenge period.

children.title: Transaction Batching and State Commitments
children.description: The sequencer groups many L2 transactions into a single compressed calldata blob and posts a state root commitment to L1. If the batching mechanism is not understood, developers cannot reason about when their transaction data is available to verifiers or how to estimate L1 data costs accurately.

children.title: The Fraud Proof Challenge Window
children.description: During the challenge window (typically 7 days) any verifier can submit a fraud proof disputing a posted state root, forcing an on-chain bisection game. Applications that finalize withdrawals before this window closes risk sending funds based on a state root that could still be disputed.

children.title: Optimism Bedrock Architecture
children.description: Bedrock separates the sequencer, batcher, and proposer into distinct services and aligns the derivation pipeline closely with Ethereum's block structure. Understanding this modularity is essential for operators running custom OP Stack chains and for developers diagnosing sequencer-related failures.

children.title: Arbitrum Nitro Architecture
children.description: Arbitrum Nitro compiles the AVM to WASM and uses an interactive fraud proof protocol called BOLD for dispute resolution. Confusing Nitro's dispute mechanism with Optimism's single-round fault proofs leads to incorrect security assumptions when building cross-rollup tooling.

children.title: Withdrawal Delays and User Experience
children.description: Native withdrawals from optimistic rollups require waiting the full challenge period before L1 funds are released, which is a significant UX hurdle for end users. Without planning for this delay through liquidity providers or fast-exit bridges, user-facing applications will see high abandonment rates.

children.title: Fast Exit Providers
children.description: Third-party liquidity networks like Across and Hop Protocol let users exit an optimistic rollup instantly by having a liquidity provider front the funds on L1. Relying solely on native bridges for withdrawal UX is a competitive disadvantage, but using fast-exit providers without verifying their trust model shifts security assumptions.

children.title: Sequencer Ordering and MEV
children.description: The sequencer determines transaction ordering within each batch, creating an opportunity for sequencer-level MEV extraction similar to builder MEV on L1. Smart contract developers who do not account for sequencer ordering may write protocols vulnerable to sandwich attacks or front-running executed at the L2 level.

children.title: Forced Inclusion and Censorship Resistance
children.description: Users can bypass the sequencer and submit transactions directly to L1 through the forced inclusion mechanism, preventing permanent censorship. Applications that do not expose this escape hatch to users have no fallback when the sequencer is unavailable or censoring specific addresses.

layer2-dev.title: Zero-Knowledge Rollups
layer2-dev.description: This layer explores zk rollups and the validity proofs that secure them. Validity proofs offer fast finality and strong cryptographic guarantees.
topics: SNARKs and STARKs at a high level
layer2-dev.challenge: Deploy a contract to a zkSync testnet and compare its finality behavior to the optimistic deployment.

children.title: SNARKs vs STARKs
children.description: SNARKs produce compact proofs quickly but require a trusted setup ceremony, while STARKs are larger but transparent and post-quantum resistant. Choosing the wrong proof system for your application leads to either unnecessary trust assumptions or unacceptably large proof sizes in your on-chain verifier.

children.title: Validity Proofs and Instant Finality
children.description: ZK rollups post a validity proof with each batch; once the verifier contract accepts the proof on L1 the state root is final with no challenge window. This instant finality fundamentally changes withdrawal UX and cross-chain messaging latency compared to optimistic rollups.

children.title: zkEVM Compatibility Levels
children.description: Different zkEVMs trade off between proof generation speed and EVM bytecode compatibility, ranging from full EVM equivalence to language-level compatibility only. Deploying to a zkEVM without understanding its compatibility level can result in subtle opcode differences that break contracts working correctly on mainnet.

children.title: Prover Infrastructure
children.description: ZK proof generation is computationally intensive and is handled by specialized prover infrastructure outside of normal contract execution. Ignoring prover capacity and latency leads to unrealistic throughput expectations and surprises when proof generation falls behind transaction volume.

children.title: Deploying on zkSync Era
children.description: zkSync Era uses a custom compiler (zksolc) that generates ZK-friendly bytecode, so the standard Solidity compiler output cannot be deployed directly. Using the wrong compiler or skipping zkSync-specific deployment scripts produces invalid bytecode that fails silently or reverts on-chain.

children.title: Deploying on Starknet
children.description: Starknet uses Cairo as its native language, which compiles to Sierra and then CASM rather than EVM bytecode. Attempting to port Solidity contracts directly without rewriting them in Cairo will fail entirely, making language selection the first critical decision for any Starknet project.

children.title: Proof Generation Latency
children.description: Although ZK rollups offer cryptographic finality, proof generation can take minutes to hours depending on batch size and prover throughput. dApps that display 'finalized' status immediately after sequencer confirmation mislead users about the actual settlement guarantee.

children.title: Comparing ZK and Optimistic Finality
children.description: ZK rollups achieve L1 finality as soon as the validity proof is verified, while optimistic rollups must wait through a challenge window of up to 7 days. The right choice between the two depends on your withdrawal frequency, security budget for running a prover, and tolerance for UX complexity around delays.

layer2-dev.title: Bridges and Cross-Layer Messaging
layer2-dev.description: This layer covers how assets and messages move between L1 and L2. Bridges are critical infrastructure and a frequent source of risk.
layer2-dev.challenge: Build a contract that sends a cross-domain message from L2 to an L1 contract on testnet.

children.title: Deposit and Withdrawal Flows
children.description: Native bridge deposits lock assets in an L1 escrow contract and mint corresponding tokens on L2; withdrawals burn L2 tokens and release escrow funds on L1 after finalization. Misunderstanding which side holds the canonical balance causes double-spend reasoning errors and incorrect total supply calculations in cross-layer token contracts.

children.title: Cross-Domain Messaging Contracts
children.description: L1CrossDomainMessenger and L2CrossDomainMessenger are the canonical contracts for passing arbitrary messages between layers; they handle relaying, retries, and replay protection. Bypassing them to build a custom bridge from scratch is a common source of critical vulnerabilities including the Nomad and Ronin bridge exploits.

children.title: Bridge Security Risks
children.description: Bridges are the largest attack surface in the L2 ecosystem because they hold significant value and involve complex cross-chain state synchronization. Understanding the failure modes — incorrect message validation, replay attacks, and admin key compromise — is mandatory before deploying any bridging logic.

children.title: Third-Party Bridges
children.description: Third-party bridges like Stargate and Synapse offer faster withdrawals and multi-chain routing but introduce additional trust assumptions beyond the rollup's native security. Using a third-party bridge without auditing its trust model can expose users to counterparty risk that users wrongly assume is covered by the rollup's security.

children.title: L2 to L1 Message Passing
children.description: Sending a message from L2 to L1 requires posting the message in a transaction on L2, waiting for the batch to be finalized on L1, and then relaying the message proof manually on L1. Applications that automate the relay step only for deposits but not for withdrawals leave users stranded with unclaimed L1 funds.

resources.label: Optimism L2 to L1 Messaging Tutorial

children.title: Message Replay Protection
children.description: Cross-domain messages must include a nonce or other replay-prevention mechanism to ensure a valid message cannot be executed multiple times on the destination chain. Missing replay protection is a critical vulnerability that allows an attacker to drain a bridge by replaying a single legitimate withdrawal.

children.title: Custom ERC20 Bridge Contracts
children.description: For tokens with custom logic such as fee-on-transfer or rebasing mechanisms, the standard bridge must be extended with custom gateway contracts on both L1 and L2. Deploying a custom token through the standard bridge without a matching gateway breaks total supply invariants and can brick tokens permanently.

children.title: Canonical vs Wrapped Tokens
children.description: When the same asset is bridged through multiple bridges, each bridge creates its own wrapped version, fragmenting liquidity unless one canonical representation is established. Teams that do not designate a canonical bridge early create ecosystems with multiple competing token addresses for the same asset.

layer2-dev.title: L2-Aware Contract Development
layer2-dev.description: This layer teaches how to write contracts that account for layer 2 specifics. Subtle differences in gas, fees, and opcodes affect correctness and cost.
layer2-dev.challenge: Optimize a contract for L2 by minimizing calldata and verify the fee reduction on a testnet.

children.title: Calldata Optimization Techniques
children.description: On L2, the L1 data fee is proportional to the calldata size of each transaction, so encoding function arguments compactly directly reduces user costs. Developers who ignore calldata size optimization ship contracts where users pay 5-10x more in L1 data fees than necessary for common interactions.

children.title: Block and Timestamp Differences
children.description: On many L2 networks, block.number returns the L2 block number and block.timestamp may be derived differently from Ethereum mainnet. Contracts that use block.number for timelocks or randomness will behave incorrectly on L2 where block times are often one second or less.

children.title: Native Account Abstraction on zkSync
children.description: zkSync Era has account abstraction built into the protocol, allowing smart contract accounts to pay fees and validate transactions natively without ERC-4337 infrastructure. Ignoring native AA on zkSync means building paymasters and smart accounts that are unnecessarily complex and gas-inefficient compared to the native approach.

children.title: ERC-4337 on EVM L2s
children.description: On EVM-equivalent L2s like Optimism and Arbitrum, ERC-4337 provides account abstraction through an EntryPoint contract without protocol changes. Deploying ERC-4337 paymasters without understanding L2 fee structures leads to paymasters that run out of prefund deposits faster than expected due to L1 data fees.

children.title: L1 to L2 Address Aliasing
children.description: When an L1 contract sends a message to L2, its address is aliased by adding a constant offset to prevent collisions with predeployed L2 contracts. Contracts that check msg.sender on L2 without accounting for aliasing will incorrectly reject valid messages from their own L1 counterpart.

children.title: Chain-Specific Precompiles
children.description: L2 networks expose custom precompile contracts at well-known addresses for operations like L1 block info retrieval and fee calculation that have no equivalent on mainnet. Calling these precompiles without first checking the current chain ID will cause failures when the same contract is deployed on multiple networks.

children.title: Minimizing L1 Data Fees
children.description: The dominant cost driver for most L2 transactions is the L1 data fee charged for posting calldata or blobs, not L2 execution gas. Teams that optimize only execution gas miss 80-95% of the cost savings available by reducing calldata through better ABI design and compression-friendly encoding.

children.title: Storage Layout Considerations
children.description: Storage slot packing is as important on L2 as on L1 for execution gas, but L2 also charges for the calldata used in each write, making write-heavy operations disproportionately expensive. Contracts ported from L1 without reviewing storage layout and write frequency will have higher-than-expected costs on L2.

layer2-dev.title: Building a dApp Across Layers
layer2-dev.description: This layer builds a full application that spans L1 and L2 with a frontend. Cross-layer apps must coordinate state and user experience across networks.
layer2-dev.challenge: Build a dApp that lets users deposit from L1, act on L2, and track their bridge transactions.

children.title: Multi-Network Frontend Design
children.description: A cross-layer dApp must handle multiple wallet connections, chain switches, and divergent contract addresses simultaneously in the same UI. Frontends that hardcode a single chain ID or contract address break for users on different networks and create a support burden that grows with the number of supported chains.

children.title: Cross-Layer State Synchronization
children.description: State on L1 and L2 is updated asynchronously, so a dApp must decide which layer is the source of truth for each piece of data and how to handle the lag. Applications that read L2 state while waiting for an L1 confirmation can show incorrect balances or allow double-actions during the asynchronous window.

children.title: Tracking Bridge Transactions
children.description: Bridge transactions have multiple asynchronous phases — L2 confirmation, batch submission, L1 finality, and withdrawal relay — each requiring a different status display in the UI. Applications that show a single loading spinner without phase-level feedback cause users to think transactions are stuck and generate unnecessary support requests.

children.title: Handling Withdrawal Delays in UI
children.description: The 7-day withdrawal window must be communicated clearly before a user initiates a native bridge withdrawal to set correct expectations and prevent support escalations. Burying withdrawal delay information or displaying it only after the transaction is submitted results in frustrated users and chargebacks from centralized off-ramps.

children.title: Chain ID Detection and Switching
children.description: Wallets expose the current chain ID and provide the wallet_switchEthereumChain RPC method for programmatic network switching. Applications that do not detect and prompt for chain switches before contract calls send transactions to the wrong network, wasting gas and confusing users.

children.title: L2 Confirmations vs L1 Finality
children.description: L2 soft confirmations from the sequencer are fast but not final; applications must decide whether to act on soft confirmation or wait for L1 proof verification depending on the value at risk. High-value operations that act on soft confirmation expose users to sequencer reorg risk during the finalization window.

children.title: Subgraph Indexing for L2
children.description: The Graph Protocol supports indexing contracts deployed on Optimism and Arbitrum, allowing dApps to query historical event data without maintaining custom infrastructure. Building custom event listeners instead of using a subgraph creates unreliable data pipelines that miss events during node downtime or RPC rate limiting.

children.title: L2 Node RPC Rate Limits
children.description: Public L2 RPC endpoints enforce strict rate limits that are easily hit when polling for events or querying many block ranges in the same frontend session. Applications that do not implement request batching, caching, or fallback RPC providers will degrade severely under modest user load.

layer2-dev.title: Running and Customizing a Rollup
layer2-dev.description: This layer uses rollup frameworks to spin up a custom L2. Rollup-as-a-service stacks let teams launch their own chains quickly.
topics: Setting up a batcher and proposer
layer2-dev.challenge: Launch a local rollup using a stack framework and deploy your cross-layer dApp to it.

children.title: OP Stack Component Architecture
children.description: The OP Stack decomposes a rollup into discrete services — op-node (consensus), op-geth (execution), op-batcher, and op-proposer — each with its own configuration and failure modes. Operators who treat the stack as a black box cannot diagnose service-level failures or tune throughput independently per component.

children.title: Derivation Pipeline
children.description: The derivation pipeline reconstructs the L2 canonical chain by reading L1 batches and deposit events, making it the source of truth for L2 state. Misconfigured derivation parameters cause chain splits where different nodes derive divergent canonical chains from the same L1 data.

children.title: Sequencer Configuration and Operation
children.description: Running a sequencer requires configuring its L1 connection, P2P networking, and fee parameters, plus monitoring its posting cadence to ensure batches are submitted within the configured window. A sequencer that falls behind in posting batches increases user-perceived finality latency and can trigger forced inclusion on L1.

children.title: Batcher and Proposer Services
children.description: The batcher compresses and posts transaction data to L1 as calldata or blobs, while the proposer submits state root commitments; both require funded L1 accounts and continuous operation. Failing to monitor batcher wallet balances is the most common cause of L2 production outages, as an empty wallet stops all batch submissions.

children.title: Custom Gas Tokens
children.description: OP Stack and ZK Stack chains support using an ERC20 token as the native gas token instead of ETH, enabling application-specific chains with their own economic models. Misconfiguring the custom gas token bridge contracts can freeze user funds or allow unauthorized minting of the gas token on L2.

children.title: Connecting to L1 and Superchain
children.description: Each OP Stack chain requires a deployment of L1 system contracts (OptimismPortal, L2OutputOracle, SystemConfig) that anchor the rollup to a specific L1. Incorrectly parameterizing these contracts during deployment is not easily fixable after launch because the L1 anchor contracts govern withdrawal security.

children.title: Managed Rollup Platforms
children.description: Platforms like Conduit, Caldera, and AltLayer abstract away sequencer operations and infrastructure management, letting teams launch production rollups without deep DevOps expertise. Choosing a managed platform without understanding the underlying stack creates operational lock-in and makes incident response dependent on the provider.

children.title: ZK Stack for Custom ZK Chains
children.description: The ZK Stack allows teams to launch sovereign ZK chains that share proof infrastructure and can interoperate through the ZK chain ecosystem. Launching a ZK Stack chain without understanding the shared proof model means missing the cost-sharing benefits and incorrectly sizing prover infrastructure.

layer2-dev.title: Layer 2 Security and Decentralization
layer2-dev.description: This layer examines the security assumptions and decentralization roadmap of rollups. Sequencer centralization and upgrade keys are key risks to evaluate.
layer2-dev.challenge: Evaluate your custom rollup against the L2BEAT risk framework and document its weak points.

children.title: Sequencer Centralization Risks
children.description: Most production rollups operate a single centralized sequencer, creating a single point of failure for liveness and a potential vector for censorship. Security-conscious protocols must understand the escape hatch mechanisms that allow users to bypass a misbehaving sequencer and submit transactions directly to L1.

children.title: Upgrade Keys and Timelocks
children.description: Most rollup system contracts are upgradeable via multisig or governance, and the absence of a timelock means the controlling keys can change contract logic without user recourse. Protocols that do not audit the upgrade key holders and timelock duration are exposed to a rug pull risk that is independent of the smart contract code quality.

children.title: On-Chain vs Off-Chain Data Availability
children.description: Rollups that post data on-chain (Ethereum calldata or blobs) inherit Ethereum's data availability guarantees, while validiums that use off-chain DA committees trade security for lower costs. Choosing off-chain DA without understanding the data withholding attack vector can result in users being unable to reconstruct their funds even if the L1 contract is intact.

children.title: Celestia and Modular DA
children.description: Modular data availability layers like Celestia decouple DA from execution and consensus, allowing rollups to post data more cheaply than Ethereum blobs while maintaining verifiable availability guarantees. Integrating a modular DA layer without understanding the light client security model weakens the overall security of the rollup for users relying on those guarantees.

children.title: Interactive Fraud Proof Games
children.description: Optimistic rollup fraud proofs work through a bisection game where the challenger and defender narrow down a dispute to a single instruction step, which is then executed on L1. Protocols that depend on the fraud proof mechanism to be secure must ensure the challenge period is long enough for an honest challenger to respond even under censorship.

children.title: Validity Proof Soundness
children.description: ZK validity proofs guarantee that the prover cannot produce a valid proof for an invalid state transition, making the security assumption cryptographic rather than economic. However, bugs in the verifier circuit itself can break soundness, which is why ZK rollup verifiers undergo extensive auditing before mainnet deployment.

children.title: Stage 0, 1, and 2 Rollups
children.description: L2BEAT classifies rollups by decentralization stage: Stage 0 has training wheels with a security council, Stage 1 has limited governance, and Stage 2 is fully governed by on-chain proofs. Protocols building on a Stage 0 rollup inherit its trust assumptions and should clearly communicate to users that a multisig can override state.

children.title: Optimism Governance and Security Council
children.description: The Optimism Collective governs protocol upgrades through a dual governance system with a token house and a citizens house, backed by a security council with veto power. Developers building on Optimism should monitor governance proposals because accepted upgrades change the behavior of system contracts their protocols depend on.

layer2-dev.title: Production Deployment and Operations
layer2-dev.description: This final layer ships a layer 2 application to a production network and operates it. Running cross-layer systems in production demands monitoring and safe upgrades.
layer2-dev.challenge: Deploy and verify your cross-layer dApp on a public L2 network and set up bridge and chain monitoring.

children.title: Mainnet L2 Deployment Checklist
children.description: Deploying to a public L2 mainnet requires contract verification, multisig ownership transfer, and documentation of all deployed addresses before going live. Teams that skip the ownership transfer step leave deployer private keys as admin, creating a critical single point of compromise for the entire protocol.

children.title: Upgrade Governance for L2 Contracts
children.description: L2 application contracts need an upgrade process that is consistent with the rollup's own upgrade governance, including timelocks that give users time to exit before a contested upgrade takes effect. Protocols that can upgrade contracts immediately with a single key give users no exit window in case of a governance attack or key compromise.

children.title: Bridge Event Monitoring
children.description: Production bridge monitoring must track deposit and withdrawal events on both L1 and L2, alerting operators when withdrawals are not relayed within expected time bounds. Without automated bridge monitoring, stuck withdrawals go unnoticed until users escalate support tickets days after the issue first appeared.

children.title: Batcher and Proposer Health Monitoring
children.description: Operators must monitor batcher submission frequency, proposer state root posting cadence, and the L1 wallet balances of both services to detect production outages before users notice increased finality latency. Silent batcher failures that go undetected for hours cause transactions to pile up in the sequencer mempool and can trigger forced inclusion mechanics.

children.title: L2 Incident Runbooks
children.description: Production L2 operations require pre-written runbooks for common failure scenarios including sequencer downtime, batcher wallet depletion, and bridge relay failures. Teams without runbooks take significantly longer to resolve incidents because engineers must reason from first principles under pressure instead of following a tested procedure.

children.title: Emergency Pause Mechanisms
children.description: Critical L2 contracts including bridges and system contracts often include a guardian or security council role that can pause the system in response to an active exploit. Protocols that do not integrate with the rollup's pause mechanism lose the ability to halt damage during an incident and expose users to continued losses while a fix is prepared.

children.title: Sequencer and Prover Cost Management
children.description: Running a sequencer and prover at scale requires ongoing cost management as L1 blob fees fluctuate and transaction volume grows. Operators who do not model their cost structure per transaction cannot make informed decisions about batch sizes, compression settings, or when to migrate to cheaper data availability layers.

children.title: Protocol Upgrade Coordination
children.description: Rolling out upgrades to a live L2 requires coordinating hard forks across the sequencer, full nodes, and L1 system contracts, with clear communication to ecosystem participants. Uncoordinated upgrades that change consensus rules cause chain splits where some nodes reject blocks produced by an upgraded sequencer.
